definition: An activity is a game if and only if (i) it is governed by rules, (ii) it involves competition between participants, and (iii) it is undertaken for amusement.
conditions: governed by rules
involves competition
undertaken for amusement
counterexample: A child throwing a ball against a wall and catching it is governed by self-imposed rules, is undertaken for amusement, but involves no competition. It is nonetheless a game. Conversely, professional sport is governed by rules and involves competition, but the participants may not find it amusing — it is their livelihood.
missing_condition: Games need not involve competition, and the motive of amusement may be absent without disqualifying the activity as a game.
passage: One is inclined to define a game as an activity governed by rules, involving competition between participants, and undertaken for amusement. This definition captures many familiar games — chess, football, card games of various kinds. But it proves too narrow. Consider a child who amuses herself by throwing a ball against a wall and catching it, counting the number of consecutive catches. She has imposed rules upon herself (she must catch the ball cleanly, she must not move from her spot), and the activity is undertaken for amusement. But there is no competition: she plays alone, against no opponent. The second condition fails, yet we should not hesitate to call this a game. Now consider a professional footballer who plays not for amusement but for his livelihood. The activity is governed by elaborate rules and involves intense competition, but the element of amusement may be entirely absent; the player may find the game exhausting and tedious. The third condition fails. Yet football remains a game. The definition has identified features that are typical of games but not essential to all of them.

definition: A statement is a lie if and only if (i) it is false, (ii) the speaker believes it to be false, and (iii) the speaker intends to deceive the hearer.
conditions: statement is false
speaker believes it false
speaker intends to deceive
counterexample: A man tells his friend, 'Your wife is faithful to you,' believing this to be false and intending to deceive. But unknown to the speaker, the wife is in fact faithful: the statement is true. The first condition fails. Yet the man has surely lied, for he said what he believed to be false with the intention of deceiving.
missing_condition: A lie requires that the speaker assert what he believes to be false with intent to deceive; the actual truth or falsity of the statement is irrelevant.
passage: The common analysis holds that a statement is a lie if and only if it is false, the speaker believes it to be false, and the speaker intends to deceive the hearer. This definition, though plausible on first inspection, encounters a decisive objection. Consider a man who tells his friend, 'Your wife is faithful to you.' The man believes this to be false — he has heard rumours of infidelity and regards them as reliable. He makes the statement with the intention of deceiving his friend, wishing to spare him pain or to manipulate his behaviour. But it transpires that the rumours were unfounded: the wife is in fact entirely faithful. The statement is true. The first condition — that the statement be false — is not met. Yet the man has surely lied. He asserted what he believed to be false, with the deliberate aim of producing a false belief in his hearer. That the statement happened, by accident, to be true does not redeem it from being a lie. The definition has erred in requiring actual falsity; what matters for lying is the speaker's belief that his statement is false, not whether it is so in fact.

definition: An object is a vehicle if and only if (i) it is designed to transport persons or goods, (ii) it has wheels, and (iii) it is powered by a motor.
conditions: designed to transport
has wheels
powered by motor
counterexample: A rowing boat is designed to transport persons, but it has no wheels and no motor. Yet it is plainly a vehicle. A horse-drawn cart has wheels and transports goods, but has no motor. A sedan chair has neither wheels nor motor. All are vehicles.
missing_condition: The definition is too closely tied to the motorcar as paradigm; vehiclehood requires only the function of conveyance, not a particular mechanism.
passage: It is tempting to define a vehicle as an object designed to transport persons or goods, having wheels, and powered by a motor. This definition fits the motorcar, the lorry, and the omnibus. But it excludes a great many things that are plainly vehicles. A rowing boat is designed to transport persons across water; it has no wheels and no motor, yet it is a vehicle. A horse-drawn cart has wheels and is designed to transport goods, but it is not powered by a motor; it too is a vehicle. A sedan chair has neither wheels nor motor; it is carried by men and is designed to transport a single person; and it has been called a vehicle since the seventeenth century. The definition has taken the motorcar as its paradigm and has mistaken contingent features of that paradigm — wheels, a motor — for essential features of the concept. What makes something a vehicle is its function: it is a means of conveyance. The mechanism by which conveyance is achieved — wheels, sails, oars, or human bearers — is incidental.

definition: An utterance is a promise if and only if (i) the speaker commits himself to performing a future action, (ii) the commitment is voluntarily undertaken, and (iii) the utterance is addressed to a specific person.
conditions: commits to future action
voluntarily undertaken
addressed to specific person
counterexample: A man makes a solemn vow to himself to stop drinking. He commits to a future action (abstinence), the commitment is voluntary, but it is not addressed to any other person. Yet we commonly speak of promises to oneself, and the commitment carries genuine moral weight.
missing_condition: Promises need not be addressed to another person; one may make binding commitments to oneself.
passage: A plausible analysis holds that an utterance is a promise if and only if the speaker commits himself to performing a future action, the commitment is voluntarily undertaken, and the utterance is addressed to a specific person. The third condition reflects the common view that a promise is essentially a social act — a commitment made to another. But consider a man who, after a period of self-destructive behaviour, makes a solemn private vow to himself: he will stop drinking. No one else is present; the vow is addressed to no specific person. The first two conditions are satisfied — there is a commitment to a future action, and it is voluntary — but the third fails. Yet the man has made a promise, and he would feel, should he break it, that he has violated an obligation. We commonly speak of promises to oneself, and such promises carry genuine moral weight. The definition has erred in treating the social dimension of promising as essential, when in fact the core of a promise — the binding of one's future will — may occur in the absence of any other person.

definition: An object is a chair if and only if (i) it has a seat, (ii) it has a back, (iii) it has legs, and (iv) it is designed for one person to sit on.
conditions: has a seat
has a back
has legs
designed for one person
counterexample: A beanbag has no back, no legs, and no rigid seat, yet it is designed for one person to sit on and is commonly called a chair. A tree stump used as a seat has no back. A hanging egg chair has no legs. These are all chairs in common parlance.
missing_condition: The concept of a chair is defined by its function (seating one person) rather than by structural features that are typical but not essential.
passage: One might define a chair as an object that has a seat, a back, legs, and is designed for one person to sit on. This definition captures the paradigm case — the four-legged wooden chair with a backrest — but it proves too narrow upon reflection. A beanbag has no rigid seat, no back, and no legs; it is an amorphous sack filled with polystyrene beads. Yet it is designed for one person to sit on, and it is sold as a chair, called a chair, and used as a chair. A hanging chair, suspended from the ceiling by a chain, has no legs whatever. A backless stool, if we are generous with our categories, might be called a chair in some contexts. In each case, one or more of the structural conditions fail, yet the object serves the function of a chair and is recognised as such. The definition has mistaken the typical features of chairs — seats, backs, legs — for essential features. What makes something a chair is not its structure but its function: it is an object designed and used for the purpose of seating a single person.

definition: A substance is food if and only if (i) it is consumed by ingestion, (ii) it provides nutritional value to the organism, and (iii) it is not harmful when consumed in normal quantities.
conditions: consumed by ingestion
provides nutritional value
not harmful in normal quantities
counterexample: The pufferfish (fugu) is consumed by ingestion and provides nutritional value, yet its liver and certain other organs contain tetrodotoxin, a potent poison that can be lethal even in small quantities. It is harmful in normal quantities unless expertly prepared. The third condition fails, yet fugu is undeniably food — it is a prized delicacy in Japan.
missing_condition: The definition excludes foods that are inherently dangerous unless specially prepared; what matters is that the substance is treated and consumed as food within a cultural practice, not that it is intrinsically harmless.
passage: One might suppose that a substance is food if and only if it is consumed by ingestion, it provides nutritional value to the organism, and it is not harmful when consumed in normal quantities. This definition captures the paradigm cases: bread, fruit, meat, vegetables. Each is ingested, each provides nourishment, and none is harmful in ordinary amounts. Yet the definition proves too restrictive. Consider fugu, the pufferfish prized as a delicacy in Japanese cuisine. It is consumed by ingestion and provides nutritional value. But the pufferfish contains tetrodotoxin, concentrated in its liver, ovaries, and skin, which is among the most potent poisons known to science. A single fish, improperly prepared, can kill a man. The substance is harmful in the most extreme degree if consumed in the wrong manner. The third condition fails. Yet fugu is indisputably food; it has been prepared and eaten for centuries, and elaborate culinary traditions have developed around its safe handling. The definition has required intrinsic harmlessness, when in practice many foods — cassava, rhubarb leaves, certain mushrooms — are harmful unless properly prepared, and their status as food depends upon cultural knowledge and technique rather than upon innate safety.

definition: An object functions as money if and only if (i) it serves as a medium of exchange, (ii) it serves as a store of value, and (iii) it is issued or sanctioned by a recognised authority.
conditions: serves as medium of exchange
serves as store of value
issued or sanctioned by recognised authority
counterexample: Cigarettes in prisoner-of-war camps during the Second World War served as a medium of exchange and, to a reasonable degree, as a store of value. They were not issued or sanctioned by any authority — they emerged spontaneously as currency among the prisoners. The third condition fails, yet cigarettes functioned as money in every practical sense.
missing_condition: The definition requires official sanction, but money may arise spontaneously through social convention whenever a community converges on a commodity as a medium of exchange, without any authoritative decree.
passage: It is commonly said that an object functions as money if and only if it serves as a medium of exchange, it serves as a store of value, and it is issued or sanctioned by a recognised authority. This definition captures the familiar cases of coined and printed currency: the pound sterling, the franc, the dollar. Each serves as a medium of exchange, holds its value (more or less), and is issued by a central bank or treasury. Yet the definition is too narrow. Consider the use of cigarettes as money in prisoner-of-war camps during the Second World War, a phenomenon documented in detail by the economist R. A. Radford. In the camps, cigarettes became the universal medium of exchange: goods and services were priced in cigarettes, debts were denominated in cigarettes, and cigarettes circulated from hand to hand just as coins do in the outside world. They served, imperfectly but genuinely, as a store of value. Yet they were not issued or sanctioned by any recognised authority; they emerged as currency through the spontaneous convergence of the prisoners upon a convenient commodity. The third condition fails entirely. The definition has treated official sanction as essential, when in truth money is a social institution that may arise wherever a community settles upon a common medium of exchange, with or without the imprimatur of authority.

definition: A relationship is a friendship if and only if (i) both parties have mutual affection for one another, (ii) the relationship is voluntary, and (iii) both parties share personal confidences.
conditions: mutual affection
relationship is voluntary
both share personal confidences
counterexample: Two soldiers who have fought side by side in prolonged combat may regard one another with deep mutual affection and consider one another friends, having voluntarily chosen to maintain the bond. Yet they may never share personal confidences; their friendship is expressed entirely through loyalty, shared hardship, and wordless solidarity. The third condition fails, yet the friendship is genuine and profound.
missing_condition: The definition requires a specific mode of intimacy (sharing confidences) that is characteristic of some friendships but not all; friendships of shared endeavour or mutual loyalty may be no less genuine for their reticence.
passage: One might define friendship as a relationship in which both parties have mutual affection for one another, the relationship is voluntary, and both parties share personal confidences. This definition captures a familiar type of friendship — the intimate companionship in which friends confide their fears, their hopes, and their private thoughts. Yet it proves too narrow. Consider two soldiers who have served together through a long and arduous campaign. They have shared exhaustion, danger, and the loss of comrades. Each regards the other with deep affection and would, without hesitation, risk his life for the other. The relationship is voluntary in the relevant sense: it persists after the war, sustained by choice rather than by circumstance. Yet they never share personal confidences. Their bond is expressed through loyalty, through presence in difficulty, and through a wordless understanding born of shared experience. The third condition fails entirely; they are reticent men who do not speak of private matters. Yet no one who observed their relationship would deny that it is a friendship, and a deep one. The definition has mistaken one form of intimacy — the verbal sharing of confidences — for an essential feature of friendship, when in truth the bonds of shared endeavour and mutual loyalty may constitute a friendship fully as genuine.

definition: An action constitutes punishment if and only if (i) it imposes a burden or deprivation upon a person, (ii) it is imposed in response to a transgression, and (iii) it is administered by someone in authority over the person punished.
conditions: imposes burden or deprivation
imposed in response to transgression
administered by someone in authority
counterexample: A group of villagers collectively ostracises a man who has violated a social norm. The ostracism imposes a severe burden (isolation from community life) and is imposed in response to a transgression. But it is not administered by any single authority figure; it arises from the collective action of equals. The third condition fails, yet the ostracism is plainly a form of punishment.
missing_condition: The definition requires a hierarchical authority relation, but punishment may be administered collectively by a community of equals without any central authority.
passage: A standard account holds that an action constitutes punishment if and only if it imposes a burden or deprivation upon a person, it is imposed in response to a transgression, and it is administered by someone in authority over the person punished. This definition captures the paradigm cases: the judge sentences the criminal, the schoolmaster canes the pupil, the parent confines the child to his room. In each case, a recognised authority imposes a burden in response to wrongdoing. Yet the definition proves too narrow. Consider a small village in which a man has grievously violated a social norm — he has, let us say, betrayed a trust that the community regards as sacred. The villagers, acting collectively, ostracise him: they refuse to speak to him, exclude him from communal meals, and decline to trade with him. The ostracism imposes a severe burden, and it is plainly a response to his transgression. But it is not administered by any single authority figure. There is no chief, no magistrate, no designated punisher. The villagers are equals, and the punishment arises from their collective decision. The third condition fails. Yet the ostracism is punishment in every meaningful sense — it is a deliberate infliction of deprivation in response to wrongdoing. The definition has required a hierarchical authority relation where none need exist.

definition: A transfer of property is a gift if and only if (i) it is voluntary, (ii) it is made without expectation of return, and (iii) it is intended to benefit the recipient.
conditions: voluntary transfer
made without expectation of return
intended to benefit recipient
counterexample: In many societies, the exchange of gifts at weddings or festivals carries a strong social expectation of reciprocity: one gives precisely because one expects to receive in return. The transfer is voluntary and intended to benefit the recipient, but the second condition fails — there is a clear expectation of return. Yet these exchanges are universally called gifts.
missing_condition: The definition idealises the gift as a purely altruistic act, when in practice gift-giving is deeply embedded in social systems of reciprocity and obligation.
passage: One naturally supposes that a transfer of property is a gift if and only if it is voluntary, it is made without expectation of return, and it is intended to benefit the recipient. This definition captures the pure case: a man gives his friend a book because he thinks the friend will enjoy it, expecting nothing in return. Yet the definition proves too idealised when confronted with the rich anthropological evidence concerning gift exchange. In many societies, the giving of gifts at weddings, funerals, or seasonal festivals carries a powerful social expectation of reciprocity. A family that receives a generous wedding gift is expected to reciprocate with a gift of comparable value when the giver's child is married. The transfer is voluntary in the sense that no legal compulsion attaches to it, and it is intended to benefit the recipient. But the second condition — that the gift is made without expectation of return — fails entirely. The giver expects, and the community expects, that the gift will be repaid in kind. Yet these transfers are universally called gifts, and the practices surrounding them are called gift-giving. The definition has taken the idealised, purely disinterested transfer as the paradigm, when in truth most gift-giving is embedded in elaborate systems of social reciprocity and obligation.

definition: A place is a person's home if and only if (i) he resides there, (ii) he has a legal right to occupy it, and (iii) it provides him with shelter and security.
conditions: person resides there
has legal right to occupy it
provides shelter and security
counterexample: A man who has lived for twenty years in the same house, which provides him shelter and security, loses his legal right to occupy it through a bank's foreclosure. He continues to reside there while the eviction proceedings are pending. The second condition fails, yet the house remains, in every meaningful sense, his home.
missing_condition: The definition ties the concept of home to a legal right of occupation, but the sense in which a place is one's home depends upon habitual residence, emotional attachment, and personal history, not upon legal title.
passage: It is tempting to define a person's home as a place where he resides, to which he has a legal right of occupation, and which provides him with shelter and security. This definition captures the ordinary case: a man lives in his house, holds the deed or lease, and is sheltered and secure within it. Yet the definition is too closely tied to legal entitlement. Consider a man who has lived in the same house for twenty years. He has raised his children there, received his neighbours, and accumulated the associations of a lifetime. The house provides him with shelter and security. But through misfortune he falls behind on his payments, and the bank forecloses. He has lost his legal right to occupy the house, though the eviction proceedings have not yet been completed and he continues to reside there. The second condition fails. Yet we should surely say that the house remains his home. He belongs to it, in a sense, as much as it belongs — or belonged — to him. The concept of home is not primarily a legal one; it is bound up with habitual residence, emotional attachment, and the accumulation of personal history. A man may lose his legal right to a place without that place ceasing to be his home.

definition: An object is a weapon if and only if (i) it is designed to inflict harm, (ii) it is capable of causing injury or death, and (iii) it is used or intended to be used against a person or animal.
conditions: designed to inflict harm
capable of causing injury or death
used or intended for use against person or animal
counterexample: A kitchen knife is not designed to inflict harm — it is designed to prepare food. Yet when a man takes up a kitchen knife in a quarrel and threatens his adversary, the knife becomes a weapon: it is capable of causing injury and is intended for use against a person. The first condition fails, yet the knife is a weapon in the relevant circumstances.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the object be designed for harm, but any object becomes a weapon when it is employed or intended for use as an instrument of violence, regardless of its original design purpose.
passage: A plausible definition holds that an object is a weapon if and only if it is designed to inflict harm, it is capable of causing injury or death, and it is used or intended to be used against a person or animal. This definition captures the paradigm cases: the sword, the rifle, the spear. Each is designed for the purpose of inflicting harm, each is manifestly capable of causing injury, and each is used against persons or animals. But consider a kitchen knife. It is designed for the preparation of food — for slicing vegetables, carving meat, and the like. Its design purpose is entirely domestic. Now suppose a man, in the heat of a quarrel, seizes a kitchen knife from the counter and advances upon his adversary. The knife is capable of causing grave injury, and it is intended for use against a person. The second and third conditions are met. But the first fails: the knife was not designed to inflict harm. Yet in the circumstances described, the knife is a weapon. The police would call it a weapon, the court would call it a weapon, and common sense would call it a weapon. The definition has erred in requiring a design purpose directed at harm; what makes something a weapon is not its design but its employment or intended employment as an instrument of violence.

definition: An activity is an art if and only if (i) it requires acquired skill, (ii) the skill is exercised in the production of an object or performance, and (iii) the object or performance is valued for its aesthetic qualities.
conditions: requires acquired skill
skill exercised in production of object or performance
object or performance valued for aesthetic qualities
counterexample: The art of surgery requires great acquired skill and is exercised in the production of a result (the healed patient), yet the result is not valued for its aesthetic qualities but for its therapeutic ones. We nonetheless speak of the 'art' of surgery, and the usage is not merely metaphorical. The third condition fails.
missing_condition: The definition restricts 'art' to the aesthetic domain, but in its broader everyday sense the word designates any practice requiring consummate skill and judgement, whether or not the result is aesthetically valued.
passage: One might define an art as an activity that requires acquired skill, in which the skill is exercised in the production of an object or performance, and in which the object or performance is valued for its aesthetic qualities. This definition captures the fine arts admirably: painting, sculpture, music, and poetry all satisfy the three conditions. Yet the word 'art' has a broader use in everyday speech that the definition fails to accommodate. Consider the practice of surgery. It requires skill of a very high order — skill acquired through years of training and practice. The surgeon exercises this skill in the production of a result: the repair of a damaged organ, the excision of a tumour, the restoration of a patient's health. Yet the result is valued not for its aesthetic qualities but for its therapeutic ones. The third condition fails. Nevertheless, we commonly speak of the art of surgery, and the usage does not seem merely figurative; there is something genuinely art-like about a practice that demands such refined judgement and dexterity. The same may be said of the art of navigation, the art of diplomacy, or the art of cookery. The definition has captured one sense of the word but has missed the broader sense in which an art is any practice requiring consummate skill and judgement, regardless of whether the product is valued aesthetically.

definition: An activity is a sport if and only if (i) it involves physical exertion, (ii) it is governed by a codified set of rules, and (iii) it involves competition between individuals or teams.
conditions: involves physical exertion
governed by codified rules
involves competition
counterexample: Chess is governed by a codified set of rules and involves competition between individuals, yet the physical exertion it requires is negligible. The first condition fails, yet chess is recognised as a sport by the International Olympic Committee and is commonly referred to as such.
missing_condition: The definition insists upon physical exertion, but the concept of sport extends to activities demanding primarily mental skill, provided they are competitive and governed by formal rules.
passage: A natural definition holds that an activity is a sport if and only if it involves physical exertion, it is governed by a codified set of rules, and it involves competition between individuals or teams. This definition captures the central cases: football, tennis, swimming, athletics. Each demands physical effort, each is governed by elaborate rules, and each is competitive. Yet the definition is too narrow in at least one important respect. Consider chess. It is governed by a precise and codified set of rules. It involves intense competition between individuals. Tournaments are organised, rankings are maintained, and international championships are held. The International Olympic Committee recognises chess as a sport. Yet the physical exertion involved in playing chess is negligible: the players sit at a table and move small pieces of wood or ivory. The first condition fails. If the definition is correct, chess is not a sport — a conclusion that conflicts with the considered judgement of a great many people and organisations. The definition has taken the athleticism of the paradigm cases as essential, when in fact the concept of sport is broad enough to encompass activities whose competitive demands are primarily mental rather than physical.

definition: A sequence of sounds constitutes music if and only if (i) the sounds are organised according to principles of melody, harmony, or rhythm, (ii) the organisation is intentional, and (iii) the result is intended to be heard by an audience.
conditions: sounds organised by melody, harmony, or rhythm
organisation is intentional
intended to be heard by audience
counterexample: John Cage's composition 4′33″ consists of four minutes and thirty-three seconds of silence: the performer sits at the piano without playing a note. There is no melody, no harmony, and no rhythm. Yet it is intentional, it is presented to an audience, and it is widely regarded as a work of music. The first condition fails entirely.
missing_condition: The definition requires the positive organisation of sounds, but music may consist in the framing of silence or ambient sound within a performative context; what is essential is the act of musical presentation, not the presence of organised tones.
passage: It is natural to suppose that a sequence of sounds constitutes music if and only if the sounds are organised according to principles of melody, harmony, or rhythm, the organisation is intentional, and the result is intended to be heard by an audience. This definition captures the great bulk of what we call music, from a folk song to a symphony. Yet it encounters a celebrated difficulty. In 1952, the composer John Cage presented a work entitled 4′33″, in which a performer sits at the piano for four minutes and thirty-three seconds without playing a single note. The 'music' consists of whatever ambient sounds happen to occur during the performance: the rustle of programmes, the coughing of the audience, the hum of the ventilation. There is no melody, no harmony, and no rhythm in any conventional sense. The first condition fails entirely. Yet the work is intentional — Cage composed it with deliberation — and it is presented to an audience. It is catalogued as a musical composition, performed in concert halls, and discussed in the literature of musicology. The definition has identified music with the organisation of sounds, but Cage's work suggests that music may consist in the framing of silence and ambient noise within a context of performance and attention. The boundary of music, it appears, is wider than the definition allows.

definition: An activity is work if and only if (i) it requires effort, (ii) it is undertaken for the purpose of producing something useful, and (iii) it is remunerated.
conditions: requires effort
undertaken to produce something useful
remunerated
counterexample: A woman who spends her days caring for her children and maintaining the household exerts considerable effort and produces something indisputably useful — a functioning home and the well-being of her family. Yet she receives no remuneration. The third condition fails, yet her activity is plainly work.
missing_condition: The definition ties work to payment, but a great deal of essential work — domestic labour, voluntary service, subsistence farming — is performed without remuneration and is no less work for that.
passage: A common definition holds that an activity is work if and only if it requires effort, it is undertaken for the purpose of producing something useful, and it is remunerated. This definition captures the paradigm cases of employment: the factory worker, the clerk, the tradesman. Each exerts effort, each produces something useful, and each is paid for his labour. Yet the definition excludes a vast category of activity that is, by any reasonable account, work. Consider a woman who spends her days caring for young children, preparing meals, cleaning the house, and managing the affairs of the household. She exerts considerable effort — more, perhaps, than many who are employed outside the home. She produces something indisputably useful: a functioning household, nourished and cared-for children, the conditions under which the rest of the family can go about their lives. Yet she receives no remuneration; no one pays her a wage for her labour. The third condition fails. Yet to say that she does not work is absurd; her labour is essential to the welfare of her family and, by extension, to the functioning of society. The definition has mistaken a feature of wage employment — remuneration — for an essential feature of work as such. Much of the world's most important work is done without pay.

definition: A group of persons constitutes a family if and only if (i) they are related by blood or marriage, (ii) they share a common household, and (iii) they have reciprocal obligations of care and support.
conditions: related by blood or marriage
share common household
reciprocal obligations of care and support
counterexample: An adopted child is not related by blood to his adoptive parents, yet the adoptive parents, the child, and any biological children of the parents plainly constitute a family. They share a household and have reciprocal obligations of care, but the first condition fails for the adopted child. The relationship is established by law and affection, not by blood or marriage.
missing_condition: The definition restricts family to biological and marital ties, but families may be constituted by adoption, fostering, or bonds of sustained mutual commitment that have no basis in blood or marriage.
passage: One might define a family as a group of persons related by blood or marriage, sharing a common household, and bound by reciprocal obligations of care and support. This definition captures the nuclear family of common experience: parents, children, perhaps grandparents, united by ties of blood and marriage under a single roof. Yet it proves too restrictive. Consider a child who has been adopted in infancy. He shares a household with his adoptive parents and any biological children they may have. The obligations of care and support are fully reciprocal: the parents care for the child, and the child, as he grows, cares for the parents. By every experiential and moral criterion, the group constitutes a family. But the adopted child is not related to his adoptive parents by blood, and the relationship between them is not one of marriage. The first condition fails. The definition would exclude him from the family, which is plainly wrong. The concept of family is not exhausted by biological and marital ties; it extends to relationships established by adoption, fostering, and bonds of sustained mutual commitment. The definition has privileged one basis of family membership — consanguinity and marriage — when the concept is in truth broader.

definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is governed by a fixed set of explicit rules, (ii) A involves two or more competing participants, and (iii) A terminates in a determinate outcome whereby one participant wins and the others lose.
conditions: A is governed by a fixed set of explicit rules
A involves two or more competing participants
A terminates in a determinate outcome whereby one participant wins and the others lose
counterexample: Consider a child playing a solitary game of patience — laying out cards upon a table, turning them according to familiar rules, seeking to arrange the entire deck into ordered sequences. The activity is governed by a fixed set of explicit rules, and it terminates in a determinate outcome (the child either succeeds or fails). Yet there is no second participant; the child plays alone. The second condition fails, and the game cannot produce a winner and loser in the competitive sense the definition requires. The third condition is likewise unsatisfied. Nevertheless, patience is unquestionably a game.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that all games are competitive and multi-player, yet many paradigmatic games — patience, solitaire, various puzzles — are solitary activities in which the player contends not against another person but against the structure of the game itself. What is wanted is a broader account of what it is for an activity to have a game-like character, one that does not require the presence of an opponent.
passage: One might propose that an activity is a game if and only if it is governed by a fixed set of explicit rules, it involves two or more competing participants, and it terminates in a determinate outcome whereby one participant wins and the others lose. The definition has considerable initial plausibility. Chess, draughts, tennis, and cricket all satisfy these conditions: each is rule-governed, each involves competition between two or more players, and each concludes with a winner and a loser, or at least with a determinate result. One might suppose that these three features jointly capture what it is to be a game. Yet one must consider the case of patience. A person sits alone at a table, lays out a pack of cards according to well-known rules, and attempts to arrange the entire deck into ordered sequences. The activity is governed by explicit rules — rules as precise and binding as those of chess. And it terminates in a determinate outcome: the player either succeeds in ordering the deck or fails. But there is no second participant. The player contends against the arrangement of the cards, against the structure of the game itself, not against another person. The second condition fails entirely, and since there is no opponent, the third condition — which requires that one participant win and the others lose — is unsatisfied as well. Yet patience is a game; no competent speaker of English would hesitate to call it one. The difficulty is that the definition has generalised from competitive, multi-player activities and mistaken competition for a necessary feature of games as such.

definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is engaged in for the purpose of amusement or recreation, (ii) A is clearly demarcated from ordinary life by spatial or temporal boundaries, and (iii) A is trivial in the sense that nothing of serious consequence depends upon its outcome.
conditions: A is engaged in for the purpose of amusement or recreation
A is clearly demarcated from ordinary life by spatial or temporal boundaries
A is trivial in the sense that nothing of serious consequence depends upon its outcome
counterexample: Consider the case of a professional chess player competing in a world championship match. The player engages in an activity — chess — that is indisputably a game. The match is demarcated from ordinary life by clear spatial and temporal boundaries: it takes place upon a stage, within fixed time controls, according to tournament regulations. Yet the player does not play for mere amusement; the player's livelihood, reputation, and standing in the chess world depend upon the outcome. The third condition fails decisively, for the result is of the gravest consequence. Nevertheless, world championship chess is a game.
missing_condition: The definition conflates the typical motive for playing games — amusement — with a necessary condition, and it wrongly assumes that games must be inconsequential. Many games, when played at the highest levels, carry serious professional, financial, and reputational stakes, yet they remain games for all that. The concept of a game does not require triviality of outcome.
passage: Suppose one holds that an activity is a game if and only if it is engaged in for the purpose of amusement or recreation, it is clearly demarcated from ordinary life by spatial or temporal boundaries, and it is trivial in the sense that nothing of serious consequence depends upon its outcome. This definition captures something important about many familiar games. Children playing tag in a garden, friends gathering for an evening of cards, a family assembling a jigsaw puzzle on a rainy afternoon — these activities are recreational, bounded in time and space, and inconsequential in the sense that nothing of lasting importance hangs upon the result. One might suppose that triviality and the motive of amusement are essential to the concept. Yet one must attend to the case of professional chess at the highest level. When two grandmasters meet in a world championship match, they engage in an activity that is indisputably a game — chess is the paradigm of a game. The match is spatially and temporally bounded: it is played upon a stage, under tournament rules, with fixed time controls. But the players do not play for amusement. They play with an intensity and seriousness that rivals that of any professional endeavour. And the outcome is far from trivial: the winner's career, reputation, and financial security are materially affected, and the result will be remembered and discussed for decades. The third condition fails, and the first is at best only partially satisfied. The definition has confused a feature frequently accompanying casual play — the absence of serious stakes — with a necessary condition of games as such. What is wanted is a definition that accommodates both the casual garden game and the professional championship.

definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A involves the manipulation of physical objects such as balls, cards, or pieces, (ii) A requires bodily movement or physical dexterity, and (iii) A takes place in a designated physical space such as a field, court, or board.
conditions: A involves the manipulation of physical objects such as balls, cards, or pieces
A requires bodily movement or physical dexterity
A takes place in a designated physical space such as a field, court, or board
counterexample: Consider the game of twenty questions, in which one player thinks of an object and the others attempt to identify it by asking up to twenty questions answerable only by 'yes' or 'no.' This is manifestly a game: it has rules, it has participants, and it is played with evident enjoyment. Yet it involves no physical objects — no balls, no cards, no pieces. It requires no bodily movement or dexterity; the players may sit perfectly still. And it takes place in no designated physical space; it may be played anywhere — in a drawing room, on a train, while walking along a road. All three conditions fail, yet twenty questions is a game.
missing_condition: The definition identifies games with their physical apparatus, yet many games are purely verbal or mental, requiring no objects, no bodily skill, and no special location. What is essential to a game is not physical embodiment but the presence of a rule-governed structure within which participants act.
passage: One might define a game as an activity that involves the manipulation of physical objects such as balls, cards, or pieces, that requires bodily movement or physical dexterity, and that takes place in a designated physical space. The definition has a natural appeal, for it describes a great many familiar games. Football involves a ball and bodily movement upon a pitch. Chess involves the manipulation of pieces upon a board. Tennis requires racquets and balls and is played upon a court. One might think that the physicality of these activities — the objects, the movement, the space — is what makes them games. Yet one must consider twenty questions. In this game, one player selects an object in thought, and the remaining players attempt to identify it by posing up to twenty questions, each answerable only by 'yes' or 'no.' The game has rules, and it is plainly a game: it is played at parties, on long journeys, in schools, and in homes. Yet there are no physical objects to manipulate — no ball, no card, no piece. No bodily movement or dexterity is required; the players may sit in perfect stillness. And no designated physical space is needed; the game may be played anywhere at all, in any posture, in any setting. All three conditions of the definition fail. The difficulty is that the definition has taken the physical apparatus of certain prominent games to be essential to the concept of a game itself, when in truth a game may be entirely verbal, entirely mental, and entirely independent of physical embodiment.

definition: An object x is a chair if and only if (i) x has four legs, (ii) x has a flat horizontal seat, and (iii) x has a vertical back-rest.
conditions: x has four legs
x has a flat horizontal seat
x has a vertical back-rest
counterexample: Consider a three-legged milking stool. It has a flat horizontal seat upon which one may sit comfortably, and it is used in precisely the manner one uses a chair — one sits upon it while performing a task. Yet it has three legs, not four, and it has no back-rest whatsoever. The first and third conditions fail. Nevertheless, a milking stool is, in ordinary parlance, a kind of chair, or at the very least a piece of furniture that serves the characteristic function of a chair.
missing_condition: The definition mistakes a common configuration — four legs and a back-rest — for a necessary one. Many objects that function as chairs, and that are called chairs, depart from this configuration. A rocking chair has curved runners rather than four discrete legs; a bean-bag chair has no legs and no back-rest in the relevant sense. What unifies chairs is their function, not their geometry.
passage: One might propose that an object is a chair if and only if it has four legs, it has a flat horizontal seat, and it has a vertical back-rest. The proposal has a straightforward appeal. The kitchen chair, the dining chair, the desk chair — all have four legs, a horizontal seat, and a back-rest. One might suppose that these features are jointly necessary and sufficient for an object to be a chair. Yet one must consider the three-legged milking stool. A milking stool is a low, sturdy seat used by dairy workers; it has a flat horizontal surface upon which one sits, and it is used in precisely the fashion one uses any chair — one lowers oneself onto the seat and remains seated while performing a task. But a milking stool has three legs, not four, and it has no back-rest of any kind. The first and third conditions of the definition fail. Yet the milking stool is a piece of seating furniture that fulfils the central function of a chair. Nor is the milking stool an isolated difficulty. A rocking chair has no four discrete legs but curved runners; a bean-bag chair has no rigid structure at all; a folding stool has legs that cross rather than standing independently. The definition has identified the particular geometrical configuration of one common type of chair with the concept of a chair itself, when what is essential to chairs is not their geometry but their capacity to serve as seats for human beings.

definition: An object x is a chair if and only if (i) x is manufactured for the purpose of seating, (ii) x is designed to accommodate exactly one person, and (iii) x is made of wood, metal, or plastic.
conditions: x is manufactured for the purpose of seating
x is designed to accommodate exactly one person
x is made of wood, metal, or plastic
counterexample: Consider a large flat-topped boulder in a garden, placed there by a landscape designer specifically to serve as a seat. The designer selected the boulder for its height, its flat upper surface, and its suitability for sitting. It was positioned with the explicit purpose of providing seating for one person. It accommodates exactly one person. Yet it is made of stone — not wood, metal, or plastic. The third condition fails. The boulder is, by the designer's intention and by the use to which it is put, a chair, yet the definition excludes it.
missing_condition: The definition restricts the material composition of chairs to a closed list, yet chairs may be made of virtually any material — stone, wicker, leather stretched over a frame, upholstered fabric, even compressed paper. What matters is not the substance but the form and function.
passage: Suppose one defines a chair as an object manufactured for the purpose of seating, designed to accommodate exactly one person, and made of wood, metal, or plastic. The first two conditions seem unexceptionable: chairs are indeed manufactured for seating, and a chair, as distinct from a bench or a sofa, is typically designed for a single occupant. The third condition names the three materials most commonly used in chair construction, and one might suppose that these three exhaust the relevant possibilities. Yet one must consider the garden boulder. A landscape designer, wishing to provide seating in a garden, selects a large, flat-topped boulder of suitable height and positions it deliberately as a seat. The boulder serves admirably: visitors sit upon it, rest upon it, and treat it as a chair in every functional respect. It was manufactured — or at least arranged — for the purpose of seating, and it accommodates exactly one person. But it is made of stone, a material absent from the definition's list. The third condition fails. Nor is stone the only material the definition overlooks. Chairs have been fashioned from wicker, from rattan, from bamboo, from leather stretched upon a frame, from compressed cardboard, and from woven fabric suspended upon a metal skeleton. The definition has enumerated the most common materials and mistaken a contingent generalisation for a necessary condition, when in truth a chair may be made of any substance whatsoever, provided it can support the weight of a seated person.

definition: An object x is a chair if and only if (i) x is a freestanding, movable piece of furniture, (ii) x supports a person in a seated posture with the thighs approximately horizontal, and (iii) x is located indoors.
conditions: x is a freestanding, movable piece of furniture
x supports a person in a seated posture with the thighs approximately horizontal
x is located indoors
counterexample: Consider a deckchair on the beach. It is freestanding and movable; one may carry it from place to place. It supports a person in a seated posture (albeit at a reclined angle, the thighs are approximately horizontal relative to the frame). Yet it is located outdoors — on a beach, a lawn, a terrace. The third condition fails. A deckchair is indisputably a chair; the word 'chair' is contained within its very name. The definition wrongly restricts chairs to indoor settings.
missing_condition: The definition supposes that chairs are essentially indoor objects, but many paradigmatic chairs — deckchairs, garden chairs, park benches fitted with individual seats — are designed for outdoor use. Location is irrelevant to whether an object is a chair; what matters is form and function, not setting.
passage: One might hold that an object is a chair if and only if it is a freestanding, movable piece of furniture, it supports a person in a seated posture with the thighs approximately horizontal, and it is located indoors. The definition captures a large class of familiar chairs: the kitchen chair, the armchair by the fire, the office chair at a desk. These are freestanding, movable, supportive of a seated posture, and located within buildings. One might be tempted to suppose that indoor location is essential to the concept, perhaps on the ground that chairs are items of domestic or commercial furniture, and furniture is, by its nature, found indoors. Yet one must consider the deckchair. A deckchair is a light, portable, foldable seat made of canvas stretched upon a wooden or metal frame. It is freestanding and movable; it supports a person in a seated or semi-reclined posture; and it is a chair — the word 'chair' appears in its very name. But a deckchair is characteristically found outdoors: on the beach, on the lawn, on the deck of a ship. The same is true of the garden chair, the camp chair, and the canvas director's chair used on film sets. These are all chairs, and they are all outdoor objects. The third condition of the definition fails in every such case. The difficulty is that the definition has been drawn from a sample of indoor chairs and has mistaken their location for a necessary property. Chairs may be found indoors or out, and the concept of a chair is indifferent to setting.

definition: An object x is a work of art if and only if (i) x is a unique, singular object, (ii) x is created by the hand of an individual artist, and (iii) x hangs upon a wall or stands upon a pedestal.
conditions: x is a unique, singular object
x is created by the hand of an individual artist
x hangs upon a wall or stands upon a pedestal
counterexample: Consider a woodcut print produced in an edition of fifty. Each impression is pulled from the same carved block, and the fifty impressions are, in the relevant respects, identical. The woodcut is created by the hand of an individual artist, who carved the block and pulled the prints. Yet the work is not a unique, singular object: it exists in fifty impressions. And the prints need not hang upon a wall or stand upon a pedestal; they may be collected in a portfolio, stored in a drawer, or bound into a book. The first and third conditions fail, yet the woodcut is a work of art.
missing_condition: The definition confuses the properties of one category of art — the unique painting or sculpture displayed in a gallery — with necessary conditions of art as such. Many works of art exist in multiple copies (prints, photographs, cast bronzes), and many are not displayed on walls or pedestals (books, musical scores, films). What the definition lacks is recognition of the diversity of artistic media and modes of presentation.
passage: Suppose one proposes that an object is a work of art if and only if it is a unique, singular object, it is created by the hand of an individual artist, and it hangs upon a wall or stands upon a pedestal. The definition describes the paradigm case as many persons conceive it: the oil painting in its frame upon the gallery wall, the marble sculpture upon its plinth. Such objects are unique, handmade, and displayed in the manner the definition describes. One might think these features essential to art. Yet one must consider the woodcut print. A woodcut is created by an artist who carves an image into a block of wood, inks the surface, and presses paper against it to produce an impression. The process may be repeated: the artist may pull fifty, a hundred, or more impressions from the same block, each substantially identical to the others. The woodcut is unquestionably a work of art — Dürer's woodcuts are among the treasures of European art — yet it is not a unique, singular object. It exists in multiple copies. Furthermore, woodcut prints are commonly stored in portfolios, collected in albums, or housed in the drawers of print cabinets; they need not hang upon a wall. The first and third conditions fail. The definition has taken the features of the unique oil painting displayed in a gallery to be necessary features of art itself, when in truth art encompasses printmaking, photography, literature, music, and a great deal else besides.

definition: An object x is a work of art if and only if (i) x depicts or represents something — a person, a scene, a narrative, (ii) x is produced with the intention of being beautiful, and (iii) x requires technical skill in its execution.
conditions: x depicts or represents something — a person, a scene, a narrative
x is produced with the intention of being beautiful
x requires technical skill in its execution
counterexample: Consider a purely abstract painting — a canvas covered in overlapping rectangles of colour, with no representational content whatsoever. The painting depicts nothing: no person, no scene, no narrative. It is composed of flat planes of pigment arranged according to principles of colour harmony and spatial proportion. Yet it is a work of art. It may hang in a major gallery, it may be the subject of extensive critical discussion, and it may be the product of an artist of acknowledged standing. The first condition fails entirely, for the painting represents nothing at all.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that all art is representational, yet abstract art — from the geometrical compositions of the early twentieth century onward — represents nothing and depicts nothing. Art need not depict in order to be art. What is essential is not representation but the purposeful arrangement of formal elements within an artistic tradition.
passage: One might define a work of art as an object that depicts or represents something — a person, a scene, a narrative — that is produced with the intention of being beautiful, and that requires technical skill in its execution. The definition appears to capture a great many paradigm cases. A portrait depicts a person; a landscape depicts a scene; a history painting depicts a narrative. These are produced with the aim of beauty and demand considerable technical skill. One might suppose that representational content is essential to art. Yet one must consider the abstract painting. An artist produces a canvas covered in overlapping rectangles of colour — red, blue, yellow, black — arranged according to principles of proportion and chromatic harmony. The painting depicts nothing whatsoever. There is no person, no scene, no narrative; there is only colour and form. Yet the painting is a work of art. It hangs in galleries alongside representational works; it is discussed by critics with the same seriousness; it belongs to a tradition of artistic practice stretching back more than a century. The first condition of the definition — that the work must depict or represent something — fails entirely. The definition has been drawn from representational art and has mistaken a feature common to that tradition for a necessary feature of art as such. Abstract art reveals that what is essential to a work of art is not the depiction of external reality but something else — perhaps the purposeful arrangement of formal elements, perhaps the situation of the work within an ongoing artistic practice.

definition: An object x is a work of art if and only if (i) x is made by hand without mechanical assistance, (ii) x is composed of traditional materials such as oil paint, marble, or bronze, and (iii) x is intended to be permanent and enduring.
conditions: x is made by hand without mechanical assistance
x is composed of traditional materials such as oil paint, marble, or bronze
x is intended to be permanent and enduring
counterexample: Consider a sand sculpture created on a beach for a festival — an elaborate, carefully wrought figure moulded from damp sand. The sculptor works by hand, and the result is widely acknowledged as a work of art. Yet the sand sculpture is not composed of traditional artistic materials; it is composed of sand and water. Moreover, it is not intended to be permanent and enduring; the sculptor knows that the tide will destroy it within hours. The second and third conditions fail, yet the sand sculpture is a work of art for the duration of its brief existence.
missing_condition: The definition restricts art to permanent objects made of traditional materials, yet many acknowledged works of art are deliberately ephemeral — ice sculptures, sand mandalas, performance art, chalk drawings on pavements. The concept of art does not require permanence; a work may be no less a work of art for being transient.
passage: Suppose one holds that an object is a work of art if and only if it is made by hand without mechanical assistance, it is composed of traditional materials such as oil paint, marble, or bronze, and it is intended to be permanent and enduring. The definition captures a venerable conception of art. The great paintings of the Renaissance, the sculptures of antiquity, the bronzes of the Baroque — all are handmade from traditional materials and intended to endure for centuries. One might suppose that permanence and traditional craft are inseparable from the concept of art. Yet one must consider the sand sculpture. At festivals and exhibitions around the world, artists create elaborate figures from damp sand — human forms, architectural fantasies, scenes of great intricacy and skill. The sculptures are made by hand and are widely acknowledged as works of art; they are photographed, exhibited, and awarded prizes. Yet they are composed of sand, not of oil paint, marble, or bronze. The second condition fails. More significantly, they are not intended to be permanent: the sculptor knows that the tide, the wind, or the passage of a few hours will reduce the work to a formless heap. The third condition fails. The sand sculpture is art — but it is ephemeral art, art that exists for a day or an afternoon and is then gone. The definition, drawn from the tradition of permanent, museum-worthy objects, has mistaken durability for a necessary condition of art, when in truth a work may be no less a work of art for being deliberately transient.

definition: A system S is a language if and only if (i) S consists of spoken words produced by the human vocal apparatus, (ii) S has a grammar — a set of rules governing the combination of words into sentences, and (iii) S is learned in childhood from other speakers.
conditions: S consists of spoken words produced by the human vocal apparatus
S has a grammar — a set of rules governing the combination of words into sentences
S is learned in childhood from other speakers
counterexample: Consider a sign language used by a community of deaf persons. It has a grammar — a set of rules governing the combination of manual signs into well-formed expressions — and it is learned in childhood from other signers. It can express the full range of human thought and feeling. Yet it does not consist of spoken words produced by the human vocal apparatus; it consists of manual signs, facial expressions, and bodily movements. The first condition fails. Nevertheless, sign language is indisputably a language.
missing_condition: The definition identifies language with speech, but language may be realised in any modality — vocal, manual, or written. Sign languages possess all the structural properties of spoken languages (grammar, productivity, displacement) and are languages in the fullest sense. The definition has confused the most common medium of language with a necessary feature of language itself.
passage: One might propose that a system is a language if and only if it consists of spoken words produced by the human vocal apparatus, it has a grammar governing the combination of words into sentences, and it is learned in childhood from other speakers. The definition has considerable plausibility. English, French, Mandarin, and Swahili all satisfy these conditions: they are spoken, grammatical, and acquired in childhood. One might suppose that the spoken character of language is essential to the concept, perhaps on the ground that human beings have always communicated primarily through speech. Yet one must consider the sign languages used by communities of deaf persons. A sign language — such as the one used by the deaf community in Britain — has a grammar of considerable complexity, with rules governing the formation and combination of signs into well-formed expressions. It is learned in childhood, typically from deaf parents or from peers in schools for the deaf. It can express abstract thought, convey nuance and irony, and serve all the communicative functions that spoken language serves. But it does not consist of spoken words produced by the vocal apparatus. It consists of manual signs formed by the hands and fingers, accompanied by facial expressions and movements of the head and body. The first condition of the definition fails entirely. The definition has identified language with its most common medium — speech — and has thereby excluded an entire class of genuine languages. What is essential to language is not the vocal modality but the possession of a productive, rule-governed system of symbols capable of expressing an unbounded range of meanings.

definition: A system S is a language if and only if (i) S has a fixed vocabulary of words with stable meanings, (ii) S changes only very slowly over long periods of time, and (iii) S is codified in a standard grammar and a published dictionary.
conditions: S has a fixed vocabulary of words with stable meanings
S changes only very slowly over long periods of time
S is codified in a standard grammar and a published dictionary
counterexample: Consider the language spoken by the inhabitants of an isolated village in a remote region — a tongue that has never been written down, that possesses no published grammar and no dictionary, and whose vocabulary shifts and adapts with each generation as speakers coin new words, borrow from neighbours, and allow old terms to fall into disuse. This tongue has a grammar, a vocabulary, and a community of native speakers. It is a language in every meaningful sense. Yet it is not codified in any standard grammar or dictionary, and its vocabulary is neither fixed nor stable. The first and third conditions fail.
missing_condition: The definition conflates language with its codification. Many languages — indeed, the majority of the world's languages — have never been written down, have no published grammars or dictionaries, and are characterised by rapid and continuous change. Codification is a cultural product applied to some languages; it is not a necessary feature of language as such.
passage: Suppose one holds that a system is a language if and only if it has a fixed vocabulary of words with stable meanings, it changes only very slowly over long periods of time, and it is codified in a standard grammar and a published dictionary. The definition reflects a common view: languages are stable, codified systems — one thinks of the Académie française preserving the purity of French, or of the Oxford English Dictionary recording the settled meanings of English words. Yet one must consider the many tongues spoken by communities in remote regions of the world — tongues that have never been written down, that possess no published grammar, and that have no dictionary. Such a language is spoken by the inhabitants of an isolated village; it is their mother tongue, learned in infancy, used in all the affairs of daily life. It has a grammar — the speakers combine words according to consistent rules — and it has a vocabulary, though the vocabulary shifts and changes with each generation. New words are coined, old words fall out of use, meanings drift and evolve. The language is not codified: no scholar has compiled its grammar, no lexicographer has catalogued its words. The first and third conditions of the definition fail. Yet the tongue is a language in every meaningful sense: it serves all the communicative functions that any language serves, and its speakers use it with the same fluency and expressiveness as speakers of any codified tongue. The definition has confused language with its representation in books, mistaking the products of scholarship for necessary features of the phenomenon itself.

definition: A system S is a language if and only if (i) S can be used to make true or false statements about the world, (ii) S is used for the communication of factual information between persons, and (iii) S is translatable into every other language without loss of meaning.
conditions: S can be used to make true or false statements about the world
S is used for the communication of factual information between persons
S is translatable into every other language without loss of meaning
counterexample: Consider a language possessing an extensive vocabulary for kinship relations — dozens of distinct terms for cousins of different degrees, for in-laws of various removes, for relations that English collapses into a single word. Many of these terms have no precise equivalent in English or in other European languages; the kinship distinctions they mark are simply not made in those tongues. The language satisfies the first two conditions: it can be used to make true or false statements and to communicate factual information. But it is not translatable into every other language without loss of meaning; the fine-grained kinship terminology resists exact translation. The third condition fails.
missing_condition: The definition supposes that perfect translatability is a necessary feature of language, but the existence of terms, idioms, and grammatical structures that resist translation from one language to another is a well-established phenomenon. Languages carve the world differently, and the impossibility of lossless translation between all pairs of languages does not deprive any particular tongue of its status as a language.
passage: One might propose that a system is a language if and only if it can be used to make true or false statements about the world, it is used for the communication of factual information, and it is translatable into every other language without loss of meaning. The third condition reflects a tempting assumption: that all languages, being systems for expressing truths about a common world, must be mutually intertranslatable — that anything sayable in one language is sayable in every other. Yet one must consider languages whose vocabularies carve the world in ways radically different from one's own. There exist languages with extensive and finely differentiated kinship terminologies — dozens of distinct terms for relations that English designates with a single word, terms for specific degrees of cousinship, for particular kinds of in-law relationships, for connections through maternal or paternal lines that English simply does not distinguish. A speaker of such a language can make precise statements about kinship that cannot be translated into English without lengthy circumlocution, and even then the translation may fail to capture the full import of the original, for the kinship concepts are embedded in a social structure that has no exact analogue in English-speaking societies. The third condition of the definition fails: the language is not translatable into English without loss of meaning. Yet it is indisputably a language. The definition has assumed a degree of commensurability between languages that does not in fact obtain, and it has mistaken an idealised property — perfect translatability — for a necessary condition of language itself.

definition: An utterance U is a lie if and only if (i) U is a declarative statement asserting that some proposition p is the case, (ii) the speaker does not hold p to be true, and (iii) the speaker utters U with the intention of causing the hearer to accept p as true.
conditions: U is a declarative statement asserting that some proposition p is the case
the speaker does not hold p to be true
the speaker utters U with the intention of causing the hearer to accept p as true
counterexample: Consider a person who writes in a diary, 'I was at the library all afternoon,' when in fact the person was elsewhere. The writer does not hold the proposition to be true and produces a declarative statement asserting it. Yet the diary entry is not addressed to any hearer, and the writer does not intend to cause anyone to accept the falsehood — the diary is private, and the writer intends that no one shall ever read it. The third condition fails. Nevertheless, the entry is a lie — a lie committed to paper, a lie told to oneself, perhaps, but a lie for all that.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that lying requires an intended audience, but one may lie in a diary, in a private letter never sent, or in a soliloquy. The essence of a lie is the deliberate production of a statement the speaker holds to be false, not the successful communication of falsehood to another person.
passage: One might propose that an utterance is a lie if and only if it is a declarative statement asserting that some proposition is the case, the speaker does not hold the proposition to be true, and the speaker utters it with the intention of causing the hearer to accept the proposition as true. The definition captures the standard case of lying admirably. When a child tells a parent, 'I did not eat the biscuits,' knowing full well that the child did eat them, and intending the parent to accept the denial — that is a lie, and it satisfies all three conditions. Yet one must consider the private diary. A person who has spent the afternoon at a friend's house writes in a private diary, 'I was at the library all afternoon.' The person knows this to be false. The statement is declarative and asserts a proposition the writer does not hold to be true. But the diary is private; the writer does not intend that anyone shall ever read it, and there is no hearer to be deceived. The third condition fails entirely. Yet there is something dishonest in the entry — the writer is lying, even if only to the page, or to the writer's future self, or to no one at all. The intuition that the diary entry is a lie, or at least that it partakes of the character of a lie, suggests that the definition has been too narrow in requiring an intended audience. What is essential to lying may be the deliberate production of a falsehood in the form of an assertion, rather than the successful transmission of that falsehood to another mind.

definition: An utterance U is a lie if and only if (i) the speaker produces U voluntarily and deliberately, (ii) U contains a factual claim that is objectively false, and (iii) the speaker is aware at the time of utterance that U is false.
conditions: the speaker produces U voluntarily and deliberately
U contains a factual claim that is objectively false
the speaker is aware at the time of utterance that U is false
counterexample: Consider a person who tells a friend, 'There are no good restaurants on that street,' firmly holding this to be false — the person knows of an excellent restaurant there but wishes to keep it secret. As it happens, the restaurant has just closed permanently, so the claim is in fact objectively true. The speaker produces the utterance voluntarily and deliberately, and the speaker is aware — or rather, holds — that the utterance is false. Yet the utterance is not objectively false; it is, by coincidence, true. The second condition fails. Nevertheless, the speaker has lied, for the speaker asserted something the speaker held to be false with the intention of misleading.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the statement be objectively false, but a person may lie by asserting something that happens, by coincidence, to be true. What makes an utterance a lie is the speaker's intention to assert what the speaker holds to be false, not the objective truth-value of the proposition asserted. A truthful statement, sincerely held to be false by the speaker, is a lie.
passage: Suppose one defines a lie as an utterance that the speaker produces voluntarily and deliberately, that contains a factual claim that is objectively false, and that the speaker is aware at the time of utterance to be false. The definition captures many straightforward cases: the child who says 'I have washed my hands' when the child has not, the witness who says 'I was not present' when the witness was. In such cases the utterance is voluntary, the claim is objectively false, and the speaker is aware of its falsity. Yet one must consider a more peculiar case. A person tells a friend, 'There are no good restaurants on that street.' The person holds this to be false — the person knows of an excellent restaurant on that very street but wishes, for selfish reasons, to keep the discovery private. The person thus asserts something the person holds to be false, with the intention of misleading. But, unbeknown to the speaker, the restaurant has just closed permanently. The claim is, as it happens, objectively true: there are now no good restaurants on that street. The second condition of the definition fails. Yet the speaker has lied. The speaker deliberately asserted what the speaker held to be false, with the intention of producing a false impression in the hearer's mind. The coincidental truth of the statement does not redeem the speaker's dishonesty. The definition has confused the objective falsity of the proposition with the essential feature of lying, which is the speaker's intention to assert what the speaker holds to be false.

definition: An utterance U is a lie if and only if (i) U is spoken aloud in the presence of another person, (ii) U concerns a matter of fact rather than a matter of opinion, and (iii) U is intended to produce a practical advantage for the speaker.
conditions: U is spoken aloud in the presence of another person
U concerns a matter of fact rather than a matter of opinion
U is intended to produce a practical advantage for the speaker
counterexample: Consider a person who writes a letter to an elderly relative, falsely claiming that a mutual acquaintance is in good health, when in fact the acquaintance is gravely ill. The writer does this not for any practical advantage but solely to spare the relative distress. The letter concerns a matter of fact — the health of the acquaintance — and the writer holds the claim to be false. Yet the lie is written, not spoken aloud, and it is not intended to produce any practical advantage for the writer; it is intended to protect the feelings of the reader. The first and third conditions fail, yet the letter contains a lie.
missing_condition: The definition restricts lies to spoken utterances motivated by self-interest, but one may lie in writing — in letters, in telegrams, in printed notices — and one may lie for altruistic reasons, to spare another person pain. The essence of a lie is not its medium or its motive but the deliberate assertion of what one holds to be false.
passage: One might hold that an utterance is a lie if and only if it is spoken aloud in the presence of another person, it concerns a matter of fact rather than a matter of opinion, and it is intended to produce a practical advantage for the speaker. The definition describes a common type of lie: the merchant who overstates the quality of his goods to a customer, the employee who fabricates an excuse to an employer. In such cases the lie is spoken, factual, and self-serving. But one must consider the written lie told from generous motives. An elderly woman writes to her sister, who is also elderly and in frail health, to report on the condition of a mutual friend. The friend is, in fact, gravely ill. The writer, wishing to spare her sister anxiety and distress, writes that the friend is well and in good spirits. The claim is factual and false, and the writer knows it to be false. Yet the lie is written, not spoken aloud; the sister is in another city, and the letter travels by post. And the lie is told not for the writer's practical advantage but for the reader's emotional comfort. The first and third conditions of the definition fail. The letter is nonetheless a lie — a compassionate one, perhaps, but a lie. The definition has identified lying with one of its common forms — the spoken, self-interested deception — and has mistaken the features of that form for necessary conditions of lying as such.
