definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) the speaker explicitly uses the words 'I promise' or an equivalent formulaic expression, (ii) the speaker addresses the promise to a specific, identifiable individual, and (iii) the promise concerns a future action that the speaker is able to perform.
conditions: the speaker explicitly uses the words 'I promise' or an equivalent formulaic expression
the speaker addresses the promise to a specific, identifiable individual
the promise concerns a future action that the speaker is able to perform
counterexample: Consider a person who says to a friend, 'I shall certainly be there at eight o'clock tomorrow evening.' The tone is earnest, the context makes clear that the friend is relying upon the speaker's presence, and both parties understand that a commitment has been made. Yet the speaker does not use the words 'I promise' or any equivalent formula. The first condition fails. Nevertheless, a promise has been made — the commitment is as binding, in the moral sense, as any promise sealed with the explicit formula.
missing_condition: The definition requires the use of an explicit performative formula, but promises may be made implicitly, through tone, context, and the evident understanding of both parties. What is essential to a promise is the undertaking of an obligation, not the recitation of a particular form of words.
passage: One might propose that an utterance constitutes a promise if and only if the speaker explicitly uses the words 'I promise' or an equivalent formulaic expression, the speaker addresses the promise to a specific individual, and the promise concerns a future action the speaker is able to perform. The definition reflects a natural thought: that promising is a speech act requiring a specific performative formula, much as a marriage requires the recitation of particular vows. And indeed, many promises do take this form — 'I promise to return the book by Friday,' 'I promise to attend the meeting.' Yet one must consider the implicit promise. A person says to a friend, 'I shall certainly be there at eight o'clock tomorrow evening.' The tone is earnest and the context makes clear that the friend is depending upon the speaker's presence. The friend arranges the evening on the assumption that the speaker will attend. Both parties understand that a commitment has been made — that the speaker is, in effect, bound to appear. Yet the speaker has not used the words 'I promise' or any equivalent formula. The first condition of the definition fails. Nevertheless, a promise has been made. The speaker has undertaken an obligation; the friend has a legitimate expectation; and if the speaker fails to appear, the friend has grounds for reproach. The definition has mistaken a common linguistic vehicle for promising — the explicit formula — for a necessary condition, when in truth a promise may be made by any form of words, or even by a nod or a gesture, provided the context makes the commitment clear.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) U is made voluntarily, without external coercion, (ii) U creates an obligation on the part of the speaker to perform the promised action, and (iii) the speaker sincerely intends, at the time of utterance, to perform the promised action.
conditions: U is made voluntarily, without external coercion
U creates an obligation on the part of the speaker to perform the promised action
the speaker sincerely intends, at the time of utterance, to perform the promised action
counterexample: Consider a swindler who says to a credulous investor, 'I promise to double your money within a year.' The swindler has no intention whatsoever of honouring this commitment; the swindler intends to abscond with the funds. The utterance is made voluntarily and creates, at least in the investor's understanding, an obligation. But the speaker does not sincerely intend to perform the promised action. The third condition fails. Yet the swindler has made a promise — a false and dishonest promise, to be sure, but a promise nonetheless, and the investor has been wronged precisely because a promise was made and broken.
missing_condition: The definition requires sincere intention, but insincere promises are still promises. A person who promises with no intention of keeping the promise has made a promise — a dishonourable one. The obligation created by a promise arises from the act of promising itself, not from the speaker's private state of mind at the time of utterance.
passage: Suppose one holds that an utterance constitutes a promise if and only if it is made voluntarily, it creates an obligation to perform the promised action, and the speaker sincerely intends at the time of utterance to perform the action. The third condition seems natural: one might think that a promise without the intention to keep it is not really a promise at all, but merely an empty form of words. Yet one must consider the swindler. A confidence trickster approaches a credulous investor and says, 'I promise to double your money within a year. You have my word.' The swindler speaks voluntarily and the utterance creates, in the investor's understanding, a binding obligation. The investor hands over a substantial sum on the strength of the promise. But the swindler has no intention of honouring the commitment; the swindler intends to disappear with the money. The third condition of the definition fails: the speaker does not sincerely intend to perform the promised action. Yet the swindler has made a promise. It is precisely because a promise was made — and subsequently broken — that the swindler's conduct is not merely imprudent but morally reprehensible. If no promise had been made, the investor would have no ground for the particular species of reproach that attaches to broken promises. The definition has confused a condition of an honourable promise with a condition of a promise as such. Promises may be sincere or insincere, and an insincere promise is still a promise — it is simply a dishonest one.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) U is a verbal commitment to perform a specific, well-defined action, (ii) U is made in a context of mutual trust between the speaker and the hearer, and (iii) the promised action is one that benefits the hearer.
conditions: U is a verbal commitment to perform a specific, well-defined action
U is made in a context of mutual trust between the speaker and the hearer
the promised action is one that benefits the hearer
counterexample: Consider a person struggling with a vice who promises a spouse, 'I promise I shall never touch drink again.' The promised action — abstaining from alcohol — benefits both the hearer and the speaker, but it benefits the speaker at least as much as the spouse. Moreover, one might equally imagine a promise that benefits only the promisor: 'I promise myself that I shall finish this book by the end of the month.' Here there is no hearer in the relevant sense, and the benefit accrues to the speaker alone. The third condition, which requires that the promised action benefit the hearer, fails in such cases, yet these are promises.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that promises must benefit the hearer, but many promises benefit the speaker, or both parties, or neither in any straightforward sense. One may promise to do something difficult, unpleasant, or self-regarding. What is essential to a promise is the undertaking of a commitment, not the direction of its benefit.
passage: One might hold that an utterance constitutes a promise if and only if it is a verbal commitment to perform a specific action, it is made in a context of mutual trust, and the promised action benefits the hearer. The third condition reflects a common intuition: that promises are made for the sake of others, that they are a means by which we bind ourselves to serve the interests of those to whom we give our word. And indeed, many promises do take this form — the promise to help a friend move house, the promise to lend a book, the promise to attend a celebration. Yet one must consider the self-directed promise. A person struggling with a vice says to no one in particular, or perhaps to the mirror, 'I promise I shall give up this habit.' There is no hearer, and the action, if performed, benefits the speaker alone. One might also consider the promise that benefits both parties equally: a husband promises his wife to stop drinking, an action that serves his health as much as her peace of mind. In neither case is the benefit exclusively the hearer's. The third condition of the definition fails or is at best only partially satisfied. The definition has been drawn from cases in which the promisor undertakes to do something for another person's sake, and it has mistaken this feature for a necessary condition. What is essential to a promise is the voluntary undertaking of a commitment, regardless of who benefits from the action promised.

definition: An object x is a vehicle if and only if (i) x is a machine powered by an engine or motor, (ii) x has wheels, and (iii) x travels along roads or paved surfaces.
conditions: x is a machine powered by an engine or motor
x has wheels
x travels along roads or paved surfaces
counterexample: Consider a canoe. A canoe is used to transport persons and goods across water — it is, in the ordinary sense, a vehicle. One speaks of a canoe as a conveyance, as a means of transport, as a vehicle for traversing rivers and lakes. Yet a canoe is not powered by an engine or motor; it is propelled by human effort with paddles. It has no wheels. And it does not travel along roads or paved surfaces; it travels upon water. All three conditions fail, yet the canoe is a vehicle.
missing_condition: The definition identifies vehicles with motorised, wheeled, road-going machines, yet the concept of a vehicle is far broader. Boats, sledges, aircraft, and human-powered conveyances are all vehicles. What unifies vehicles is their function — the transport of persons or goods from one place to another — not their mechanism or their medium of travel.
passage: One might propose that an object is a vehicle if and only if it is a machine powered by an engine or motor, it has wheels, and it travels along roads or paved surfaces. The definition describes the motor car with tolerable accuracy, and the motor car is perhaps the first object that comes to mind when one thinks of a vehicle. Lorries, omnibuses, and motorcycles also satisfy these conditions. One might suppose that engines, wheels, and roads are essential to the concept. Yet one must consider the canoe. A canoe is a light, narrow boat propelled by one or two persons wielding paddles. It is used to transport persons and goods across rivers, lakes, and coastal waters. It is a vehicle in the ordinary sense of the word: dictionaries list boats among the paradigm cases of vehicles, and common usage confirms this classification. Yet a canoe is not powered by an engine or motor; it is propelled by human muscular effort. It has no wheels whatsoever. And it travels not upon roads or paved surfaces but upon water. All three conditions of the definition fail. The same may be said of a sledge drawn by dogs across snow, or of a hot-air balloon drifting above the countryside. These are vehicles — means of transport — yet none is motorised, none has wheels, and none travels upon roads. The definition has generalised from one species of vehicle — the motor car and its close relatives — and has mistaken the features of that species for the essence of the genus.

definition: An object x is a vehicle if and only if (i) x is designed to carry passengers, (ii) x is operated by a human driver or pilot, and (iii) x can travel at speeds greater than a person can walk.
conditions: x is designed to carry passengers
x is operated by a human driver or pilot
x can travel at speeds greater than a person can walk
counterexample: Consider a wheelbarrow. A wheelbarrow is used to transport heavy loads — soil, bricks, garden refuse — from one place to another. It is, in a perfectly natural sense, a vehicle: a conveyance, a means of moving things. Yet it is not designed to carry passengers; it carries goods. It is operated by a human, but it cannot travel at speeds greater than a person can walk; it moves at precisely the speed of the person pushing it. The first and third conditions fail, yet the wheelbarrow is a vehicle of a humble but genuine kind.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that vehicles must carry passengers and must exceed walking speed, but many vehicles carry only goods, and some move no faster than a person on foot. What is essential to a vehicle is that it serves as a means of conveyance — of persons or goods — not that it carries passengers or achieves any particular speed.
passage: Suppose one defines a vehicle as an object designed to carry passengers, operated by a human driver or pilot, and capable of travelling at speeds greater than a person can walk. The definition captures a wide range of familiar vehicles: the motor car carries passengers, is driven by a human, and moves faster than walking pace. The same is true of the omnibus, the aeroplane, and the railway carriage. Yet one must consider the wheelbarrow. A wheelbarrow is a simple conveyance: a single wheel at the front, two handles at the rear, and a shallow trough between them for carrying loads. It is used to transport soil, bricks, stone, garden refuse, and any number of other heavy materials from one place to another. It is a vehicle in the most straightforward sense — a means of conveyance, a device for moving things. Yet it is not designed to carry passengers; no one rides in a wheelbarrow, save perhaps a child being given a game. It moves at precisely the speed of the person pushing it, which is to say at walking pace or below. The first and third conditions fail. The wheelbarrow reveals that the definition has been drawn from a class of large, fast, passenger-carrying vehicles and has generalised their features to the concept as a whole. What unifies all vehicles is their function as instruments of conveyance, not their speed, their scale, or the nature of their cargo.

definition: An object x is a vehicle if and only if (i) x is manufactured in a factory, (ii) x is registered with a governmental authority, and (iii) x bears a unique identifying number or plate.
conditions: x is manufactured in a factory
x is registered with a governmental authority
x bears a unique identifying number or plate
counterexample: Consider a raft constructed by a traveller from logs lashed together with rope, used to cross a wide river. The raft transports the traveller and the traveller's belongings from one bank to the other. It is a vehicle — a conveyance, a means of transport. Yet it is not manufactured in a factory; it is assembled by hand from materials found in the forest. It is not registered with any governmental authority. And it bears no identifying number or plate. All three conditions fail, yet the raft is a vehicle.
missing_condition: The definition conflates the legal and administrative apparatus surrounding modern vehicles with the concept of a vehicle itself. Registration and factory manufacture are features of vehicles within a particular regulatory framework, not necessary features of vehicles as such. A handmade raft, a dugout canoe, a horse-drawn cart built by a village carpenter — all are vehicles, yet none need be factory-made or registered.
passage: One might propose that an object is a vehicle if and only if it is manufactured in a factory, it is registered with a governmental authority, and it bears a unique identifying number or plate. The definition applies well enough to the motor car as it exists within a modern industrial society. Cars are factory-manufactured, they are registered with licensing authorities, and they bear number plates by which they may be identified. One might suppose that these features are part of what it is to be a vehicle. Yet one must consider the raft. A traveller arrives at a wide river and, finding no bridge or ferry, fells several trees, strips them of their branches, and lashes the logs together with rope. The resulting raft is used to cross the river, carrying the traveller and the traveller's possessions to the far bank. The raft is a vehicle: it conveys a person and goods from one place to another, and the word 'vehicle' applies to it as naturally as to any motor car. But the raft is not manufactured in a factory; it is assembled by hand from found materials. It is not registered with any authority; no government has been notified of its existence. And it bears no identifying number or plate. All three conditions of the definition fail. The definition has mistaken the legal and administrative features of vehicles in a modern regulatory state for necessary features of vehicles as such. The concept of a vehicle is older and broader than any system of registration, and it encompasses every contrivance by which persons or goods are conveyed from place to place.

definition: An object x is a tool if and only if (i) x is manufactured specifically for a single, well-defined task, (ii) x is held in the hand during use, and (iii) x is made of metal.
conditions: x is manufactured specifically for a single, well-defined task
x is held in the hand during use
x is made of metal
counterexample: Consider a wooden loom. A loom is a device used for weaving cloth — it is a tool of the weaver's trade. Yet it is not held in the hand; it is a large, freestanding apparatus at which the weaver sits. It is not made of metal but of wood. And it is not designed for a single, narrowly defined task but for the complex, multi-step process of interlacing threads to produce fabric. All three conditions fail, yet the loom is a tool.
missing_condition: The definition restricts tools to hand-held, single-purpose, metal objects, but tools may be large or small, simple or complex, and made of any material. A potter's wheel, a spinning jenny, a blacksmith's anvil — all are tools, yet none is hand-held and none is made exclusively of metal. What is essential to a tool is that it extends human capacity to perform work, not that it conforms to a particular size, material, or mode of use.
passage: One might propose that an object is a tool if and only if it is manufactured specifically for a single, well-defined task, it is held in the hand during use, and it is made of metal. The definition describes a familiar class of objects: the hammer, the screwdriver, the chisel, the spanner. Each is designed for a specific task, held in the hand, and made of metal or predominantly of metal. One might suppose that these features exhaust what it is to be a tool. Yet one must consider the loom. A loom is a large, freestanding apparatus composed of a frame, heddles, a shuttle, and various other parts, used by a weaver to interlace threads and produce cloth. It is indisputably a tool — the central tool of the weaver's craft, the instrument without which weaving cannot be performed. Yet it is not held in the hand; the weaver sits before it and operates its various parts from a seated position. It is not made of metal; traditional looms are constructed almost entirely of wood. And it is not designed for a single, narrowly defined task; weaving involves a complex sequence of operations — warping, shedding, picking, beating — each requiring the coordination of multiple moving parts. All three conditions of the definition fail. The definition has been drawn from the hand tools of the carpenter's workshop and has mistaken the features of that particular class of tools for the features of tools as such.

definition: An object x is a tool if and only if (i) x is an artefact — a thing made by human beings, (ii) x extends the user's physical strength or reach, and (iii) x is rigid and maintains a fixed shape during use.
conditions: x is an artefact — a thing made by human beings
x extends the user's physical strength or reach
x is rigid and maintains a fixed shape during use
counterexample: Consider a fishing net. A fishing net is woven from cord or twine, cast into water, and used to catch fish. It is indisputably a tool — one of the oldest and most important tools of human subsistence. It is an artefact, made by human beings. But it does not extend the user's physical strength or reach in the way that a lever or a hammer does; rather, it captures and contains. And it is not rigid; it is flexible, yielding, and changes shape continually as it moves through the water, billowing and folding according to the currents. The second and third conditions fail, yet the fishing net is a tool.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that tools must amplify force and be rigid, yet many essential tools — nets, ropes, bags, bellows — are flexible and serve functions other than the extension of physical strength. What unifies tools is not rigidity or force-amplification but the purposeful augmentation of human capacity to accomplish tasks that would otherwise be difficult or impossible.
passage: Suppose one holds that an object is a tool if and only if it is an artefact made by human beings, it extends the user's physical strength or reach, and it is rigid and maintains a fixed shape during use. The definition applies well to many paradigmatic tools. A hammer extends the user's striking force and is rigid; a crowbar extends reach and leverage and is rigid; a pair of tongs extends the user's grasp into hot or dangerous spaces and maintains a fixed shape. One might suppose that force-extension and rigidity are essential features of all tools. Yet one must consider the fishing net. A net is woven from cord or twine, shaped into a mesh, and cast into water to catch fish. It is among the oldest tools known to human beings, and it remains indispensable in many communities. It is an artefact, certainly. But it does not extend the user's physical strength or reach in the manner of a lever or a hammer; its function is to capture and contain, not to amplify force. And it is not rigid. A net is soft, flexible, and continuously changing shape — billowing in the current, folding upon itself when drawn from the water, conforming to whatever it captures. The second and third conditions fail. The definition has generalised from rigid, force-amplifying tools and has mistaken the features of that class for necessary conditions of tools as such. The concept of a tool is broader than any account centred on rigidity and force can accommodate.

definition: An object x is a tool if and only if (i) x is used to modify or shape a material — to cut, carve, bore, or join, (ii) x leaves a visible mark or alteration upon the material it acts upon, and (iii) x is used by a skilled craftsperson who has received formal training.
conditions: x is used to modify or shape a material — to cut, carve, bore, or join
x leaves a visible mark or alteration upon the material it acts upon
x is used by a skilled craftsperson who has received formal training
counterexample: Consider a spirit level. A spirit level is a tool used by builders and carpenters to determine whether a surface is horizontal. It consists of a sealed glass tube containing liquid and an air bubble; when the bubble rests between two lines, the surface is level. The spirit level does not modify or shape any material; it measures. It leaves no visible mark or alteration upon the surface it is placed against. And it may be used by anyone — a homeowner hanging a shelf, a child in a school workshop — without formal training. All three conditions fail, yet the spirit level is indisputably a tool.
missing_condition: The definition restricts tools to material-shaping implements used by trained craftspersons, but many tools — measuring instruments, cleaning devices, diagnostic apparatus — do not modify materials at all, and many tools are used competently by untrained persons. What is essential to a tool is that it assists in the performance of a task, not that it shapes material or requires expertise.
passage: One might define a tool as an object used to modify or shape a material, that leaves a visible mark or alteration upon the material it acts upon, and that is used by a skilled craftsperson who has received formal training. The definition captures the chisel, the saw, the plane, and the drill admirably: each shapes material, each leaves a visible alteration, and each is associated with the skilled trades. One might suppose that material modification and skilled use are essential to the concept. Yet one must consider the spirit level. A spirit level is a device consisting of a sealed glass tube containing liquid and an air bubble, set within a straight-edged body of wood or metal. When placed upon a surface, the position of the bubble indicates whether the surface is horizontal. The spirit level is a tool — builders and carpenters regard it as an indispensable part of their kit, and it is classified and sold as a tool in every hardware shop. But it does not modify or shape any material; it measures a property of a surface without altering it in the slightest. It leaves no mark, no scratch, no alteration upon the material it touches. And it may be used competently by any person, trained or untrained — one need only place it upon a surface and observe the bubble. All three conditions of the definition fail. The definition has been drawn from the shaping tools of the workshop and has overlooked the entire class of measuring, testing, and diagnostic tools, which assist in work without modifying materials.

definition: An object x is a tool if and only if (i) x is portable — it can be carried by a single person, (ii) x is simple in construction, consisting of one or very few parts, and (iii) x is operated by direct human effort without any intermediate mechanism.
conditions: x is portable — it can be carried by a single person
x is simple in construction, consisting of one or very few parts
x is operated by direct human effort without any intermediate mechanism
counterexample: Consider a printing press. A printing press is a complex machine composed of many parts — a frame, a platen, a screw or lever mechanism, a bed for holding type, rollers for applying ink. It is heavy and cannot be carried by a single person. It is operated through an intermediate mechanism — the screw or lever — that transmits and amplifies the user's effort. Yet a printing press is a tool: it is the tool by which printers produce books, pamphlets, and broadsides. All three conditions fail, yet the printing press is a tool of the printer's trade.
missing_condition: The definition identifies tools with simple, portable, directly operated implements, but many tools are large, complex, and mechanically mediated. A lathe, a forge, a kiln, a printing press — all are tools, yet none is portable, none is simple, and all involve intermediate mechanisms. The concept of a tool encompasses the full range of instruments by which human beings accomplish work, from the simplest hand implement to the most elaborate machine.
passage: Suppose one proposes that an object is a tool if and only if it is portable, it is simple in construction, and it is operated by direct human effort without any intermediate mechanism. The definition applies to many familiar tools: a hammer is portable, simple, and operated directly; a knife is likewise portable, simple, and directly operated; a screwdriver can be carried in a pocket and consists of a single shaft and handle. One might suppose that portability, simplicity, and directness of operation are essential to the concept of a tool. Yet one must consider the printing press. A printing press, of the kind used from the fifteenth century onward to produce books, is a large, heavy, stationary machine. It cannot be carried by a single person; it requires a dedicated room and a solid floor to bear its weight. It is not simple in construction; it consists of numerous parts — a frame, a bed, a platen, a screw mechanism, rollers, and various adjustable fittings. And it is not operated by direct human effort alone; the operator turns a screw or pulls a lever, and the mechanism amplifies and redirects the effort to press the paper against the inked type. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet the printing press is a tool — the most consequential tool of the printer's trade, and one of the most transformative tools in human history. The definition has generalised from hand tools and has mistaken their small scale, simplicity, and directness for necessary features of tools as such, when the concept of a tool is broad enough to encompass any instrument by which human work is accomplished.

definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A has a clearly defined objective that all participants seek to achieve, (ii) A involves an element of chance determined by a randomising device such as dice or cards, and (iii) A concludes when one participant achieves the objective before the others.
conditions: A has a clearly defined objective that all participants seek to achieve
A involves an element of chance determined by a randomising device such as dice or cards
A concludes when one participant achieves the objective before the others
counterexample: Consider the game of chess. It has a clearly defined objective — to checkmate the opponent's king — and it concludes when one player achieves this objective. Yet chess involves no element of chance whatsoever. There is no randomising device; no dice are thrown, no cards are drawn. The outcome depends entirely upon the skill and judgement of the players. The second condition fails, yet chess is perhaps the most celebrated game in the history of civilisation.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that chance is essential to games, but many of the most highly regarded games — chess, draughts, go — are games of pure skill involving no randomness at all. The concept of a game must be broad enough to encompass activities whose outcomes are determined entirely by the choices of the participants.
passage: One might propose that an activity is a game if and only if it has a clearly defined objective, it involves an element of chance determined by a randomising device, and it concludes when one participant achieves the objective. The definition appears to describe many familiar games. In backgammon, the objective is to bear off all one's pieces; the element of chance is supplied by the dice; and the game concludes when one player achieves this end. In card games such as whist or rummy, chance governs the deal, and the game ends when a player or partnership reaches the required score. One might suppose that chance is part of what makes a game a game — that the uncertainty introduced by a randomising device is what gives a game its characteristic excitement. Yet one must consider chess. This activity is indisputably a game; it is, indeed, the game par excellence in the estimation of many. It has a clearly defined objective: the checkmate of the opponent's king. And it concludes when one player achieves this end, or when a draw is agreed, or when time expires. But chess involves no element of chance. The initial position is fixed, not randomised. No dice are cast, no cards are shuffled. Every outcome is determined entirely by the decisions of the two players. The second condition of the definition fails. The same is true of draughts, of go, of noughts and crosses, and of countless other games of pure skill. The definition has taken a feature of one important class of games — those involving chance — and elevated it to a necessary condition, when in truth games may be games of skill, of chance, or of some mixture of the two.

definition: An object O is a chair if and only if (i) O has four legs, (ii) O has a flat horizontal seat, and (iii) O has a vertical backrest.
conditions: O has four legs
O has a flat horizontal seat
O has a vertical backrest
counterexample: Consider a three-legged milking stool. It has three legs, not four, and it lacks any backrest whatsoever. Yet it is a piece of furniture upon which one sits, and many persons would call it a chair — or, at the very least, its exclusion from the category of chairs reveals that the definition is too narrow. More strikingly, consider a modern cantilever chair, which has no legs at all but is supported by a single curved steel frame. It has a seat and a backrest, and it is indisputably a chair. The first condition fails.
missing_condition: The definition requires four legs, but chairs may have three legs, one leg, no legs at all (as in a hanging or cantilever chair), or a pedestal base. The number of legs is a contingent feature of one style of chair, not an essential property of chairs as such.
passage: One might propose that an object is a chair if and only if it has four legs, a flat horizontal seat, and a vertical backrest. The definition fits the standard kitchen chair admirably: four legs supporting a flat wooden seat with a vertical back against which one leans. One might suppose that these three features capture the essence of the chair. Yet one must consider the modern cantilever chair, of the sort designed by Mart Stam and Ludwig Mies van der Rohe in the late nineteen-twenties. This chair has no legs in any ordinary sense. It consists of a single continuous tube of steel, bent into a shape that supports a seat and a backrest without any vertical members touching the ground at four distinct points. The sitter is supported by the springlike resilience of the cantilevered frame. The first condition of the definition fails: the chair has no four legs, and indeed has no legs at all, in the sense that the definition requires. Yet it is a chair — one sits upon it, one leans against its back, and it serves every function that a chair is expected to serve. The same difficulty arises for the pedestal chair, which rests upon a single central column, and for the three-legged stool, which has been a common article of seating furniture for centuries. The definition has taken one particular construction — the four-legged chair — as the paradigm and mistaken its structural features for necessary conditions of chairhood, when in truth a chair may be supported by any number of legs, by a pedestal, by a cantilever, or by suspension from above.

definition: An object O is a work of art if and only if (i) O is a physical artefact produced by human hands, (ii) O is intended by its maker to represent or depict some aspect of the visible world, and (iii) O displays technical skill of a kind requiring prolonged training.
conditions: O is a physical artefact produced by human hands
O is intended by its maker to represent or depict some aspect of the visible world
O displays technical skill of a kind requiring prolonged training
counterexample: Consider a purely abstract painting by Piet Mondrian — a canvas divided into rectangles of primary colours separated by black lines. It is a physical artefact produced by human hands, and it displays considerable technical skill in its precise geometry and colour balance. But it is not intended to represent or depict any aspect of the visible world; Mondrian was explicit that his work sought to express universal harmonies through pure abstraction, not to depict visible reality. The second condition fails, yet Mondrian's paintings are works of art by any reasonable reckoning.
missing_condition: The definition restricts art to representational work, but abstract art, conceptual art, and purely formal compositions are works of art that make no attempt to depict the visible world. What makes something a work of art cannot be reduced to its representational function.
passage: One might propose that an object is a work of art if and only if it is a physical artefact produced by human hands, it is intended by its maker to represent or depict some aspect of the visible world, and it displays technical skill requiring prolonged training. The definition captures much of what one finds in the great tradition of Western painting and sculpture. A portrait by Rembrandt is a physical object, it represents a human face, and it displays formidable technical mastery. A landscape by Constable is produced by hand, it depicts a scene in the English countryside, and the skill it embodies is the fruit of years of patient study. One might suppose that representation and skill are the twin pillars of art. Yet one must consider the paintings of Piet Mondrian — those austere canvases in which the visual field is divided into rectangles of red, yellow, blue, white, and grey, separated by heavy black lines. These paintings are physical artefacts produced by Mondrian's own hands, and they display a technical precision that, whilst different in kind from that of a Rembrandt, is the product of disciplined practice and aesthetic judgement. But they do not represent or depict any aspect of the visible world. Mondrian was emphatic upon this point: his paintings were intended to express universal principles of harmony and balance through purely abstract means, not to picture any scene, object, or person. The second condition of the definition fails. The same difficulty arises for the work of Malevich, of Kandinsky in his later period, and of the entire tradition of geometric abstraction. The definition has generalised from representational art and mistaken one function of art — the depiction of visible reality — for a necessary condition of art as such.

definition: A system of communication S is a language if and only if (i) S employs vocalised sounds produced by the human vocal apparatus, (ii) S is acquired in childhood through exposure to adult speakers, and (iii) S changes gradually over time through the accumulation of small modifications.
conditions: S employs vocalised sounds produced by the human vocal apparatus
S is acquired in childhood through exposure to adult speakers
S changes gradually over time through the accumulation of small modifications
counterexample: Consider British Sign Language, used by the deaf community in Britain. It is a language in every linguistically significant sense: it has a grammar, a lexicon, a syntax, and the capacity to express any proposition that spoken English can express. It is acquired in childhood through exposure to adult signers. It changes over time. Yet it does not employ vocalised sounds produced by the vocal apparatus; it employs manual gestures, facial expressions, and spatial configurations. The first condition fails, yet British Sign Language is indisputably a language.
missing_condition: The definition restricts language to spoken communication, but sign languages demonstrate that the medium of expression — whether vocal, gestural, or written — is not essential to what makes a system of communication a language. Language is characterised by its structural properties — grammar, productivity, displacement — not by the physical channel through which it is transmitted.
passage: One might propose that a system of communication is a language if and only if it employs vocalised sounds produced by the human vocal apparatus, it is acquired in childhood through exposure to adult speakers, and it changes gradually over time. The definition has a natural appeal, for it describes the ordinary experience of language as most people encounter it. A child growing up in England hears English spoken around her, acquires the language through immersion, and speaks it using her vocal cords, tongue, and lips. The English she speaks is not the English of Chaucer; the language has changed over centuries through the slow accumulation of modifications in pronunciation, grammar, and vocabulary. The definition seems to capture the essential features of this process. Yet one must consider British Sign Language. This system of communication is used by the deaf community throughout Britain and has been in continuous use, evolving and diversifying, for centuries. It possesses a grammar of remarkable sophistication, with rules governing word order, agreement, and aspect. It has a large lexicon, and it can express abstract concepts, hypothetical situations, and emotional nuances with the same facility as any spoken language. It is acquired in childhood by deaf children exposed to adult signers, much as spoken language is acquired by hearing children. And it changes over time, as any living language does. But British Sign Language does not employ vocalised sounds. Its medium is not the voice but the hands, the face, and the space before the signer's body. The first condition fails entirely. The definition has taken the vocal medium of spoken languages and treated it as a necessary condition of language itself, when in truth language may be realised in any modality — vocal, manual, or written — that permits the expression of structured, productive, and semantically rich communication.

definition: A statement S made by a person A constitutes a lie if and only if (i) A believes that S is false, (ii) A intends S to deceive the person to whom it is addressed, and (iii) S concerns a matter of empirical fact rather than opinion, evaluation, or prediction.
conditions: A believes that S is false
A intends S to deceive the person to whom it is addressed
S concerns a matter of empirical fact rather than opinion, evaluation, or prediction
counterexample: Consider a case in which a literary critic, asked for her opinion of a new novel, states that she considers it a work of genius, though she in fact believes it to be mediocre. She believes her statement to be false, and she intends to deceive her interlocutor — perhaps the author himself. Yet her statement concerns a matter of evaluative opinion, not empirical fact. The third condition fails, yet the critic has lied: she has deliberately misrepresented her judgement with the intention of deceiving.
missing_condition: The definition restricts lies to statements of empirical fact, but one can lie about one's opinions, evaluations, intentions, and predictions just as readily as about matters of fact. What is essential to lying is the deliberate misrepresentation of what one believes, regardless of whether the subject matter is factual or evaluative.
passage: One might propose that a statement constitutes a lie if and only if the speaker believes it to be false, the speaker intends it to deceive the hearer, and the statement concerns a matter of empirical fact. The third condition reflects the thought that lies must be about the sort of thing that can be straightforwardly true or false — that one can lie about whether it rained yesterday but not about whether a painting is beautiful. The definition thus excludes insincere expressions of opinion, taste, or prediction from the category of lies. Yet one must consider the case of a literary critic who, dining with the author of a new novel, declares that she considers the book a work of considerable merit, when in truth she regards it as a mediocre performance. She believes her statement to be false — it does not reflect her actual assessment. She intends to deceive the author, who relies upon her judgement. Yet her statement is a matter of evaluative opinion, not empirical fact. The third condition fails. But the critic has lied; she has deliberately and knowingly misrepresented her beliefs with the intention of producing a false impression in the mind of her hearer. The fact that her statement concerns aesthetic evaluation rather than observable fact does not diminish its character as a lie. One may lie about one's opinions, one's intentions, one's feelings, and one's predictions with the same facility and the same moral gravity as one lies about empirical matters. The definition has drawn an arbitrary boundary around one subject matter and treated it as the only domain in which lying is possible.

definition: An utterance U by a person A constitutes a promise if and only if (i) U is made in the presence of the person to whom the obligation is owed, (ii) U concerns a specific future action that A undertakes to perform, and (iii) U is accompanied by a verbal formula of explicit commitment such as 'I promise' or 'I give my word.'
conditions: U is made in the presence of the person to whom the obligation is owed
U concerns a specific future action that A undertakes to perform
U is accompanied by a verbal formula of explicit commitment such as 'I promise' or 'I give my word'
counterexample: Consider a letter written by a father to his daughter, posted from a distant city, in which he writes: 'I shall be home for your birthday.' The father is not in his daughter's presence; the promise is made in writing, across a distance. No explicit formula of the type 'I promise' appears in the letter. Yet the father has made a promise. He has undertaken a commitment to perform a specific future action, and both he and his daughter understand the statement as creating an obligation. The first and third conditions fail, yet the promise is genuine.
missing_condition: The definition requires physical co-presence and an explicit verbal formula, but promises may be made in writing, at a distance, and without any conventional promissory phrase. What constitutes a promise is the undertaking of a commitment, understood as such by both parties, regardless of the medium or the formula employed.
passage: One might propose that an utterance constitutes a promise if and only if it is made in the presence of the person to whom the obligation is owed, it concerns a specific future action that the speaker undertakes to perform, and it is accompanied by an explicit verbal formula of commitment such as 'I promise' or 'I give my word.' The definition captures a familiar and important class of promises — the face-to-face, formally declared commitment. One thinks of a marriage vow, spoken before the altar, or a solemn pledge given in person to a friend. The explicit formula and the physical presence of the promisee seem to lend the promise its weight and seriousness. Yet one must consider the case of a father who, stationed in a distant city, writes a letter to his young daughter. In the letter he writes simply: 'I shall be home for your birthday.' He does not say 'I promise' or 'I give my word'; he merely states what he intends to do. He is not in his daughter's presence; the letter will travel for days before she reads it. Yet both he and she understand his statement as a promise. If he fails to return, she will feel not merely disappointed but wronged — betrayed, even. He has taken on an obligation by his written words, and both parties recognise this. The first condition of the definition fails, for the promise is not made in the promisee's presence. The third condition fails, for no explicit formula accompanies the undertaking. Yet the promise is genuine. The definition has elevated the trappings of formal promising — co-presence and verbal ceremony — to the status of necessary conditions, when in truth a promise may be made quietly, informally, and across great distances, and its binding force resides not in any formula but in the mutual understanding that a commitment has been undertaken.

definition: An object O is a vehicle if and only if (i) O is powered by an engine or motor, (ii) O is designed to travel upon roads or prepared surfaces, and (iii) O is enclosed, providing its occupants with shelter from the weather.
conditions: O is powered by an engine or motor
O is designed to travel upon roads or prepared surfaces
O is enclosed, providing its occupants with shelter from the weather
counterexample: Consider a bicycle. It is not powered by an engine or motor; its motive force is supplied entirely by the rider's legs. It is not enclosed; the rider is fully exposed to the elements. Yet a bicycle is a vehicle. It conveys persons from one place to another, it travels upon roads, and it is classified as a vehicle by traffic legislation throughout the world. The first and third conditions fail, yet the bicycle is indisputably a vehicle.
missing_condition: The definition restricts vehicles to motorised, enclosed conveyances, but many vehicles — bicycles, rowboats, horse-drawn carts, sledges — are propelled by human or animal effort and offer no enclosure. What makes something a vehicle is that it serves as a means of conveyance, not that it possesses an engine or a roof.
passage: One might propose that an object is a vehicle if and only if it is powered by an engine or motor, it is designed to travel upon roads or prepared surfaces, and it is enclosed, providing its occupants with shelter from the weather. The definition fits the motor car tolerably well: a car has an engine, it is designed for road travel, and its cabin shields its passengers from rain and wind. A motor coach, a lorry, and a railway carriage all satisfy the conditions. One might suppose that these features jointly capture what it is to be a vehicle. Yet one must consider the ordinary bicycle. A bicycle conveys its rider from one place to another. It travels upon roads, paths, and lanes. It has been a principal mode of transport in Europe and Asia for well over a century. It is classified as a vehicle by traffic legislation in Britain and in virtually every other jurisdiction. Yet a bicycle is not powered by an engine or motor; its propulsion depends entirely upon the muscular effort of the rider, transmitted to the rear wheel through pedals and a chain. And a bicycle is not enclosed; the rider sits in the open air, exposed to rain, wind, and sun. The first and third conditions fail. The same difficulty arises for the horse-drawn cart, which was the standard vehicle of overland transport for millennia, and for the rowing boat, which is a vehicle upon water. The definition has taken the motor car as its model and generalised from its features, mistaking the engine and the enclosed cabin for essential properties of vehicles as such, when in truth a vehicle is any contrivance by which persons or goods are conveyed from one place to another.

definition: An object O is a tool if and only if (i) O is manufactured from metal, (ii) O is designed to be held in one hand, and (iii) O is used to shape, cut, or fasten materials.
conditions: O is manufactured from metal
O is designed to be held in one hand
O is used to shape, cut, or fasten materials
counterexample: Consider a wooden mallet used by a sculptor to strike a chisel. It is manufactured from wood, not metal. It is used not to shape materials directly but to deliver force to another tool that does the shaping. Yet a wooden mallet is unquestionably a tool. More strikingly, consider a two-handed crosscut saw. It is used to cut timber, but it is designed to be operated by two persons, each gripping one end. The second condition fails. Yet the crosscut saw is a tool in every ordinary sense of the word.
missing_condition: The definition restricts tools to metal, hand-held, material-shaping implements, but tools may be made of any material — wood, stone, bone, plastic — and may be operated with two hands, with the feet, or by multiple persons. What makes an object a tool is its instrumental use for a practical purpose, not the material of its construction or the manner of its operation.
passage: One might propose that an object is a tool if and only if it is manufactured from metal, it is designed to be held in one hand, and it is used to shape, cut, or fasten materials. The definition describes many common tools with reasonable accuracy. A hammer is made of metal, is held in one hand, and is used to drive nails. A screwdriver is metal, is held in one hand, and is used to fasten screws. A chisel is metal, is hand-held, and is used to shape wood or stone. One might suppose that these three features are essential to toolhood. Yet one must consider the wooden mallet. Sculptors and carpenters have used wooden mallets for centuries to deliver blows to chisels and gouges. A mallet is manufactured from wood — typically beech or lignum vitae — not from metal. The first condition fails. One must also consider the two-handed crosscut saw, a tool of venerable antiquity used for felling trees and cutting timber. This saw is designed to be operated by two persons, each gripping a handle at opposite ends and drawing the blade back and forth through the wood. The second condition fails. And one might consider a plumb line — a length of string with a weight attached — which is used not to shape, cut, or fasten materials but to determine whether a surface is vertical. It is a tool of the builder's trade, yet it is not made of metal (save for the small weight), it is not held in one hand in the manner of a hammer, and it does not shape or cut anything. All three conditions fail. The definition has taken the metalworker's hand tools as its paradigm and mistaken their particular features for the essential properties of tools as such.

definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is governed by a set of explicit rules that define permissible and impermissible moves, (ii) A involves competition between two or more participants who seek to win, and (iii) the outcome of A is determined by the skill or strategy of the participants rather than by chance alone.
conditions: A is governed by a set of explicit rules that define permissible and impermissible moves
A involves competition between two or more participants who seek to win
the outcome of A is determined by the skill or strategy of the participants rather than by chance alone
counterexample: Consider a child's game of ring-a-ring-o'-roses. The children join hands, dance in a circle, sing the familiar rhyme, and at the appointed words all fall down together. This is, by any ordinary usage of the term, a game. Yet it involves no competition; the children do not seek to win, for there is nothing to win. There are no explicit rules defining permissible and impermissible moves in the manner of chess or cricket; there is a customary pattern of action, but no rule-book and no umpire. And the outcome — if there can be said to be an outcome — is not determined by skill or strategy but is the same on every occasion. All three conditions fail, yet the activity is a game.
missing_condition: The definition imposes the structure of competitive, rule-governed, skill-based contests upon all games, but many games are cooperative, informal, or governed by custom rather than explicit rules. What is wanted is an account broad enough to encompass the full variety of activities that are called games.
passage: One might hold that an activity is a game if and only if it is governed by a set of explicit rules defining permissible and impermissible moves, it involves competition between two or more participants who seek to win, and the outcome is determined by skill or strategy rather than by chance alone. The definition captures a good many paradigmatic cases. Chess is governed by elaborate rules, involves competition between two players, and its outcome depends upon the strategic acumen of the contestants. Cricket is played according to codified laws, the competing sides seek victory, and skill in batting and bowling determines the result. One might suppose that these features are essential to the concept of a game. Yet one must consider a child's game of ring-a-ring-o'-roses. A group of children join hands, form a circle, dance round whilst singing the familiar rhyme, and at the words 'all fall down' tumble to the ground in a heap of laughter. This activity is called a game by children and by their parents, and it would be strained in the extreme to deny it the title. But it satisfies none of the conditions of the definition. There are no explicit rules defining permissible and impermissible moves; the children follow a customary pattern of action, but there is no rule-book, no umpire, and no penalty for deviation. There is no competition; the children do not seek to win, for there is nothing to win and no way of distinguishing a winner from a loser. The participants act in concert, not in opposition. And the outcome — if one can speak of an outcome at all — is not determined by skill or strategy; it is the same on every occasion, regardless of the abilities of the participants. The definition has abstracted the features of one class of games — competitive, rule-bound, skill-based contests — and treated them as necessary conditions of all games, overlooking the vast territory of children's games, party games, and cooperative games that share little with chess or cricket beyond the common name.

definition: A statement S made by a speaker P is a lie if and only if (i) P believes that S is false, (ii) P intends to deceive the hearer into believing that S is true, and (iii) there is no prior understanding between P and the hearer that S may be false — that is, the context is one in which truthfulness is expected.
conditions: P believes that S is false
P intends to deceive the hearer into believing that S is true
there is no prior understanding between P and the hearer that S may be false
counterexample: Consider a person who makes a false statement on a tax return. He states that his income for the year was five hundred pounds when he knows it to be one thousand. He believes the statement is false, and there is no understanding between himself and the revenue authorities that permits falsehood. But he does not intend to deceive anyone into believing that his income was five hundred pounds; he is quite indifferent to what the tax inspector believes. His purpose is not to produce a false belief in the mind of the inspector but simply to reduce the amount of tax he must pay. The second condition fails, yet the statement is, by any ordinary reckoning, a lie.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the speaker intend to produce a false belief in the hearer, but a person may lie with the sole intention of gaining a practical advantage, without caring whether the hearer is actually deceived. What is wanted is an account that does not reduce lying to the intention to deceive.
passage: Suppose one holds that a statement is a lie if and only if the speaker believes it to be false, the speaker intends to deceive the hearer into believing it to be true, and there is no prior understanding between the parties that the statement may be false. The definition captures many familiar instances. When a person tells his companion that he has never visited Paris, knowing full well that he spent a fortnight there last summer, with the intention that his companion should believe this falsehood, and in a context where truth is expected, he lies. The three conditions are jointly satisfied, and the judgement that a lie has been told accords with common intuition. Yet one must consider the case of a person who makes a false declaration upon a tax return. He states that his income for the previous year was five hundred pounds when he knows it to have been one thousand. He believes the statement to be false, and there is no prior understanding that permits falsehood on official documents. But his purpose in making the false statement is not to produce a false belief in the mind of the revenue inspector. He is quite indifferent to what the inspector believes about his income; for all he cares, the inspector may suspect the figure is false. His sole aim is to reduce the amount of tax he is required to pay — a practical advantage that follows from the statement's being accepted for administrative purposes, regardless of whether anyone genuinely believes it. The second condition of the definition fails. The speaker does not intend to deceive; he intends to gain a practical benefit. Yet the statement is a lie by any ordinary standard, and a particularly grave one at that. The definition has confused the most common motive for lying — the desire to produce a false belief — with a necessary condition of lying, when in truth a person may lie for purely practical reasons without caring in the least whether the hearer is genuinely deceived.

definition: An utterance U made by a speaker P constitutes a promise if and only if (i) U expresses P's intention to perform some future action A, (ii) P sincerely intends to perform A at the time of uttering U, and (iii) the hearer H desires or has an interest in P's performing A.
conditions: U expresses P's intention to perform some future action A
P sincerely intends to perform A at the time of uttering U
the hearer H desires or has an interest in P's performing A
counterexample: Consider a person who promises his dying father that he will visit the family grave every year. He has no sincere intention of doing so; he makes the promise only to ease the old man's final hours, fully expecting that he will never fulfil it. He utters the words 'I promise,' the father takes comfort in the assurance, and the promise is made. Yet the second condition fails: the speaker does not sincerely intend to perform the promised action. Nevertheless, a promise has been made, and if the speaker fails to visit the grave, he has broken his promise.
missing_condition: The definition requires sincere intention at the time of promising, but a promise may be genuinely binding even when the speaker does not sincerely intend to keep it. What is wanted is an account that locates the force of a promise in the act of undertaking an obligation, not in the speaker's inner state of intention.
passage: One might propose that an utterance constitutes a promise if and only if it expresses the speaker's intention to perform some future action, the speaker sincerely intends to perform that action at the time of utterance, and the hearer desires or has an interest in the action's being performed. The definition has a certain plausibility. When a man says to his friend, 'I promise to return your book by Friday,' he expresses an intention, he sincerely means to return the book, and the friend has an interest in its return. The three conditions appear to capture what is essential to promising. Yet one must consider the case of a man who promises his dying father that he will visit the family grave every year. The old man is upon his deathbed, and the son, moved by the pathos of the scene, utters the words, 'I promise.' But the son has no sincere intention of visiting the grave. He is an unsentimental man who regards such visits as pointless, and he makes the promise solely to bring comfort to his father in his final hours. He knows, even as the words leave his lips, that he will not fulfil the undertaking. The father, reassured, dies in peace. Now, has a promise been made? It seems clear that it has. The son promised, and his father received the promise; the social and moral act of promising was performed, and a corresponding obligation was incurred. If the son never visits the grave, we should say not that he never promised, but that he broke his promise. The second condition of the definition fails — the speaker did not sincerely intend to perform the action — yet the promise is genuine. The definition has confused the sincerity conditions that attend a well-made promise with the conditions that are constitutive of promising itself. A promise is made by the public undertaking of an obligation, and that undertaking may be effected regardless of the speaker's private intentions.

definition: An object O is a vehicle if and only if (i) O is a manufactured artefact designed for the purpose of transporting persons or goods, (ii) O is capable of self-propelled motion — that is, O contains within itself a source of motive power, and (iii) O operates upon a prepared surface such as a road, a railway, or a waterway.
conditions: O is a manufactured artefact designed for the purpose of transporting persons or goods
O is capable of self-propelled motion — it contains a source of motive power
O operates upon a prepared surface such as a road, a railway, or a waterway
counterexample: Consider a bicycle. A bicycle is, in ordinary usage, a vehicle. One speaks of bicycle traffic, of parking one's vehicle, of the highway code applying to all vehicles including bicycles. It is a manufactured artefact designed for the purpose of transporting a person from one place to another. But a bicycle is not self-propelled; it contains no source of motive power within itself. The power comes entirely from the rider's muscles, transmitted through the pedals and chain to the wheels. The second condition fails, yet the bicycle is a vehicle by any common understanding of the term.
missing_condition: The definition requires self-propulsion, but vehicles may be propelled by the muscular effort of the user, by animal power, or by gravity. What is wanted is an account that encompasses all means by which a vehicle may be set in motion, not only mechanical engines.
passage: One might hold that an object is a vehicle if and only if it is a manufactured artefact designed for transporting persons or goods, it is capable of self-propelled motion by virtue of containing a source of motive power, and it operates upon a prepared surface such as a road, a railway, or a waterway. The definition fits many paradigmatic cases. A motor car is a manufactured artefact, is self-propelled by an internal combustion engine, and operates upon roads. A railway locomotive is manufactured, self-propelled by steam or electricity, and operates upon rails. A steam ship is manufactured, self-propelled, and operates upon waterways. One might suppose that these features jointly characterise the concept of a vehicle. Yet one must consider the humble bicycle. The bicycle is, by universal acknowledgement, a vehicle. The highway code treats it as a vehicle; municipalities regulate its use alongside motor traffic; the cyclist speaks of riding his vehicle. It is a manufactured artefact, designed expressly for the purpose of conveying a person from one place to another, and it operates upon roads. But the bicycle is not self-propelled. It contains no engine, no motor, no source of motive power within itself. The power that drives it comes entirely from the rider's own muscles, transmitted through the pedals and the chain to the rear wheel. Without the rider's continual physical effort, the bicycle stands inert. The second condition of the definition fails. The definition has taken a feature of motorised vehicles — the presence of an internal source of motive power — and elevated it to a necessary condition of being a vehicle, when in truth many objects that are indisputably vehicles — bicycles, handcarts, horse-drawn carriages, canoes — derive their motion from the muscular effort of a human being or an animal rather than from any mechanical engine contained within the vehicle itself.

definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A has a defined beginning and end — that is, there is a point at which play commences and a point at which a winner is declared, (ii) A involves the voluntary acceptance of obstacles that make achieving the objective more difficult, and (iii) A requires at least two participants.
conditions: A has a defined beginning and end with a winner declared
A involves the voluntary acceptance of obstacles
A requires at least two participants
counterexample: Consider a person working a jigsaw puzzle alone in her sitting room on a Sunday afternoon. She tips the pieces onto the table, turns them right side up, and sets about assembling the picture. This is, by ordinary usage, a game — or at the very least a variety of play that falls squarely within the extension of the term. She voluntarily accepts an obstacle: the pieces are jumbled, and she must find how they fit together. But no winner is declared at the end; completion of the picture is not a victory over an opponent but a private satisfaction. And no second participant is required or present; she plays alone. The first and third conditions fail, yet the activity is a game in any common sense of the word.
missing_condition: The definition requires a declared winner and multiple participants, but solitary pastimes and puzzles that have no competitive element are called games in ordinary language. What is wanted is an account broad enough to encompass solitary, non-competitive activities that are pursued for their own sake under self-imposed constraints.
passage: One might hold that an activity is a game if and only if it has a defined beginning and end with a winner declared, it involves the voluntary acceptance of obstacles, and it requires at least two participants. The definition captures many familiar examples. A game of chess begins when the players sit down at the board and ends when one of them is checkmated; both players voluntarily accept the constraints of the rules, which make the capture of the opponent's king more difficult than it would be if one could simply reach across and remove it; and two participants are required. A football match, a hand of bridge, a round of draughts — in each case, the three conditions are satisfied. Yet one must consider the solitary jigsaw puzzle. A woman sits alone in her room on a Sunday afternoon with a box of five hundred pieces tipped upon the table before her. She turns them right side up, sorts them by colour and edge, and begins the slow process of fitting them together. This activity is, by the ordinary use of the term, a game — or at any rate a form of play that no competent speaker of English would hesitate to describe as such. She voluntarily accepts an obstacle: the pieces are jumbled, and the task of assembling them is made difficult by their number, their similarity of shape, and the subtlety of the colour gradations in the picture. The second condition is satisfied. But no winner is declared at the end. When the last piece clicks into place, there is no victory, no defeated opponent, no score. There is only the quiet satisfaction of completion. And no second participant is required; the whole point of the exercise is that it is done alone, in solitude, as a private amusement. The first and third conditions fail. The definition has taken the features of competitive, multi-player contests and treated them as essential to the concept of a game, overlooking the large territory of solitary puzzles, patience games, and single-player pastimes that are games in every ordinary sense.

definition: An object O is a chair if and only if (i) O has a seat — a horizontal surface upon which a person may sit, (ii) O has a back — a vertical surface against which a seated person may rest the upper body, and (iii) O has four legs that support it above the ground.
conditions: O has a seat — a horizontal surface for sitting
O has a back — a vertical surface for resting the upper body
O has four legs supporting it above the ground
counterexample: Consider a beanbag chair. It is a large, soft sack filled with polystyrene beads, and when a person sits upon it, it moulds itself to the contours of the body, forming a comfortable seat. It has no legs — not four, not three, not one. It rests directly upon the floor, a shapeless mass until someone sits in it, at which point it assumes a form that supports the sitter in a semi-reclined position. It has no distinct back; the fabric rises around the sitter on all sides, providing support not through a vertical surface but through the pressure of the filling. Yet it is called a chair — a beanbag chair — and it is used as a chair, purchased as a chair, and placed in sitting rooms and dormitories for the express purpose of providing a seat.
missing_condition: The definition requires four legs and a distinct back, but many objects commonly called chairs — beanbag chairs, pedestal chairs, kneeling chairs — lack these features. What is wanted is an account that captures the functional role of a chair without imposing a particular structural form.
passage: One might propose that an object is a chair if and only if it has a seat — a horizontal surface upon which a person may sit — a back against which the sitter may rest the upper body, and four legs that support the whole above the ground. The definition fits many paradigmatic chairs. The wooden kitchen chair has a flat seat, a slatted back, and four turned legs. The dining chair, the office chair with its swivelling base, the classroom chair — in most of these, one finds the three features specified. Yet one must consider the beanbag chair. This object is a large sack, typically of canvas or vinyl, filled with small polystyrene beads. When no one sits upon it, it lies upon the floor in an amorphous heap, possessing neither a determinate seat, nor a back, nor anything that could be called a leg. When a person lowers himself onto it, the beads shift and redistribute under his weight, and the sack moulds itself to the contours of his body, forming a kind of cradle that supports him in a semi-reclined position. The beanbag chair has no legs. It rests directly upon the floor, and its support comes not from rigid members elevating it above the ground but from the pressure of the filling against the floor beneath and the body above. It has no distinct back; the fabric surrounds the sitter on all sides, and the support for the upper body comes from the general enveloping pressure of the beads rather than from a vertical surface behind the sitter. Yet it is called a chair without the slightest hesitation. It is manufactured as a chair, sold as a chair, advertised as a chair, and placed in rooms for the express purpose of providing seating. Children and students sit in beanbag chairs for hours. The definition, by requiring four legs and a distinct back, excludes an object that falls squarely within the ordinary extension of the concept. It has mistaken the structural features of one familiar design — the wooden four-legged chair — for the essential features of chairs as such, when what makes something a chair is not its structure but its function: that it serves to support a person in a seated position.

definition: An object O is a work of art if and only if (i) O is produced by a human being with the intention of creating something of aesthetic value, (ii) O requires skill — that is, the production of O demands a degree of technical proficiency that not everyone possesses, and (iii) O is a physical artefact — a painting, a sculpture, a musical score, or a similar tangible object.
conditions: O is produced by a human being with the intention of creating aesthetic value
O requires skill — a degree of technical proficiency
O is a physical artefact
counterexample: Consider an improvised dance performed in the street by a man who, upon hearing music from a nearby window, begins to move with extraordinary grace and expressiveness. The dance is not a physical artefact; it is a sequence of movements that exist only in the moment of their performance and leave no tangible residue. When the music stops and the man walks away, the dance is gone — there is no painting, no sculpture, no score, no object of any kind that remains. Yet the dance may be a work of art of the highest order. The movements may display invention, expressiveness, and formal coherence; those who witnessed the performance may speak of it for years as one of the most beautiful things they have ever seen. The third condition fails — the dance is not a physical artefact — yet it is art.
missing_condition: The definition restricts art to physical artefacts, but many works of art — dances, musical performances, oral recitations, theatrical improvisations — are ephemeral events rather than tangible objects. What is wanted is an account that encompasses the performing and temporal arts as well as the visual and plastic arts.
passage: One might hold that an object is a work of art if and only if it is produced by a human being with the intention of creating something of aesthetic value, it requires skill, and it is a physical artefact — a painting, a sculpture, a musical score, or a similar tangible object. The definition has a long tradition behind it. The masterpieces of the visual arts — the paintings of the great masters, the sculptures of the classical world, the illuminated manuscripts of the mediaeval monasteries — are physical objects, produced with consummate skill and with the manifest intention of creating beauty. One might suppose that art is, at bottom, the making of beautiful things — the skilful production of physical objects intended to delight the senses and elevate the mind. Yet one must consider an improvised dance. A man walking through a city street hears music drifting from a nearby window — a melody of unusual beauty and rhythmic vitality. He stops, and he begins to move. His movements are extraordinary: fluid, expressive, precise, inventive. He leaps, he turns, he traces patterns in the air with his arms and in the dust with his feet. Passersby gather to watch; they stand transfixed. The dance lasts for several minutes, and when the music fades and the man straightens up and walks away, the small crowd disperses with the sense that they have witnessed something remarkable. But the dance is not a physical artefact. It is a sequence of movements existing only in the present moment of their performance. No painting hangs on a wall; no sculpture occupies a plinth; no score preserves the movements in notation. When the dancer walks away, nothing tangible remains. Yet those who saw the performance may remember it for years as one of the most powerful aesthetic experiences of their lives. The third condition of the definition fails. The definition has taken the features of the visual and plastic arts — their tangibility, their permanence, their status as physical objects — and treated them as necessary conditions of art as such, overlooking the vast domain of the performing arts, in which the work of art is an event rather than an object, and exists only in and through its performance.

definition: A person P tells a lie if and only if (i) P makes a statement S to another person, (ii) P believes that S is false, and (iii) P intends to deceive the person to whom S is addressed — that is, P intends that the listener should come to hold a false belief as a result of hearing S.
conditions: P makes a statement S to another person
P believes that S is false
P intends to deceive the listener
counterexample: Consider an actor upon the stage who delivers the line 'I am the Duke of Venice.' The actor makes a statement to others — the audience. The actor believes the statement is false; he knows perfectly well that he is not the Duke of Venice but a paid performer reciting a script. Yet no one would say that the actor has told a lie. He does not intend to deceive the audience; the audience knows that the performance is a fiction, and the conventions of the theatre ensure that no one is misled. But consider now a subtler case: a person at a social gathering who, when asked how she is, replies 'Very well, thank you,' though she is in fact in considerable distress. She makes a statement she believes to be false, and she does intend that her listener should believe it — she does not wish to burden the enquirer with her troubles. Yet one might hesitate to call this a lie. The conventions of social intercourse seem to create a category of insincere speech that is neither truthful nor a lie in the full sense.
missing_condition: The definition does not account for the role of social convention in distinguishing lies from other forms of insincere speech. What is wanted is an account that recognises that the context and conventions within which a statement is made bear upon whether an insincere assertion constitutes a lie.
