passage: One might propose that a person tells a lie if and only if the person makes a statement to another, believes the statement to be false, and intends to deceive the listener — that is, intends that the listener should come to hold a false belief as a result of hearing the statement. The definition captures many clear cases. If a man, when asked by his wife where he has been, says 'At the office' when he has in fact been at the races, he makes a statement, believes it to be false, and intends his wife to believe something that is not the case. He lies. Yet one must consider the everyday case of a person at a social gathering who, when greeted with the customary enquiry 'How are you?', replies 'Very well, thank you,' though she is in truth suffering from a painful ailment and is far from well. She makes a statement to another person. She believes the statement to be false; she knows she is not well. And she intends the listener to believe it — she does not wish the enquirer to know of her distress, and she would prefer that he take her at her word and move on to other topics. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet one hesitates to say that she has told a lie. The exchange is governed by social convention: the question 'How are you?' is, in most circumstances, not a genuine enquiry into the state of one's health but a form of greeting, and the expected reply is a conventional formula rather than a candid report. Both parties understand this. The enquirer does not expect a truthful answer; the respondent does not suppose that she is deceiving anyone; the whole transaction is conducted within a framework of understood conventions that strip the words of their literal force. The definition, by attending only to the speaker's belief and intention and not to the conventions within which the statement is made, classifies as a lie what is in truth a perfectly ordinary piece of social intercourse, no more dishonest than the actor's 'I am the Duke of Venice' is a lie upon the stage. What is wanted is an account that recognises the role of context and convention in determining whether an insincere statement amounts to a lie.

definition: A person P makes a promise if and only if (i) P utters words to the effect that P will perform some future action A, (ii) P intends, at the time of utterance, to perform A, and (iii) P's utterance is made freely — that is, P is not coerced or compelled by external force to utter the words.
conditions: P utters words to the effect that P will perform a future action
P intends at the time of utterance to perform the action
P's utterance is made freely without coercion
counterexample: Consider a man who, in the course of a casual conversation, says to his friend, 'I shall pop round tomorrow afternoon.' He utters words to the effect that he will perform a future action; he intends, at the time of speaking, to visit his friend the following day; and his utterance is entirely free and uncoerced. Yet it appears doubtful whether he has made a promise. He has expressed an intention, certainly, and perhaps even created a mild expectation, but neither he nor his friend would ordinarily regard the remark as a promise. If he fails to appear, his friend might be disappointed but would not accuse him of breaking a promise. The three conditions are satisfied, yet a promise has not been made; something further — a solemnity of undertaking, a communication that one is placing oneself under an obligation — seems to be required.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish a mere expression of intention from a promise. What is wanted is a condition requiring that the speaker communicate the undertaking of an obligation — that the speaker present the utterance as creating a binding commitment, not merely as reporting a plan.
passage: One might hold that a person makes a promise if and only if the person utters words to the effect that he will perform some future action, intends at the time of utterance to perform the action, and makes the utterance freely without coercion. The definition captures certain clear cases. When a man says to his creditor, 'I promise to repay you by Friday,' freely and with the genuine intention of repaying, he has made a promise. The three conditions are satisfied, and no one would doubt that a promise has been given. Yet one must consider a more commonplace exchange. A man is speaking with a friend on the telephone. As the conversation draws to a close, he says, casually, 'I shall pop round tomorrow afternoon.' He utters words to the effect that he will perform a future action — visiting his friend the following day. He intends, at the moment of speaking, to do so; he is not dissembling. And his utterance is perfectly free; no one has compelled him to say it. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet it seems doubtful that he has made a promise. He has expressed an intention, certainly. He has given his friend reason to expect a visit. But neither party would ordinarily regard the remark as carrying the weight of a promise. If the man fails to appear — because he is detained at work, or because the weather is foul, or simply because he has changed his mind — his friend may be mildly disappointed but will not accuse him of having broken a promise. The remark lacks the solemnity, the sense of binding commitment, that distinguishes a promise from a mere statement of intention. A promise, it appears, requires something more than the expression of an intention to act: it requires that the speaker present the utterance as creating an obligation, as placing himself under a commitment from which he cannot withdraw at will without moral cost. The definition, by attending only to the content of the utterance, the speaker's intention, and the absence of coercion, fails to capture this essential element of obligatory force.

definition: An object O is a vehicle if and only if (i) O is designed to transport persons or goods from one place to another, (ii) O is powered by an engine or motor, and (iii) O travels upon a prepared surface — a road, a track, or a rail.
conditions: O is designed to transport persons or goods
O is powered by an engine or motor
O travels upon a prepared surface
counterexample: Consider a sailing boat. It is designed to transport persons — and often goods — from one place to another. It is, by ordinary usage, a vehicle; one speaks of 'a vehicle for crossing the channel' without any sense of strain. Yet a sailing boat is not powered by an engine or motor; it is propelled by the wind acting upon its sails. And it does not travel upon a prepared surface; it moves upon the open sea, which is not a road, a track, or a rail, but a natural expanse of water. The second and third conditions fail, yet the sailing boat is a vehicle.
missing_condition: The definition restricts vehicles to engine-powered conveyances travelling upon prepared surfaces, but vehicles may be propelled by wind, animal power, or human effort, and may travel upon water, through the air, or across open terrain. What is wanted is an account centred upon the function of transportation rather than upon a particular mode of propulsion or medium of travel.
passage: One might propose that an object is a vehicle if and only if it is designed to transport persons or goods from one place to another, it is powered by an engine or motor, and it travels upon a prepared surface — a road, a track, or a rail. The definition captures a large class of familiar vehicles. A motorcar is designed for transport, is powered by an engine, and travels upon roads. A railway locomotive is designed for transport, is powered by a motor, and travels upon rails. A lorry, a bus, a tram — each satisfies the three conditions without difficulty. Yet one must consider the sailing boat. A boat designed for ocean passage is intended to transport persons and goods from one port to another; this much is undeniable. It is a conveyance in the fullest sense — people board it at one place and disembark at another, and the purpose of the vessel is to effect this transit. It is called a vehicle without hesitation in many contexts: maritime law speaks of vessels as vehicles of commerce; ordinary language admits the sailing boat as a vehicle of transportation. But the sailing boat is not powered by an engine or motor. It is propelled by the wind — a natural force acting upon sheets of canvas. The skill of the sailor consists in the management of the sails and the rudder, not in the operation of a motor. And the sailing boat does not travel upon a prepared surface. The open sea is not a road, a track, or a rail; it is a vast, unprepared expanse of water, as wild and unimproved as any feature of the natural world. The second and third conditions of the definition fail. The definition has modelled the concept of a vehicle upon the motorcar and the railway train, and has incorporated features specific to those conveyances — mechanical propulsion and prepared surfaces — as necessary conditions, when in truth the concept of a vehicle is far broader, encompassing any conveyance designed to transport persons or goods, regardless of its source of power or the medium through which it moves.

definition: An object O is a tool if and only if (i) O is a physical artefact — that is, a material object fashioned by human hands, (ii) O is designed to extend the capabilities of the human body in the performance of a specific task, and (iii) O is manipulated directly by the hand of the user during the performance of that task.
conditions: O is a physical artefact fashioned by human hands
O is designed to extend the capabilities of the human body for a specific task
O is manipulated directly by the hand of the user
counterexample: Consider an irrigation canal dug by a farmer to bring water from a river to his fields. The canal is a physical artefact, fashioned by human effort. It is designed to extend the farmer's capabilities — without it, he cannot water his distant fields. It serves a specific task: the conveyance of water. Yet the canal is not manipulated directly by the hand of the user during the performance of the task; once dug, it operates passively, conveying water by gravity without any manual intervention. The third condition fails. Yet the canal is, in an important sense, a tool — an instrument devised by human ingenuity to accomplish a task that unaided human effort could not achieve.
missing_condition: The definition requires direct manual manipulation, but many tools — canals, traps, dams, windmills — perform their function without being held or guided by the hand. What is wanted is an account that includes passive instruments and fixed installations as well as hand-held implements.
passage: One might hold that an object is a tool if and only if it is a physical artefact, it is designed to extend the capabilities of the human body in the performance of a specific task, and it is manipulated directly by the hand of the user during the performance of that task. The definition captures many paradigmatic tools. A hammer is a physical artefact, it extends the hand's ability to drive nails, and it is held and swung by the user. A saw, a chisel, a screwdriver, a pair of pliers — each is fashioned by human hands, extends human capability, and is directly manipulated by the user. One might suppose that direct manipulation by the hand is what distinguishes a tool from a mere machine or structure. Yet one must consider an irrigation canal dug by a farmer across his land. The canal is a physical artefact in every sense; the farmer has dug it with considerable labour, shaping the earth to form a channel of precise gradient and cross-section. It is designed to extend his capabilities: without the canal, he cannot bring water from the river to his distant fields, and his crops would fail. It serves a specific task — the conveyance of water — as surely as a hammer serves the driving of nails. But the canal is not manipulated directly by the hand of the user during the performance of its task. Once dug, it conveys water by gravity, silently and continuously, without any manual intervention from the farmer. He does not hold the canal, guide it, or direct its operation; he simply opens the sluice and allows the water to flow. The third condition fails. Yet the canal is an instrument — a tool, in the broader sense of the word — devised by human ingenuity to accomplish a practical task. The definition has modelled the concept of a tool upon the hand-held implement and has required manual manipulation as a necessary condition, when in truth tools may be fixed installations, passive devices, or self-operating mechanisms that extend human capability without being directly manipulated by the hand.

definition: A system of communication S is a language if and only if (i) S employs a finite set of discrete symbols that can be combined according to rules, (ii) S permits the expression of an unlimited number of novel meanings through the combination of these symbols, and (iii) S is transmitted from one generation to the next by deliberate instruction.
conditions: S employs a finite set of discrete symbols combinable by rules
S permits the expression of an unlimited number of novel meanings
S is transmitted from one generation to the next by deliberate instruction
counterexample: Consider the language spoken by a small, isolated community that has no tradition of formal instruction. Children in this community learn to speak not by being deliberately taught but by immersion — by hearing the speech of their parents and elders and gradually coming to reproduce it, in the way that they learn to walk or to recognise faces. There is no schooling, no correction of grammar, no explicit teaching of vocabulary. The language employs discrete symbols combined by rules, and it permits the expression of novel meanings. It is, by any reckoning, a language. Yet it is not transmitted by deliberate instruction; it is acquired by exposure and imitation, as naturally and as unconsciously as breathing. The third condition fails.
missing_condition: The definition requires deliberate instruction, but most natural languages are acquired by children through immersion and unconscious pattern-extraction rather than through formal teaching. What is wanted is an account of language transmission that encompasses both formal instruction and informal acquisition.
passage: One might propose that a system of communication is a language if and only if it employs a finite set of discrete symbols combinable by rules, it permits the expression of an unlimited number of novel meanings, and it is transmitted from one generation to the next by deliberate instruction. The definition captures something important about language: its combinatorial structure, its creative capacity, and its cultural continuity. English, for instance, employs a finite stock of words that can be combined according to the rules of English grammar to produce an unlimited number of sentences, many of which have never been uttered before; and English is, in some measure, taught — children are corrected, vocabulary is explained, and schools devote considerable time to the formal study of the language. Yet one must consider the language of a small, isolated community that possesses no tradition of formal education whatever. In this community there are no schools, no grammars, no dictionaries, and no deliberate instruction in language. Children learn to speak as they learn to walk — by immersion in the practices of their elders. A child hears the speech of those around her from the day of her birth; she babbles, experiments, gradually produces recognisable words, and by the age of four or five speaks the language fluently, with a command of its grammar that would astonish a professional linguist. No one has taught her. No one has corrected her grammar, drilled her in vocabulary, or explained the rules of combination. She has extracted the patterns of the language from the speech she has heard, as unconsciously and as naturally as she has learned to distinguish the faces of her family. The language is transmitted from one generation to the next, but not by deliberate instruction; it is acquired by exposure, imitation, and the unconscious internalisation of regularities. The third condition of the definition fails. The definition has confused one means by which languages are sometimes transmitted — deliberate instruction, which is characteristic of literate societies — with a necessary condition of language transmission as such, when in truth the primary mode of language acquisition, across all human communities, is the unconscious absorption of patterns from ambient speech.

definition: An object O is a work of art if and only if (i) O is produced by an individual working alone, (ii) O is an original creation — not a copy or reproduction of an existing work, and (iii) O is intended by its creator to express a personal vision or emotional state.
conditions: O is produced by an individual working alone
O is an original creation, not a copy or reproduction
O is intended to express a personal vision or emotional state
counterexample: Consider a mediaeval cathedral — Chartres, let us say. It is, by wide consensus, one of the supreme works of art in the Western tradition. Its soaring vaults, its sculptured portals, its stained glass windows constitute an artistic achievement of the first order. Yet it was not produced by an individual working alone; it was the collective labour of hundreds of masons, sculptors, glaziers, and carpenters, working over many decades under the direction of successive master builders. The first condition fails. Moreover, the builders did not set out to express a personal vision or emotional state; they set out to build a house of worship according to established liturgical requirements and architectural conventions. The third condition fails as well. Yet the cathedral is art.
missing_condition: The definition requires individual authorship and the expression of personal vision, but many of the greatest works of art are collaborative productions created to serve communal purposes. What is wanted is an account that admits collective as well as individual creation and functional as well as expressive aims.
passage: One might hold that an object is a work of art if and only if it is produced by an individual working alone, it is an original creation rather than a copy, and it is intended by its creator to express a personal vision or emotional state. The definition reflects a conception of the artist as a solitary genius, pouring a unique inner vision into material form. The Romantic painter, alone in his studio, wrestling with canvas and pigment to give expression to the turmoil of his soul — this is the paradigm that the definition has in view, and it captures a good many works that we rightly call art. Yet one must consider the mediaeval cathedral. Chartres cathedral, built over the course of the twelfth and thirteenth centuries, is one of the supreme achievements of Western art. Its western facade, with its three great portals covered in sculptured figures of extraordinary refinement, its rose windows casting pools of coloured light upon the stone floor, its ribbed vaults soaring above the nave — these constitute an artistic accomplishment that few individual creators have rivalled. But the cathedral was not produced by an individual working alone. It was the collaborative labour of hundreds of craftsmen: masons who cut and laid the stone, sculptors who carved the figures, glaziers who designed and fitted the windows, carpenters who built the scaffolding and the roof. These men worked over many decades, and the master builders who directed the work succeeded one another as the generations passed. No single individual can claim authorship of the cathedral. The first condition of the definition fails. And the builders did not intend to express a personal vision or emotional state. They intended to build a house of worship — to create a space that would serve the liturgical needs of the community and manifest the glory of what they reverenced. The motivating purpose was communal and functional, not personal and expressive. The third condition fails. The definition has abstracted features of the Romantic conception of the artist — individual genius, personal expression — and treated them as necessary conditions of art, when in truth many of the greatest works of art in human history are the products of collective labour, directed towards communal purposes, and expressive of shared convictions rather than personal emotions.

definition: An object O is a vehicle if and only if (i) O transports persons or goods by moving itself from one location to another, (ii) O has an operator who controls its direction and speed during transit, and (iii) O is manufactured — that is, it is a product of industrial or artisanal fabrication.
conditions: O transports persons or goods by moving from one location to another
O has an operator who controls its direction and speed
O is manufactured
counterexample: Consider a raft hastily assembled from driftwood and rope by a man stranded on a riverbank. The raft transports him across the river; it moves from one location to another; and he steers it, after a fashion, with a makeshift paddle. Yet the raft is not manufactured in any ordinary sense. It is not the product of industrial or artisanal fabrication; it was assembled on the spot from materials found at hand, with no pre-existing design, no workshop, and no tools beyond the man's own hands and a length of rope. The third condition fails, yet the raft is a vehicle — it conveys a person from one place to another, which is the very essence of what a vehicle does.
missing_condition: The definition requires that a vehicle be manufactured, but improvised conveyances assembled from found materials may serve the function of a vehicle perfectly well. What is wanted is an account that focuses upon the function of conveyance rather than upon the mode of production.
passage: One might propose that an object is a vehicle if and only if it transports persons or goods by moving from one location to another, it has an operator who controls its direction and speed, and it is manufactured — a product of industrial or artisanal fabrication. The definition fits many paradigmatic vehicles. A motorcar is manufactured in a factory, transports persons from place to place, and has a driver who controls its direction and speed. A railway carriage is manufactured, transports passengers, and is controlled by an engine driver. A bicycle, a horse-drawn cart, a steam locomotive — in each case, the three conditions are satisfied. Yet one must consider the case of a man stranded on a riverbank who needs to cross to the other side. He gathers driftwood from the bank, binds the logs together with rope salvaged from a ruined fence, and fashions a crude but serviceable raft. He pushes off from the bank, paddles with a flat piece of board, and crosses the river safely. The raft transports him from one location to another; he controls its direction and speed, however imperfectly, with his paddle. But the raft is not manufactured. It was not produced in a factory or a workshop; it was assembled on the spot from found materials, with no pre-existing plan and no fabrication beyond the binding of logs. No artisan crafted it; no factory produced it; the man simply lashed together what the river had provided. The third condition of the definition fails. Yet the raft is a vehicle in every functional sense of the term: it conveys a person from one place to another across a body of water. The definition has confused a common feature of the vehicles we encounter in daily life — their manufactured origin — with a necessary condition of vehiclehood, when in truth an improvised conveyance assembled from found materials serves the function of a vehicle as completely as any product of the most elaborate factory.

definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is governed by a set of explicit rules that the participants voluntarily accept, (ii) A has a determinate outcome — there is a way of winning or losing, and (iii) A is undertaken for the purpose of amusement or recreation rather than material gain.
conditions: A is governed by explicit rules voluntarily accepted
A has a determinate outcome — winning or losing
A is undertaken for amusement rather than material gain
counterexample: Consider a child engaged in an elaborate game of make-believe, pretending to be a pirate sailing to a distant island. There are no explicit rules — the child invents the scenario freely, and the play evolves without fixed constraints. There is no way of winning or losing — the play has no determinate outcome but simply unfolds until the child tires of it. Yet it is plainly undertaken for amusement, and we should not hesitate to call it a game. The first two conditions are violated, yet the activity is a paradigmatic instance of play.
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in requiring explicit rules and a determinate outcome. It fails to accommodate open-ended, improvisational forms of play that are nevertheless recognised as games by ordinary speakers.
passage: One might propose that an activity is a game if and only if it is governed by explicit rules that the participants voluntarily accept, it has a determinate outcome in the form of winning or losing, and it is undertaken for amusement rather than material gain. This definition captures a great deal: chess, football, card games, and board games all satisfy these conditions comfortably. The requirement of voluntary rule-acceptance distinguishes games from compulsory tasks; the requirement of a determinate outcome gives the activity its characteristic structure of striving toward a goal; and the requirement of recreational purpose separates games from work. Yet one might observe that the definition is too narrow to accommodate the full range of activities that we call games. Consider a child absorbed in an elaborate game of make-believe. The child pretends to be a pirate, invents a scenario involving a sea voyage and hidden treasure, and plays the part with great enthusiasm and invention. There are no explicit rules — the scenario is freely improvised, and what happens next is determined by the child's fancy rather than by any prior stipulation. There is no way of winning or losing — the play simply continues until the child chooses to stop. Yet it is plainly recreational, and any ordinary speaker of the language would call it a game. The definition has captured the features of competitive, rule-governed games but has failed to accommodate the open-ended, improvisational character of imaginative play.

definition: An object O is a chair if and only if (i) O has a seat — a horizontal surface upon which a person may sit, (ii) O has a back — a vertical or inclined surface against which the sitter may lean, and (iii) O is designed or intended to support a single seated person.
conditions: O has a seat — a horizontal surface for sitting
O has a back — a vertical surface for leaning
O is designed to support a single seated person
counterexample: Consider a beanbag. A beanbag has no rigid horizontal seat and no identifiable back in the structural sense — it is an amorphous sack that conforms to the sitter's body. Yet it is designed and intended to support a single seated person, and it is commonly referred to as a beanbag chair. One sits in it comfortably, one leans back against it, and furniture shops sell it alongside conventional chairs. The first two conditions are not met in any straightforward sense, yet the object is called a chair and functions as one.
missing_condition: The definition is too closely modelled on rigid, structured seating and fails to accommodate objects that serve the function of a chair — supporting a single seated person in comfort — without possessing the specific structural features of a seat and a back.
passage: It seems a simple enough matter to define a chair: an object with a seat, a back, and a design intended to support a single seated person. This definition works well for a large class of familiar objects — the wooden kitchen chair, the upholstered armchair, the office swivel chair. Each possesses a clearly identifiable seat, a clearly identifiable back, and an evident purpose of supporting a single person in a seated position. Yet one might observe that the definition, for all its apparent simplicity, is drawn too narrowly. Consider the beanbag — or, as it is often called, the beanbag chair. It has no rigid horizontal seat: the surface upon which one sits is a yielding, amorphous sack that takes its shape from the weight of the person who sits upon it. It has no identifiable back in the structural sense: there is no vertical or inclined surface against which one leans, though the sack conforms around the sitter in such a way as to provide support from several directions. Yet it is designed to support a single person in a seated position, it is sold alongside conventional chairs in furniture shops, and it is called a chair without hesitation by ordinary speakers. The definition has extracted two specific structural features — seat and back — from the paradigm cases and elevated them to necessary conditions, when what is common to all chairs may be something more functional and less structural: the capacity to support a single person in a comfortable seated posture.

definition: An object O is a work of art if and only if (i) O is produced by a human being through deliberate effort, (ii) O is intended by its maker to be an object of aesthetic contemplation, and (iii) O possesses formal qualities — such as harmony, balance, or expressiveness — that reward such contemplation.
conditions: O is produced by a human being through deliberate effort
O is intended to be an object of aesthetic contemplation
O possesses formal qualities that reward contemplation
counterexample: Consider Marcel Duchamp's submission of a commercially manufactured urinal to an art exhibition under the title 'Fountain.' The urinal was not produced by Duchamp through deliberate artistic effort — it was a mass-produced industrial object. Whether it possesses formal qualities that reward aesthetic contemplation is debatable. Yet it has been accepted, discussed, and exhibited as a work of art for over a century. It satisfies at most the second condition — that its presenter intended it as an object of contemplation — while failing the first and arguably the third, yet it is widely regarded as one of the most significant works of twentieth-century art.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the role of institutional and contextual factors — the gallery, the art world, the act of selection and presentation — in conferring the status of art upon an object, independently of the object's origin or formal properties.
passage: One might define a work of art as an object produced by a human being through deliberate effort, intended by its maker to be an object of aesthetic contemplation, and possessing formal qualities that reward such contemplation. This definition has a long and distinguished pedigree: it captures the traditional notion of art as the skilful production of objects that are beautiful or expressive. The requirement of human production excludes natural objects — a sunset is not a work of art, however beautiful. The requirement of intention ensures that accidental products of human activity are excluded. And the requirement of formal qualities ensures that the object merits the attention it is intended to receive. Yet the twentieth century has produced cases that reveal the insufficiency of these conditions. Consider the episode in which a commercially manufactured urinal was submitted to an art exhibition and subsequently became one of the most discussed objects in the history of art. The urinal was not produced by the person who submitted it; it was a mass-produced industrial object, identical to thousands of others. Whether it possesses formal qualities that reward aesthetic contemplation is, at best, controversial. Yet it has been exhibited, analysed, and debated as a work of art for over a century. The definition, it appears, has failed to capture the role of institutional context — the gallery, the art world, the act of presentation — in constituting an object as art.

definition: A system S is a language if and only if (i) S consists of a set of symbols or signs that are conventionally associated with meanings, (ii) S is governed by syntactic rules that determine which combinations of symbols are well-formed, and (iii) S is used by a community of speakers for the purpose of communication.
conditions: S consists of symbols conventionally associated with meanings
S is governed by syntactic rules determining well-formedness
S is used by a community of speakers for communication
counterexample: Consider the formal language of propositional logic. It consists of symbols — variables, connectives, parentheses — that are associated with definite meanings. It is governed by strict syntactic rules that determine which strings are well-formed formulae. Yet it is not used by a community of speakers for communication in the ordinary sense — it is a tool of analysis, employed by logicians for the purpose of representing the structure of arguments, not for the everyday exchange of information, requests, and feelings that characterises natural language. The three conditions are satisfied, yet propositional logic is not a language in the sense that English or French is.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish natural languages from formal systems by omitting any requirement of expressive richness — the capacity to convey an open-ended range of thoughts, emotions, and purposes — which distinguishes genuine languages from symbolic calculi.
passage: It is natural to suppose that a language is a system of symbols conventionally associated with meanings, governed by syntactic rules, and used by a community for communication. These conditions capture the essential features of the paradigmatic cases: English, French, Mandarin, and other natural languages all consist of symbols with conventional meanings, are governed by syntactic rules, and serve as instruments of communication. Yet the definition, one might observe, extends to systems that we should hesitate to call languages in the ordinary sense. Consider the formal language of propositional logic. Its symbols — p, q, the horseshoe, the tilde, parentheses — are conventionally associated with definite meanings. Its syntactic rules are explicit and precise, determining which strings count as well-formed formulae. And it is used by a community — logicians and philosophers — for a communicative purpose, namely the representation and assessment of arguments. All three conditions are satisfied, yet propositional logic is not a language in the sense that English or French is. It cannot express a joke, a plea, a prayer, or an insult. It cannot convey the infinite variety of human thought and feeling that natural languages routinely manage. What the definition lacks is a condition requiring the expressive richness and open-endedness that distinguish natural languages from formal calculi designed for specialised purposes.

definition: A person S tells a lie if and only if (i) S asserts a proposition P, (ii) S does not hold P to be true — that is, S does not sincerely affirm P, and (iii) S intends the hearer to accept P as true.
conditions: S asserts a proposition P
S does not hold P to be true
S intends the hearer to accept P as true
counterexample: Consider an actor performing a play who delivers the line 'I am the Prince of Denmark.' The actor asserts a proposition; the actor does not hold the proposition to be true; and the actor arguably intends the audience to accept it as true within the context of the performance — the audience is to believe, for the duration of the play, that the character before them is the Prince. All three conditions are met, yet we should not say that the actor is lying. The assertion occurs within a conventionally understood framework of pretence that neutralises the deceptiveness that is essential to lying.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the assertion occur outside a conventionally recognised context of pretence, fiction, or performance. A condition excluding assertions made within such contexts is necessary to distinguish lying from acting, storytelling, and other sanctioned forms of non-sincere assertion.
passage: One might define lying as asserting a proposition that one does not hold to be true with the intention that the hearer accept it as true. This definition captures the paradigmatic cases of mendacity well: when a person looks another in the eye and says something that he privately regards as false, intending thereby to deceive, we call it a lie. The first condition — that there be an assertion — excludes mere silence and evasion. The second — that the speaker not hold the proposition to be true — excludes honest error. And the third — that the speaker intend the hearer to accept the proposition — excludes cases of irony and jest, where the speaker does not intend to be taken seriously. Yet the definition, as it stands, admits a case that we should plainly wish to exclude. Consider an actor upon the stage who delivers the line 'I am the Prince of Denmark.' The actor asserts a proposition; the actor does not hold it to be true; and the actor intends, in a certain sense, that the audience accept it — the performance aims to create the illusion that the person before them is indeed the Prince. All three conditions are met, yet no one would say the actor is lying. The difficulty is that the assertion occurs within a conventional framework of pretence, and both actor and audience understand this. The definition needs a further condition stipulating that the assertion take place outside any conventionally recognised context of fiction, performance, or play.

definition: A person S makes a promise to a person R if and only if (i) S utters words to the effect that S will perform some future action A, (ii) R understands S's utterance as a commitment to perform A, and (iii) S intends, at the time of utterance, to perform A.
conditions: S utters words committing to a future action A
R understands the utterance as a commitment
S intends at the time of utterance to perform A
counterexample: Consider a person who, under duress — with a pistol held to his head — utters the words 'I promise to give you all my money tomorrow.' The person utters words committing to a future action; the listener understands the utterance as a commitment; and we may even suppose that the person intends, at the moment of utterance, to comply in order to survive. All three conditions are met, yet we should hesitate to say that a genuine promise has been made. The utterance was extracted by force, and the speaker's will was not freely exercised. A promise made under duress lacks the voluntariness that is essential to the moral force of promising.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the promisor's utterance be voluntary — that it be made freely and without coercion — which is essential to the binding character of a genuine promise.
passage: It is tempting to define a promise as an utterance by which a person commits to performing some future action, where the hearer understands the utterance as such a commitment, and where the speaker intends to perform the action. This definition captures a great deal of what we mean by promising: the verbal commitment, the mutual understanding, and the sincerity of intention. It excludes idle remarks, since the hearer must understand the utterance as a commitment; it excludes insincere pledges made with no intention of fulfilment, since the speaker must intend to perform the action; and it requires an actual verbal act, thereby excluding mere private resolutions. Yet the definition fails to accommodate a case that is, one might observe, rather important. Suppose a man with a pistol demands that another person promise to hand over all his money on the following day. The victim, fearing for his life, utters the required words. The utterance is made; the listener understands it as a commitment; and the speaker may even intend to comply, at least in the moment. All three conditions are satisfied. But we should hesitate to call this a genuine promise. It is an utterance extracted by force, and its binding character — if it has any — is of a very different sort from that of a promise freely given. The definition requires a condition of voluntariness: the speaker must make the utterance of his own free will, without coercion or duress.

definition: An object O is a vehicle if and only if (i) O is a physical artefact — that is, it has been made or assembled by human beings, (ii) O is designed for the transportation of persons or goods from one place to another, and (iii) O is self-propelled or capable of being propelled by a mechanism other than direct human muscular effort.
conditions: O is a physical artefact
O is designed for transportation of persons or goods
O is self-propelled or propelled by a mechanism other than direct human muscular effort
counterexample: Consider a bicycle. A bicycle is a physical artefact; it is designed for the transportation of a person from one place to another; yet it is propelled by direct human muscular effort — the rider's legs turning the pedals. The third condition is not met. Yet a bicycle is universally regarded as a vehicle: it is subject to traffic laws, it appears in vehicle registries, and no ordinary speaker would deny that it is a vehicle. The definition excludes an object that is a paradigmatic case of what it purports to define.
missing_condition: The definition's third condition is too restrictive in excluding human-powered conveyances. It should require only that the object be designed to facilitate transportation — whether by mechanical, motorised, or human-powered means — rather than insisting on a non-muscular source of propulsion.
passage: One might propose that a vehicle is a physical artefact designed for the transportation of persons or goods and self-propelled or propelled by a mechanism other than direct human muscular effort. The first condition excludes natural objects — a log floating downstream may carry a person, but it is not a vehicle. The second condition captures the essential function of vehicles: they move things from place to place. And the third condition distinguishes vehicles from mere containers or platforms by requiring an independent source of motion. Cars, lorries, trains, ships, and aeroplanes all satisfy these conditions handsomely. Yet the definition, one might observe, excludes an object that is, by any ordinary reckoning, a vehicle. Consider the bicycle. It is a physical artefact; it is designed to transport a person from one place to another; but it is propelled entirely by the rider's muscular effort, transmitted through pedals and a chain to the wheels. The third condition is plainly not met. Yet the bicycle is subject to traffic regulations, it appears in dictionaries under the entry for 'vehicle,' and no competent speaker of the language would deny that it is one. The definition has been drawn too narrowly, excluding human-powered conveyances that satisfy the functional requirements of a vehicle in every respect save the source of propulsion.

definition: An object O is a tool if and only if (i) O is a physical object that can be grasped and manipulated by a human hand, (ii) O is used to perform a task that would be difficult or impossible to perform with the bare hand alone, and (iii) O has been deliberately fashioned or selected for the purpose of performing that task.
conditions: O can be grasped and manipulated by a human hand
O is used for a task difficult or impossible without it
O has been deliberately fashioned or selected for the task
counterexample: Consider a large industrial lathe. It is used to shape metal — a task that cannot be performed by the bare hand. It has been deliberately fashioned for this purpose. Yet it is not an object that can be grasped and manipulated by a human hand; it is a heavy, stationary machine that the operator controls by means of levers and wheels. The first condition is not satisfied, yet we should not hesitate to call a lathe a tool — or, at the very least, we recognise that the definition has excluded a large class of instruments that serve precisely the function the definition is intended to capture.
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in requiring that the object be hand-held. It should instead require only that the object be operated or directed by a human agent, whether by direct manual manipulation or by indirect control through levers, switches, or other interfaces.
passage: A natural definition of a tool holds that it is a physical object that can be grasped and manipulated by a human hand, that is used to perform a task difficult or impossible without it, and that has been deliberately fashioned or selected for that purpose. This definition covers a wide range of familiar cases: the hammer, the screwdriver, the saw, the chisel, the pliers. Each is a hand-held object, each enables a task that the bare hand cannot perform, and each has been made or chosen with that task in view. Yet one might observe that the definition, by insisting on the capacity to be grasped by a human hand, excludes a great many objects that serve precisely the function that the definition is intended to capture. Consider a large industrial lathe. It is a physical object; it is used to shape metal — a task that no human hand could accomplish unaided; and it has been deliberately designed and manufactured for that purpose. But it is not an object that can be grasped and manipulated by hand — it is a stationary machine of considerable weight, controlled by levers, wheels, and, in modern versions, computer interfaces. The definition excludes it, yet it is, in the broadest and most natural sense, a tool. The difficulty is that the definition has been drawn from the paradigm of hand tools and has elevated a feature of that paradigm — graspability — to a necessary condition, when the essential feature of a tool is its instrumental relation to a task, not the manner of its manipulation.

definition: A practice P is a custom if and only if (i) P has been performed regularly by members of a community over an extended period of time, (ii) P is transmitted from one generation to the next through imitation or instruction rather than through formal codification, and (iii) members of the community perform P without reflecting on reasons for doing so — that is, P is followed as a matter of habit or unreflective conformity.
conditions: P has been performed regularly over an extended period
P is transmitted through imitation or instruction, not formal codification
P is followed unreflectively, as a matter of habit
counterexample: Consider the custom, widespread in many cultures, of offering food to guests upon their arrival. This practice has been performed regularly for generations and is transmitted through imitation and family instruction rather than through any formal code. Yet hosts who follow this custom often do so reflectively and deliberately — they consider what to prepare, they take pride in their hospitality, and they are quite capable of articulating the reasons for the practice: that it is a mark of welcome, a gesture of generosity, a way of putting the guest at ease. The third condition — unreflective conformity — is not met, yet the practice is a paradigmatic custom.
missing_condition: The definition errs in requiring that customs be followed unreflectively. Many customs are performed with full awareness of their significance and with a conscious sense of the reasons behind them. The condition should instead require only that the practice be performed as part of an inherited communal pattern, whether reflectively or not.
passage: One might define a custom as a practice that has been performed regularly by members of a community over an extended period, transmitted from generation to generation through imitation or instruction, and followed unreflectively as a matter of habit. This definition captures a certain picture of custom: the practice that is simply done because it has always been done, without thought or deliberation, passed on through the unreflective mechanisms of socialisation. And there are cases that fit this picture — minor courtesies, everyday rituals, habitual patterns of greeting and parting — that are performed without the agent pausing to consider why. Yet one might observe that many of the most important customs are followed with a considerable degree of reflection and awareness. Consider the widespread custom of offering food to guests upon their arrival. This practice has been performed for generations and is transmitted through family example and instruction rather than through any formal code. But the host who follows it is often highly reflective: she considers what to prepare, she takes pride in the quality of her hospitality, and she can articulate the reasons for the practice — that it expresses welcome, generosity, and care. The third condition of the definition is not satisfied, yet the practice is a custom by any ordinary standard. The definition has confused a feature of some customs — unreflective performance — with a necessary condition of all customs.

definition: An arrangement A is a social institution if and only if (i) A consists of a set of rules, roles, and procedures that are publicly known and recognised within a community, (ii) A has been established by some identifiable act of founding — a charter, a decree, or a deliberate collective decision, and (iii) A serves a recognised social function — it addresses a need or interest shared by the members of the community.
conditions: A consists of publicly known rules, roles, and procedures
A was established by an identifiable founding act
A serves a recognised social function
counterexample: Consider the institution of money. Money consists of publicly known rules and conventions — one recognises that certain tokens can be exchanged for goods and services. It serves a recognised social function — facilitating exchange and enabling the division of labour. Yet money was not established by any identifiable act of founding. There was no charter, no decree, no deliberate collective decision that brought money into being. It emerged gradually, through the convergent practices of countless individuals over millennia, from barter to commodity money to coinage to paper currency. The second condition is not met, yet money is among the most fundamental of social institutions.
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in requiring an identifiable founding act. Many of the most important social institutions — money, language, the family — emerged gradually through unplanned social evolution rather than deliberate establishment. The definition needs to accommodate institutions that arise spontaneously.
passage: One might propose that a social institution is an arrangement consisting of publicly known rules, roles, and procedures, established by some identifiable founding act, and serving a recognised social function. This definition captures a range of familiar institutions: universities, parliaments, courts of law, and churches all consist of publicly recognised rules and roles, were established by deliberate acts of founding, and serve recognised social functions. The founding condition is intended to distinguish genuine institutions from mere habitual patterns of behaviour, and the functional condition ensures that the arrangement is not an arbitrary contrivance but addresses a genuine social need. Yet one might observe that the second condition — the requirement of an identifiable founding act — excludes some of the most fundamental institutions of human society. Consider money. Money consists of publicly recognised conventions: certain tokens are accepted in exchange for goods and services, and the rules governing their use are known to all participants. Money serves a recognised and indeed indispensable social function: it facilitates exchange, enables the division of labour, and provides a measure of value. But money was not established by any identifiable founding act. No individual or assembly decreed that shells, or metal discs, or paper notes should serve as a medium of exchange. The institution of money emerged gradually, through the convergent and unplanned practices of countless individuals over thousands of years. The definition fails to accommodate institutions that arise through spontaneous social evolution rather than deliberate creation.

definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is governed by a set of explicit rules that the participants voluntarily agree to follow, (ii) A involves competition — that is, the participants strive to achieve a goal, and at most one participant or team can achieve it, and (iii) A is undertaken for the purpose of amusement or recreation rather than for material gain.
conditions: A is governed by explicit rules voluntarily agreed upon
A involves competition with at most one winner
A is undertaken for amusement rather than material gain
counterexample: Consider a group of children playing ring-a-ring-o'-roses in a garden. The children hold hands, walk in a circle, sing a rhyme, and all fall down together at the end. They repeat this with evident delight. The activity is governed by a set of rules — hold hands, walk in a circle, sing the rhyme, fall down at the designated moment — that the children voluntarily follow. And it is undertaken purely for amusement. But there is no competition: no one wins, no one loses, and there is no goal that at most one participant can achieve. All the children fall down together and all are equally pleased. The second condition fails. Yet ring-a-ring-o'-roses is as clearly a game as any activity could be.
missing_condition: The definition imposes a competition requirement that excludes cooperative, collaborative, and purely playful games in which there is no winner and no contest. A game may be structured around shared enjoyment rather than competitive achievement, and the definition needs a condition broad enough to encompass both competitive and non-competitive forms of play.
passage: One might propose that an activity is a game if and only if it is governed by explicit rules that participants voluntarily agree to follow, involves competition in which at most one participant or team can prevail, and is undertaken for amusement rather than material gain. This definition captures the features of many familiar games: chess, football, and card games all involve explicit rules, competition, and recreational purpose. The rules condition distinguishes games from unstructured play; the competition condition provides the characteristic tension and goal-directedness; and the amusement condition separates games from work and obligation. Yet one might observe that these conditions, though they fit the competitive paradigm admirably, are too narrow for the concept they purport to define. Consider children playing ring-a-ring-o'-roses. The children hold hands, walk in a circle, sing a traditional rhyme, and all fall down together at the appointed moment, dissolving into laughter. The activity is governed by rules: there is a right way to play, and a child who refused to fall down would be corrected. It is undertaken purely for amusement. But there is no competition whatsoever. All the children do the same thing, all fall down together, and no one wins or loses. The competition condition fails entirely, yet the activity is unambiguously a game — indeed, it is among the oldest and most universal of children's games. The definition mistakes a feature of competitive games for a feature of games as such, excluding the vast domain of cooperative and purely playful activities that lack any competitive element.

definition: An object O is a chair if and only if (i) O has a raised seat designed for a single person, (ii) O has a back — that is, a vertical support rising from the rear of the seat against which the sitter may rest, and (iii) O has four legs that raise the seat to a height suitable for sitting.
conditions: O has a raised seat designed for a single person
O has a back against which the sitter may rest
O has four legs raising the seat to a suitable height
counterexample: Consider a modern cantilever chair of the sort designed by Mart Stam or Marcel Breuer — a single continuous tube of chrome-plated steel that curves from the floor forward, then upward to form the seat, and then upward again to form the back. Such a chair has a raised seat designed for a single person. It has a back against which the sitter rests. But it has no legs at all, let alone four of them; the entire structure rests on the front curve of the tube and the flat section that runs along the floor. The third condition fails. Yet the cantilever chair is indisputably a chair — it was designed as one, sold as one, and used as one, and no competent speaker of English would hesitate to call it a chair.
missing_condition: The definition specifies four legs as a necessary condition, but many chairs — cantilever chairs, pedestal chairs, bean-bag chairs — have no legs whatsoever. What is needed is merely the condition that the seat be supported at a suitable height by some structure or other, without specifying the form of that support.
passage: One might propose that an object is a chair if and only if it has a raised seat designed for a single person, a back against which the sitter may rest, and four legs that raise the seat to a suitable height. This definition captures the prototypical chair rather well: the ordinary kitchen chair, the dining chair, the wooden classroom chair all have seats, backs, and four legs. The single-person condition distinguishes chairs from sofas and benches. The back condition distinguishes chairs from stools. And the four-leg condition reflects the most common structural design. Yet one might observe that the four-leg condition is too specific. Consider a cantilever chair of the sort designed by Mart Stam in the late 1920s — a continuous tube of chrome-plated steel that curves from the floor forward, rises to form the seat, and curves upward again to form the back. The chair has a seat; it has a back; it is designed for a single person. But it has no legs at all. The entire structure is supported by the tension of the curved tube, which rests on the floor at two parallel runners rather than on four discrete legs. The third condition fails completely. Yet no one would deny that the cantilever chair is a chair. It was designed, manufactured, sold, and used as a chair, and it has occupied a place of honour in the history of furniture design for nearly a century. The definition has mistaken the structural form of the most common chairs for a necessary feature of chairs as such. What is needed is the weaker condition that the seat be supported at a suitable height by some structure, without prescribing the particular form that the support must take.

definition: An object or performance O is a work of art if and only if (i) O is produced by intentional human activity — that is, it is deliberately made or arranged by a human agent, (ii) O is designed to produce an aesthetic response in its audience — pleasure, admiration, contemplation, or similar, and (iii) O exhibits technical skill — that is, its production requires training, practice, or mastery of a craft.
conditions: O is produced by intentional human activity
O is designed to produce an aesthetic response
O exhibits technical skill in its production
counterexample: Consider Marcel Duchamp's Fountain — a mass-produced porcelain urinal submitted to an art exhibition in 1917 under the pseudonym R. Mutt. Duchamp's act was intentional: he deliberately selected the object and submitted it. And it was designed to produce a response — albeit one of provocation, conceptual puzzlement, and critical reflection rather than conventional aesthetic pleasure. But the object exhibits no technical skill on Duchamp's part; he did not make the urinal, and its selection required no craft or training. The third condition fails. Yet Fountain is now among the most discussed and influential works of art of the twentieth century, and its status as art is defended by virtually every major institution and critic in the field.
missing_condition: The definition requires technical skill in production, but this excludes conceptual and ready-made art in which the artistic achievement lies in the act of selection, framing, or recontextualisation rather than in the craft of making. A condition recognising that art may consist in the conceptual act of designating an object for aesthetic or critical attention, rather than in the skilled production of the object itself, is needed.
passage: One might propose that an object or performance is a work of art if and only if it is produced by intentional human activity, is designed to produce an aesthetic response, and exhibits technical skill. This definition captures a great deal of what is traditionally meant by art: a painting by Vermeer, a sonata by Mozart, a novel by Austen — each is intentionally made, each aims at aesthetic effect, and each displays consummate skill. The intentionality condition distinguishes art from natural beauty; the aesthetic condition distinguishes it from functional craft; and the skill condition distinguishes accomplished art from idle doodling. Yet one might observe that the definition, shaped as it is by traditional paradigms, fails to accommodate certain of the most significant developments in modern art. Consider Duchamp's Fountain — a mass-produced porcelain urinal submitted to an art exhibition in 1917. Duchamp's act was intentional; he chose the object with deliberation and submitted it under a pseudonym. And the submission was designed to produce a response — not conventional aesthetic pleasure, perhaps, but conceptual shock and critical reflection. Yet the object exhibits no technical skill on Duchamp's part. He did not manufacture the urinal; he merely selected it and placed it in a new context. The skill condition fails. But Fountain is now regarded as one of the most important works of art of the twentieth century, and its influence on subsequent art has been immense. The definition mistakes the means by which traditional art achieves its effects — skilled craftsmanship — for a necessary condition of art as such, overlooking the possibility that art may consist in the act of selecting, framing, or recontextualising an object for aesthetic and critical attention.

definition: A statement S made by a speaker A to a hearer B is a lie if and only if (i) S is false, (ii) A believes that S is false at the time of utterance, and (iii) A intends B to accept S as true.
conditions: S is false
A believes S is false at the time of utterance
A intends B to accept S as true
counterexample: Consider an actor on stage who delivers the line 'I am the King of Denmark' in a performance of Hamlet. The statement is false — the actor is not the King of Denmark. The actor believes the statement is false; he is under no illusion about his identity. And the actor intends, in a certain sense, for the audience to accept the statement — he aims to create a dramatic illusion in which the audience imaginatively takes him for the character. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the actor is not lying. The theatrical context establishes a shared understanding that the statements made on stage are not assertions of fact but elements of a dramatic performance. No one in the audience believes that the actor is genuinely claiming to be the King of Denmark.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the statement be made in a context in which the speaker purports to be making a sincere assertion — that is, in which the norms of truthfulness are understood to be in force. Without such a condition, fictional performances, hypothetical illustrations, and other non-assertoric utterances are wrongly classified as lies.
passage: One might propose that a statement is a lie if and only if it is false, the speaker believes it to be false, and the speaker intends the hearer to accept it as true. These three conditions capture the core of lying as ordinarily understood: the liar says what is not so, knows it is not so, and aims to deceive. The falsity condition ensures that accidentally true statements are not lies; the belief condition ensures that honest mistakes are not lies; and the intention condition ensures that slips of the tongue and other unintended falsehoods are excluded. Yet one might observe that these conditions, taken jointly, classify certain perfectly innocent utterances as lies. Consider an actor on stage delivering the line 'I am the King of Denmark' in a production of Hamlet. The statement is false. The actor believes it to be false. And the actor intends, in a theatrical sense, for the audience to accept the statement — the success of the performance depends on the audience's willingness to entertain the dramatic fiction. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet the actor is not lying. The theatrical context makes it clear to all parties that the statements made on stage are not sincere assertions but elements of a shared imaginative exercise. The definition omits a crucial contextual condition: the requirement that the statement be made in circumstances in which the speaker purports to be making a sincere assertion — circumstances in which the norms of truthfulness are understood to be operative. Without this condition, dramatic performances, illustrative hypotheticals, and other non-assertoric utterances are wrongly counted as lies.

definition: An utterance U by a speaker A to a hearer B constitutes a promise if and only if (i) U expresses A's commitment to perform some future action F, (ii) B understands U as expressing such a commitment, and (iii) A intends to perform F at the time of utterance.
conditions: U expresses A's commitment to perform some future action F
B understands U as expressing such a commitment
A intends to perform F at the time of utterance
counterexample: Consider a person who, under threat of violence, tells a kidnapper 'I promise I will bring you the money tomorrow.' The utterance expresses a commitment to perform a future action. The kidnapper understands it as such. But the speaker has no intention whatsoever of bringing the money; the speaker intends to go directly to the police. The third condition fails. Yet the utterance has the form and social force of a promise — the kidnapper will hold the speaker to it, and even the speaker recognises that the utterance creates a binding, if coerced, commitment. The speaker has made a promise, albeit one extracted under duress and with no intention of fulfilment.
missing_condition: The definition requires sincere intention to perform the promised action, but this excludes coerced and insincere promises, which are nevertheless promises — albeit defective ones. What is needed is the recognition that a promise is constituted by the public act of undertaking a commitment, not by the private mental state of intending to fulfil it.
passage: One might propose that an utterance constitutes a promise if and only if it expresses the speaker's commitment to perform a future action, the hearer understands it as such, and the speaker intends to perform the action at the time of utterance. This definition aims to capture the essential features of promising: the forward-looking commitment, the communicative success, and the sincerity of the promisor. The commitment condition distinguishes promises from mere predictions; the understanding condition ensures that the promise has been successfully communicated; and the sincerity condition ensures that the speaker is not merely uttering words but genuinely undertaking an obligation. Yet one might observe that the sincerity condition is too strong. Consider a person held captive by a kidnapper who demands a ransom. The captive says, 'I promise I will bring you the money tomorrow.' The utterance expresses a commitment; the kidnapper understands it as a promise and acts upon it. But the captive has no intention of bringing the money — the captive intends to go to the police at the earliest opportunity. The sincerity condition fails entirely. Yet it seems clear that the captive has made a promise — a promise extracted under duress, a promise made with the intention of breaking it, but a promise nonetheless. The kidnapper can intelligibly accuse the captive of having broken a promise. What the definition lacks is the recognition that a promise is constituted by the public act of undertaking a commitment in a social context, not by the private mental state of the promisor. A promise may be insincere, coerced, or made with every intention of violation, and yet remain a promise for all that.

definition: An object O is a vehicle if and only if (i) O is designed primarily for the transport of persons or goods from one place to another, (ii) O is powered by an engine or motor, and (iii) O travels along the surface of the ground.
conditions: O is designed primarily for transporting persons or goods
O is powered by an engine or motor
O travels along the surface of the ground
counterexample: Consider a sailing ship of the sort that carried passengers and cargo across the Atlantic for centuries. The ship is designed primarily for the transport of persons and goods from one place to another. But it is not powered by an engine or motor — it is propelled by the wind acting upon its sails. And it does not travel along the surface of the ground — it travels across the surface of the sea. The second and third conditions both fail. Yet a sailing ship is as clear an instance of a vehicle as one could wish for; it was for centuries the principal means of long-distance transport.
missing_condition: The definition is too narrow in two respects: it restricts the power source to engines and motors, excluding wind-powered, animal-drawn, and human-powered vehicles, and it restricts the medium of travel to the ground, excluding waterborne and airborne vehicles. A condition requiring only that the object be designed and used for the conveyance of persons or goods by any means of propulsion through any medium would be more adequate.
passage: One might propose that an object is a vehicle if and only if it is designed primarily for the transport of persons or goods, is powered by an engine or motor, and travels along the surface of the ground. This definition captures the features of the most familiar modern vehicles — motor cars, lorries, and buses — admirably well. The transport condition distinguishes vehicles from buildings and tools; the engine condition distinguishes them from static containers; and the ground-travel condition places them in the familiar landscape of roads and railways. Yet one might observe that the definition is far too narrow even for ordinary usage. Consider a sailing ship — a vessel of the sort that carried passengers and cargo across the oceans for centuries before the advent of steam power. A sailing ship is designed for transport; no one would deny that. But it has no engine or motor; it is propelled entirely by the wind. And it does not travel along the ground; it moves across the surface of the sea. Both the second and third conditions fail, yet a sailing ship is unquestionably a vehicle. Indeed, for most of recorded history, the sailing ship was the most important vehicle in the world, enabling trade, exploration, and the movement of peoples on a scale that no ground-based vehicle could match. The definition has taken the features of the motor car — the dominant vehicle of the present day — and elevated them to necessary conditions of vehiclehood as such, forgetting that the concept of a vehicle is far older and broader than the internal combustion engine and the paved road.

definition: An object O is a tool if and only if (i) O is a physical artefact — that is, it has been made or modified by a human being, (ii) O is used to extend or amplify the user's physical capacities, and (iii) O is held in the hand during use.
conditions: O is a physical artefact made or modified by a human
O extends or amplifies the user's physical capacities
O is held in the hand during use
counterexample: Consider a potter's wheel — a heavy rotating disc upon which the potter shapes clay. The potter's wheel is a physical artefact, made by human hands. It extends and amplifies the potter's physical capacities, enabling the production of symmetrical vessels that would be extremely difficult to form by hand alone. But the potter's wheel is not held in the hand during use; it sits on the floor or on a workbench, and the potter operates it with a foot pedal or kick while using both hands to shape the clay. The third condition fails. Yet the potter's wheel is among the oldest and most important tools in the history of human craft.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the tool be hand-held, but many essential tools — lathes, looms, anvils, kilns — are stationary during use. What is needed is merely the condition that the tool be operated or directed by the user in the course of its employment, whether by hand, by foot, or by any other means of human control.
passage: One might propose that an object is a tool if and only if it is a physical artefact made or modified by a human, extends or amplifies the user's physical capacities, and is held in the hand during use. This definition captures the prototypical tools — the hammer, the screwdriver, the chisel — very well. The artefact condition distinguishes tools from natural objects used in their unmodified state; the amplification condition captures the purpose of tools, which is to enable us to do what we could not do, or could not do as well, with our bare hands; and the hand-held condition reflects the intimate, manual character of tool-use. Yet one might observe that the hand-held condition is too restrictive. Consider a potter's wheel, a device of great antiquity. The wheel is a physical artefact, purpose-built for a specific task. It extends and amplifies the potter's capacities, enabling the creation of symmetrical vessels of a quality and consistency that hand-building alone could scarcely achieve. But it is not held in the hand; it is a heavy disc mounted on an axle, operated by a foot pedal or a kick, while the potter's hands are occupied with shaping the clay. The third condition fails. Yet the potter's wheel is indisputably a tool — indeed, one of the most important tools in the history of human civilisation. The definition has taken a feature of small, portable tools and made it a necessary condition of toolhood as such, overlooking the vast class of stationary tools — lathes, looms, anvils, and kilns — that are operated but not held. What is needed is the weaker condition that the tool be under the user's operational control during use, whether that control is exercised by hand, by foot, or by any other means.

definition: A system of communication S is a language if and only if (i) S employs a finite vocabulary of discrete symbols, (ii) S has a grammar — a set of rules for combining symbols into well-formed expressions, and (iii) every well-formed expression in S has a determinate meaning that is fixed by convention.
conditions: S employs a finite vocabulary of discrete symbols
S has a grammar governing the combination of symbols
Every well-formed expression has a determinate meaning fixed by convention
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'He went to the bank.' This sentence is a well-formed expression of English, composed from a finite vocabulary according to the rules of English grammar. Yet it does not have a determinate meaning. It may mean that a person went to the edge of a river, or that a person went to a financial institution. The meaning is fixed not by convention alone but by the context of utterance — the surrounding sentences, the situation, the shared background of speaker and hearer. The third condition, which requires that every well-formed expression have a single determinate meaning fixed by convention, fails for this sentence and for the vast number of ambiguous expressions in any natural language. Yet English is as clearly a language as anything could be.
missing_condition: The definition requires that meaning be wholly determined by convention, but in natural languages, meaning is pervasively underdetermined by convention and must be resolved by context. A condition acknowledging that expressions may have meanings that are conventionally constrained but contextually completed would be more adequate.
passage: One might propose that a system of communication is a language if and only if it employs a finite vocabulary of discrete symbols, has a grammar governing the combination of symbols into well-formed expressions, and assigns a determinate meaning, fixed by convention, to every well-formed expression. This definition captures the features of formal languages — the languages of logic and mathematics — admirably. In such languages, the vocabulary is finite, the grammar is explicit, and the meaning of every well-formed formula is precisely determined. But one might observe that the definition fits natural languages rather less comfortably. Consider the English sentence 'He went to the bank.' The sentence is composed from a finite vocabulary and is well-formed by the rules of English grammar. But it does not have a determinate meaning. The word 'bank' may refer to the edge of a river or to a financial institution, and the pronoun 'he' may refer to any male individual. The sentence's meaning is not fixed by convention alone; it depends upon the context of utterance — the surrounding discourse, the physical situation, the shared assumptions of speaker and hearer. The third condition fails, not just for this sentence but for a great proportion of the expressions of any natural language, which are rife with ambiguity, polysemy, and context-dependence. Yet English is a language par excellence. The definition has been shaped by the model of formal languages, in which meaning is conventionally determined, and has mistakenly imposed this requirement on natural languages, in which convention constrains meaning but context completes it.

definition: An object O is a work of art if and only if (i) O is a unique, one-of-a-kind creation — it is not a mass-produced item, (ii) O is intended by its creator to express an emotion or idea, and (iii) O belongs to one of the recognised fine arts — painting, sculpture, music, poetry, or architecture.
conditions: O is a unique, one-of-a-kind creation
O is intended to express an emotion or idea
O belongs to one of the recognised fine arts
counterexample: Consider a woodblock print by Hokusai, such as The Great Wave off Kanagawa. Each impression is printed from a carved wooden block, and hundreds of impressions were produced — the print is by no means a unique, one-of-a-kind creation. Yet the print is intended to express an idea — the power of the sea, the frailty of human endeavour — and it belongs to the visual arts.
