The first condition fails; but The Great Wave is universally recognised as one of the great works of art in the history of human civilisation. The definition excludes prints, photographs, and all other art forms in which multiple copies are an integral part of the medium.
missing_condition: The definition wrongly requires uniqueness, which excludes printmaking, photography, and other reproductive media that are full-fledged art forms. What is needed is a condition requiring that the work bear the impress of its creator's artistic intention, without stipulating that only a single physical instance can exist.
passage: One might propose that an object is a work of art if and only if it is a unique, one-of-a-kind creation, it is intended by its creator to express an emotion or idea, and it belongs to one of the recognised fine arts. This definition reflects a venerable conception of art: the masterpiece as the singular, unrepeatable product of individual genius. The uniqueness condition captures the aura of the original; the expressive condition ties art to the inner life of the artist; and the categorical condition anchors the definition in the established tradition of the fine arts. Yet one might observe that the uniqueness condition is too restrictive. Consider a woodblock print by Hokusai — The Great Wave off Kanagawa, perhaps the most famous print in the history of art. Each impression is pulled from a carved block, and hundreds of impressions were produced during the artist's lifetime. The print is not unique; it is, by design, a multiple. Yet it is intended to express an idea — the sublime power of the ocean — and it belongs to the visual arts. The first condition fails. But no one would deny that The Great Wave is a work of art; it is, on the contrary, among the most celebrated works ever produced. The definition has been shaped by the paradigm of painting and sculpture, in which the physical object is typically unique, and has mistakenly elevated this contingent feature to a necessary condition. Printmaking, photography, and other reproductive arts are no less artistic for existing in multiple copies. What is needed is not uniqueness but the impress of artistic intention.

definition: A statement S by a speaker A is a lie if and only if (i) A knows that S is false, (ii) A makes S with the intention of causing the hearer to form a false belief, and (iii) A benefits materially from the hearer's forming the false belief.
conditions: A knows that S is false
A intends to cause the hearer to form a false belief
A benefits materially from the hearer's forming the false belief
counterexample: Consider a parent who tells a young child that Father Christmas delivered the presents under the tree on Christmas morning. The parent knows the statement is false. The parent intends the child to form the belief that Father Christmas is real and that he delivered the presents. But the parent derives no material benefit from the child's belief — the parent has paid for the presents and gains nothing financially or materially from the deception. The third condition fails. Yet the parent has told the child a lie — a benevolent, culturally sanctioned, and perhaps even charming lie, but a lie nonetheless.
missing_condition: The definition imposes a material-benefit condition that is plainly too narrow. Many lies are told for reasons having nothing to do with material gain — to spare feelings, to maintain a fiction, to avoid embarrassment, or out of sheer habit. The condition should require only that the speaker make the false statement deliberately, regardless of the motive.
passage: One might propose that a statement is a lie if and only if the speaker knows it to be false, makes it with the intention of causing the hearer to form a false belief, and benefits materially from the hearer's forming that belief. The first two conditions capture the deceptive core of lying — the conjunction of known falsehood and intention to mislead. The third condition adds a motive: the liar lies for gain. This addition is intended to distinguish malicious lies from innocent or inconsequential falsehoods. Yet one might observe that the material-benefit condition is far too restrictive. Consider a parent who tells a young child on Christmas morning that Father Christmas delivered the presents under the tree. The parent knows the statement is false. The parent intends the child to believe it — indeed, the parent goes to some trouble to maintain the fiction, setting out biscuits and milk the night before and carefully disguising the handwriting on the gift tags. But the parent derives no material benefit whatsoever from the child's belief. The motive is sentimental and cultural, not mercenary. The third condition fails, yet the statement is plainly a lie — a kindly one, embedded in a beloved tradition, but a falsehood knowingly told with the intention to deceive. The definition has confused the motive that accompanies some lies — the desire for material gain — with a necessary condition of lying as such. Lies may be told for gain, for kindness, for convenience, or for no discernible reason at all; what makes them lies is not the motive but the knowing utterance of falsehood with intent to deceive.

definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is governed by a set of rules that constrain the behaviour of participants, (ii) A involves competition between two or more parties aiming at a determinate outcome, and (iii) A is undertaken voluntarily for the purpose of amusement or recreation.
conditions: A is governed by rules constraining participant behaviour
A involves competition between two or more parties
A is undertaken voluntarily for amusement or recreation
counterexample: Consider the activity of solitaire — a single player arranging cards according to fixed rules, attempting to achieve a particular configuration. Solitaire is governed by rules. It is undertaken voluntarily for amusement. But it involves no competition between two or more parties; the player contends only against the arrangement of the deck. Yet no one would deny that solitaire is a game.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate games played by a single person against chance, a puzzle, or an impersonal obstacle, by requiring that competition involve two or more parties rather than allowing for challenges that are non-competitive or self-directed.
passage: One might propose that an activity counts as a game if it is rule-governed, competitive, and voluntarily undertaken for amusement. This definition captures many central cases: chess, tennis, and bridge are rule-governed, competitive, and recreationally motivated. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by the familiar activity of solitaire. The player deals cards according to a fixed set of rules, attempts to arrange them in a specified order, and undertakes the activity voluntarily for the pleasure of it. Two of the three conditions are clearly satisfied. But solitaire is not competitive in the sense the definition requires — there is no second party, no opponent, no rival. The player contends against the contingencies of the deal, not against another agent. If we insist upon competition between two or more parties as a necessary condition of being a game, we must exclude solitaire from the category — a conclusion that strikes one as plainly incorrect, for solitaire is among the most familiar of card games. What is needed is a broader condition that encompasses not only competition against opponents but also the overcoming of impersonal challenges, puzzles, or the resistances of chance.

definition: An object O is a chair if and only if (i) O has a seat designed to support a single person in a sitting posture, (ii) O has a back against which the sitter may rest, and (iii) O is a freestanding piece of furniture intended primarily for sitting.
conditions: O has a seat designed to support a single person in sitting posture
O has a back for the sitter to rest against
O is a freestanding piece of furniture intended primarily for sitting
counterexample: Consider a bar stool of the sort found in public houses throughout Britain. It has a seat designed to support a single person. It is freestanding and intended primarily for sitting. But it has no back. Yet one would not hesitate to call it a piece of furniture for sitting, and in common parlance it is frequently referred to as a kind of chair — a 'bar chair' or simply 'a seat at the bar.' The definition excludes it, though ordinary usage does not.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for seats that lack a back but are nonetheless classified as chairs in ordinary language, suggesting that the presence of a back is a typical but not a necessary feature of the concept.
passage: A natural analysis of the concept of a chair might require a seat for one person, a back to rest against, and the status of a freestanding piece of furniture intended for sitting. One might observe that this captures the paradigmatic chair — a dining chair, an armchair, a desk chair — quite well. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the common bar stool. A bar stool has a seat designed to support a single person in a sitting posture. It is freestanding. It is intended primarily for sitting. But it possesses no back — the sitter perches upon the seat without any surface to lean against. The definition, applied strictly, excludes the bar stool from the category of chairs. Yet in ordinary speech, bar stools are frequently spoken of as a kind of chair, and establishments advertise them as seating without the least suggestion of impropriety. The difficulty is that the presence of a back, while typical of chairs, appears not to be essential to the concept. A more adequate definition would need to treat the back as a characteristic but dispensable feature, perhaps replacing the strict requirement with a weaker condition acknowledging that most chairs possess backs without insisting that all must.

definition: An object O is a work of art if and only if (i) O is an artefact — that is, a product of intentional human activity, (ii) O is created with the primary intention of being contemplated for its aesthetic qualities, and (iii) O possesses some degree of formal organisation or design.
conditions: O is an artefact produced by intentional human activity
O is created with the primary intention of aesthetic contemplation
O possesses some degree of formal organisation or design
counterexample: Consider a piece of driftwood, shaped by the sea into a form of striking beauty, placed upon a mantelpiece and admired for its graceful curves. The driftwood possesses formal organisation — its shape has a balance and elegance that invite contemplation. It is contemplated for its aesthetic qualities. But it is not an artefact; no human hand shaped it. The first condition is not met, yet many would regard it as a work of art, or at least as something that functions as one.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate objects not produced by human intention that are nevertheless appropriated, displayed, and contemplated as art, suggesting that the concept of art may depend upon the context of presentation and reception as much as upon the manner of production.
passage: One might define a work of art as an artefact created with the primary intention of aesthetic contemplation and possessing some degree of formal organisation. This definition has the virtue of excluding natural objects — sunsets, crystals, mountain vistas — which are beautiful but not art, and of insisting on intentionality rather than mere accident. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the phenomenon of found art. Consider a piece of driftwood, worn by the sea into a form of remarkable elegance and balance. A person discovers it on the shore, carries it home, and places it upon a shelf where it is admired by visitors for its graceful curves and pleasing proportions. It possesses formal organisation of a high order — the sea has sculpted it more finely than many a human carver could manage. It is contemplated for its aesthetic qualities. But it is not an artefact. No human being fashioned it; no intention governed its production. If we insist that art must be the product of intentional human activity, we exclude this driftwood, along with innumerable objects that galleries and collectors treat as art. What the definition overlooks is the possibility that an object may enter the category of art through its context of display and reception, rather than through the manner of its making.

definition: A system S is a language if and only if (i) S comprises a set of symbols or signs, (ii) S is governed by syntactic rules that determine how symbols may be combined, and (iii) the combinations of symbols in S are used by a community to communicate propositions.
conditions: S comprises a set of symbols or signs
S is governed by syntactic rules for combining symbols
Combinations of symbols are used by a community to communicate propositions
counterexample: Consider the formal system of first-order predicate logic. It comprises a set of symbols — variables, quantifiers, connectives. It is governed by syntactic rules specifying which strings of symbols are well-formed. And logicians use it to express and communicate propositions. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might hesitate to call predicate logic a language in the ordinary sense; it is a formal calculus, lacking the expressive richness, ambiguity, and historical accretion that characterise natural languages such as English or French.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish natural languages from formal calculi by requiring that the system exhibit features characteristic of natural linguistic practice — historical development, pragmatic context-dependence, or the capacity for figurative and non-literal expression.
passage: A common analysis holds that a language is a system of symbols governed by syntactic rules and used by a community to communicate propositions. One might observe that this captures the essential structure of English, Mandarin, and Arabic alike: each comprises symbols (words or morphemes), each is governed by grammatical rules, and each serves as a medium of propositional communication. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis presents itself when we consider formal systems. First-order predicate logic comprises a set of symbols — variables, constants, predicates, quantifiers, connectives. It is governed by precise syntactic rules that determine which strings are well-formed formulae. And logicians use it to express and communicate propositions with great precision. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet to call predicate logic a language in the same sense as English seems to elide a distinction of considerable importance. Predicate logic lacks the historical accretion, the pragmatic sensitivity to context, the capacity for metaphor, irony, and figurative expression, and the systematic ambiguity that characterise natural languages. A more satisfactory definition would need to include some condition capturing these features — perhaps the requirement that the system have evolved through communal use over time, or that it possess the resources for non-literal expression.

definition: A statement S made by a speaker A to a hearer B is a lie if and only if (i) S is false, (ii) A believes S to be false at the time of utterance, and (iii) A intends B to accept S as true.
conditions: S is false
A believes S to be false at the time of utterance
A intends B to accept S as true
counterexample: Consider a doctor who tells a patient, falsely, that a sugar pill is a powerful analgesic, believing the statement to be false, and intending the patient to accept it as true so as to produce a placebo effect. All three conditions are met. Yet many would hesitate to classify this as a lie in the morally loaded sense, for the doctor's intention is therapeutic rather than deceptive for personal advantage, and the practice is sanctioned by medical tradition.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the speaker's intention involve some element of deception for its own sake or for personal advantage, rather than for the perceived benefit of the hearer, thereby conflating benevolent falsehoods with culpable lies.
passage: One might analyse a lie as a false statement made by someone who believes it false and intends the hearer to accept it as true. This analysis captures the paradigmatic case: the swindler who tells a prospective buyer that a watch is genuine when he knows it to be counterfeit. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises in medical practice. A doctor tells a patient that a sugar pill is a potent analgesic. The statement is false; the doctor knows it to be false; and the doctor intends the patient to accept it as true, so that the patient may benefit from the placebo effect. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet one might observe that to classify this as a lie in the full moral sense is to assimilate it to fraud and deception in a manner that many would find excessive. The doctor does not seek personal advantage; she acts from therapeutic concern; and the practice has been sanctioned, with qualifications, by the medical profession. What the definition neglects is a condition distinguishing deception practised for the speaker's benefit or for its own sake from benevolent falsehood uttered for the hearer's welfare. Without such a condition, the definition casts too wide a net, capturing cases that common moral judgment would separate from lies properly so called.

definition: An utterance U by a speaker A constitutes a promise if and only if (i) U expresses A's commitment to perform some future action F, (ii) A intends to be bound by U, and (iii) U is directed toward a specific hearer B who understands U as creating an obligation.
conditions: U expresses A's commitment to perform a future action F
A intends to be bound by U
U is directed toward a specific hearer who understands it as obligation-creating
counterexample: Consider a politician who, at a public rally, declares to the assembled crowd: 'I shall reduce taxation if elected.' The utterance expresses a commitment to a future action. The politician intends, at the moment of speaking, to be bound by the declaration. And the audience understands the declaration as creating an obligation. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet the utterance is not directed toward a specific hearer — it is addressed to an indefinite multitude — and it is unclear that it creates an obligation in the same way that a promise between two individuals does.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate promises made to indefinite or collective audiences, where the requirement of a specific hearer is not met but the binding force of the commitment is nonetheless recognised.
passage: One might analyse a promise as an utterance expressing a commitment to future action, made with the intention of being bound, and directed toward a specific hearer who understands the commitment as obligation-creating. This captures the core case: 'I promise to return your book on Tuesday,' spoken by one friend to another, satisfies all three conditions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises in the public sphere. When a politician declares before a crowd of thousands that she will reduce taxation if elected, her utterance expresses a commitment to a future course of action. She intends, at the moment of speaking, to be held to her word. And the audience understands the declaration as creating an obligation — they will reproach her if she fails to act upon it. Yet the utterance is not directed toward any specific hearer; it is addressed to an indefinite multitude, none of whom stands in the particular relationship of promisee to promisor. If the definition requires a specific hearer, it excludes campaign pledges, public oaths, and ceremonial vows addressed to assemblies — utterances that ordinary language treats as promissory. A more adequate definition would need to relax the specificity condition, permitting the obligation to run not only to identified individuals but also to communities, institutions, or the public at large.

definition: An object O is a vehicle if and only if (i) O is designed for the transport of persons or goods, (ii) O is capable of self-propelled movement, and (iii) O operates on or above a surface such as a road, track, or waterway.
conditions: O is designed for transport of persons or goods
O is capable of self-propelled movement
O operates on or above a surface
counterexample: Consider a horse-drawn carriage of the sort that once served as the principal conveyance in European cities. The carriage is designed for the transport of persons. It operates upon a road. But it is not self-propelled — it moves only by virtue of the horses harnessed to it. The second condition is not satisfied, yet the carriage is unquestionably a vehicle. Statutes, insurance documents, and ordinary speech all treat it as one.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for vehicles propelled by external animate or mechanical agents rather than by an internal engine, thereby excluding horse-drawn carriages, rickshaws, and towed conveyances that are plainly vehicles.
passage: A natural analysis of the concept of a vehicle might hold that a vehicle is an object designed for transport, capable of self-propelled movement, and operating on or above a surface. This captures the motorcar, the locomotive, and the steamship. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the horse-drawn carriage. For centuries, the carriage was the principal vehicle of overland transport in Europe. It is designed for the conveyance of persons and goods. It operates upon roads and tracks. But it is not self-propelled; it has no engine, no motor, no internal source of motive power. It moves only because horses are harnessed to it and pull it along. If we insist upon self-propulsion as a necessary condition, we must exclude the carriage from the category of vehicles — along with rickshaws, sedan chairs, bicycles, and indeed every conveyance that preceded the invention of the steam engine. This is a consequence that ordinary usage and legal definition alike refuse to countenance. A horse-drawn carriage is a vehicle. What the definition overlooks is that a vehicle need not contain its own motive power; it may be propelled by an external agent, whether animate or mechanical, and remain a vehicle nonetheless.

definition: An object O is a tool if and only if (i) O is an artefact fashioned by human beings, (ii) O is designed to extend or amplify a specific human physical capacity, and (iii) O is manipulated directly by the hand or body of the user during its operation.
conditions: O is an artefact fashioned by human beings
O is designed to extend or amplify a specific human physical capacity
O is manipulated directly by hand or body during operation
counterexample: Consider a mousehole trap — a device set by a householder and left to operate unattended. The trap is an artefact, fashioned by human hands. It is designed to extend a human capacity — the capacity to catch vermin — far beyond what the unaided hand could accomplish. Yet it is not manipulated directly by the user during its operation; the user sets it and departs, and the trap operates autonomously when a mouse disturbs the trigger. The third condition is not met, yet the trap is plainly a tool.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate tools that operate autonomously once set in motion by the user, requiring direct bodily manipulation throughout rather than merely at the moment of deployment.
passage: One might define a tool as a human-made artefact designed to amplify a physical capacity and manipulated directly by the user during operation. This captures the hammer, the saw, and the trowel: each is an artefact, each amplifies a human physical power, and each requires the user's hand throughout. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by the common mousetrap. The mousetrap is an artefact, fashioned with considerable ingenuity. It extends a human capacity — the capacity to catch vermin — in a manner that the unaided hand could scarcely match. But it is not manipulated directly by the user during its operation. The householder sets the trap, places it beside the mousehole, and retires for the night. The trap operates autonomously when the mouse disturbs the trigger. At the moment of operation, no human hand is upon it. If direct manipulation during operation is a necessary condition, the mousetrap is excluded from the category of tools — as would be fishing nets, snares, and any device that operates by delayed or autonomous action. A more adequate definition would need to relax the requirement of continuous manipulation to encompass tools that are set in motion by the user but thereafter function on their own.

definition: A transfer T of an object O from person A to person B constitutes a gift if and only if (i) A voluntarily relinquishes possession of O to B, (ii) A receives no material compensation in return, and (iii) A intends T as a benefit to B.
conditions: A voluntarily relinquishes possession to B
A receives no material compensation
A intends the transfer as a benefit to B
counterexample: Consider a wealthy patron who gives a large sum of money to a struggling artist, not out of benevolence toward the artist, but solely to cultivate a reputation for generosity in the eyes of the community. The patron voluntarily relinquishes possession. She receives no material compensation. Yet her intention is not to benefit the artist but to benefit herself — to enhance her social standing. The third condition requires that the transfer be intended as a benefit to the recipient, and in a formal sense the patron does intend the artist to receive the money. But the motivating purpose is self-regarding, and to call this a gift in the fullest sense seems to strain the concept.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the donor's primary motivation be directed toward the recipient's welfare rather than toward the donor's own advantage, thereby admitting transfers motivated by self-interest that merely happen to benefit the recipient.
passage: One might analyse a gift as a voluntary, uncompensated transfer intended to benefit the recipient. This analysis captures the paradigmatic case admirably: a parent who gives a child a birthday present acts voluntarily, receives nothing in return, and intends the child's delight. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis presents itself in the sphere of patronage. Consider a wealthy patron who bestows a large sum upon a struggling artist. She acts voluntarily and receives no material compensation. In a formal sense, she intends the artist to benefit — she means him to receive the money. All three conditions are satisfied. But her purpose in making the transfer is not the artist's welfare; it is the cultivation of her own reputation for generosity. She gives not because she cares for the artist's well-being but because she wishes to be seen as magnanimous. The artist is incidental to her design; any equally visible recipient would have served as well. To call this a gift in the full sense seems to stretch the concept past its natural limits. What the definition lacks is a condition requiring that the donor's primary motivation be directed toward the recipient's welfare rather than toward the donor's own social or psychological advantage.

definition: A relationship R between persons A and B constitutes a friendship if and only if (i) A and B have mutual goodwill toward one another, (ii) A and B regularly engage in shared activities or conversation, and (iii) A and B are aware of and reciprocate the other's goodwill.
conditions: A and B have mutual goodwill
A and B regularly engage in shared activities or conversation
A and B are aware of and reciprocate the other's goodwill
counterexample: Consider two soldiers who serve together for years, share dangers, rely upon one another, and part with genuine mutual affection — but who, once the war ends, never contact one another again and live on opposite sides of the world. During their years of service, all three conditions were met. But their friendship, if it was one, persists in memory and affection even though they no longer engage in shared activities or conversation. The definition, tied to regular interaction, would declare their friendship to have ceased the moment they parted.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the persistence of friendship through long periods of separation, requiring regular interaction when the bond may in fact endure through memory, loyalty, and standing goodwill that does not require continual activation.
passage: A natural analysis of friendship might hold that it consists in mutual goodwill, regular shared activity, and reciprocal awareness of the goodwill. This captures many cases: two colleagues who lunch together daily, enjoy one another's company, and openly acknowledge their affection satisfy all three conditions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider friendships that survive separation. Two soldiers serve together through a long war, sharing dangers, relying upon one another, and coming to feel a deep mutual regard. When the war ends, they part. One returns to Scotland, the other to New Zealand. They never meet again, yet each retains a vivid memory of the other and a warm sense of enduring affection. Decades later, each would unhesitatingly call the other a friend. The goodwill persists. The awareness persists. But the regular shared activity has ceased entirely. The definition, which requires ongoing interaction, would pronounce the friendship over at the moment of parting — a verdict that seems false to the phenomenon. What is needed is a condition allowing for the persistence of friendship through periods of separation, recognising that the bond may endure in memory, loyalty, and a standing disposition to goodwill that does not require continual exercise.

definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is governed by a set of rules accepted by all participants, (ii) A has a determinate outcome — a winner, a loser, or a draw, and (iii) A is undertaken for the purpose of amusement or recreation.
conditions: A is governed by rules accepted by all participants
A has a determinate outcome such as winning or losing
A is undertaken for amusement or recreation
counterexample: Consider a child playing with a set of wooden blocks, building towers and knocking them down. There are no rules accepted by all participants — the child plays alone and imposes no fixed structure on the activity. There is no determinate outcome: no winning or losing, no draw, no score. Yet the activity is undertaken for amusement, and most people would call it a game — or at the very least, would say the child is playing a game. The first two conditions fail, yet the concept applies.
missing_condition: The definition requires rules and determinate outcomes, but many activities we call games — free play, sandbox play, imaginative play — lack both fixed rules and competitive outcomes, being structured instead by the participant's own spontaneous purposes.
passage: One might propose that an activity is a game if and only if it is governed by rules accepted by all participants, has a determinate outcome such as winning or losing, and is undertaken for amusement or recreation. This definition captures the competitive games that first come to mind: chess has its rules, its winners and losers, and is played for the pleasure of the contest. Cricket, bridge, and draughts likewise satisfy all three conditions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the play of young children. Consider a child of three or four years sitting on a nursery floor with a set of wooden blocks. The child builds a tower, contemplates it for a moment, then knocks it down with evident delight, and begins again. No rules govern the activity; the child is free to build as high or as low as she pleases, to knock the tower down at any moment or to leave it standing, to use all the blocks or only some. There is no determinate outcome — no winning or losing, no score to be tallied, no draw to be declared. The first two conditions of the definition fail entirely. Yet the activity is undertaken for amusement, and most of us would say without hesitation that the child is playing a game, or at least engaged in an activity that falls within the extension of the concept. One might observe that the definition has been modelled upon the competitive, rule-governed games of adults and has made rules and outcomes necessary conditions, overlooking the broad category of free, unstructured play that constitutes a great deal of what we call gaming in ordinary speech — the play of children, the play of imagination, the play that proceeds without competition and without constraint.

definition: An object O is a tool if and only if (i) O has been manufactured or modified for a specific practical purpose, (ii) O is used by a human agent to perform a task that the agent could not perform, or could not perform as efficiently, with bare hands, and (iii) O is portable — that is, it can be carried by a single person.
conditions: O has been manufactured or modified for a specific practical purpose
O is used to perform a task not achievable or not efficient with bare hands
O is portable — carriable by a single person
counterexample: Consider a large industrial lathe bolted to the floor of a factory. The lathe has been manufactured for a specific practical purpose — the shaping of metal. It is used by human agents to perform tasks that could not be performed with bare hands. But it is not portable; it cannot be carried by a single person, or indeed by several persons, without the aid of a crane. The third condition fails. Yet the lathe is a tool — one of the most important tools in metalworking — and to exclude it on grounds of portability seems to mistake a frequent feature of small hand-tools for a necessary feature of tools in general.
missing_condition: The definition requires portability, but many of the most important tools — lathes, printing presses, looms, forges — are stationary and heavy, so that portability is characteristic only of a subclass of tools rather than a defining feature of the category.
passage: One might define a tool as an object that has been manufactured or modified for a specific practical purpose, is used by a human agent to perform a task that bare hands could not accomplish as efficiently, and is portable. The definition captures the paradigmatic cases with ease. A hammer has been manufactured for driving nails, enables the carpenter to perform a task impossible with bare hands, and can be carried in one hand. A screwdriver, a saw, and a chisel likewise satisfy all three conditions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the large, stationary instruments of industry. Consider a lathe in a metalworking factory. The lathe has been manufactured for the specific purpose of shaping metal by rotation and cutting. It is used by a skilled machinist to perform tasks utterly beyond the capacity of bare hands — to turn a cylinder to precise tolerances, to cut threads, to bore holes of exact diameter. The first two conditions are amply satisfied. But the lathe is not portable. It weighs several hundred pounds, it is bolted to the factory floor, and it requires a crane to move. No single person can carry it, and the suggestion that it ought to be carriable in order to qualify as a tool is plainly absurd. The lathe is a tool — indeed, it is one of the most fundamental tools in the history of manufacture. One might observe that the definition has taken the hand-tool as its model and has elevated portability, which is merely a characteristic of one class of tools, to the status of a necessary condition. In doing so it excludes the looms, forges, presses, and lathes upon which modern civilisation depends — a class of tools whose importance dwarfs that of the portable implements the definition was designed to capture.

definition: A transfer of an object O from a person A to a person B constitutes a gift if and only if (i) A voluntarily relinquishes possession of O to B, (ii) A does not receive or expect any material compensation in return, and (iii) A intends the transfer as an expression of goodwill or affection towards B.
conditions: A voluntarily relinquishes possession to B
A does not receive or expect material compensation
A intends the transfer as an expression of goodwill or affection
counterexample: Consider the practice, common in many cultures, of offering a gift to a powerful official in order to secure a favourable decision. A merchant gives a fine cloak to a magistrate. The merchant voluntarily relinquishes the cloak. He does not receive or expect direct material compensation — the magistrate does not pay for the cloak. Yet the merchant's intention is not to express goodwill or affection but to influence the magistrate's judgement. The third condition fails. Nonetheless, both parties call the transfer a gift, and the social conventions surrounding the exchange are the conventions of gift-giving, not of commerce. The concept of a gift, as ordinarily employed, appears to extend to cases where the donor's motive is strategic rather than affectionate.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the donor's intention be the expression of goodwill, but many transfers that are socially recognised as gifts are motivated by strategic, diplomatic, or obligatory considerations rather than by genuine affection, so that the requirement of affectionate intent is too narrow.
passage: One might hold that a transfer of an object constitutes a gift if and only if the donor voluntarily relinquishes possession, does not receive or expect material compensation, and intends the transfer as an expression of goodwill or affection. This captures the paradigmatic birthday or Christmas present: a parent gives a toy to a child out of love, freely and without expectation of return. It also captures the gift between friends — a book chosen with care and given with warmth. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the widespread practice of strategic or diplomatic gift-giving. Consider a merchant in a trading town who gives a fine cloak to the local magistrate. The transfer is voluntary; the merchant hands over the cloak of his own accord. No material compensation is received or expected; the magistrate does not pay for the cloak. But the merchant's intention is not to express goodwill or affection. He does not particularly care for the magistrate as a person. His aim is to cultivate a favourable relationship, to incline the magistrate towards leniency in future disputes, to secure preferential treatment. The transfer is strategic, not affectionate. The third condition of the definition fails. Yet both parties recognise the transaction as a gift. The social forms observed — the wrapping, the presentation, the words of thanks — are the forms of gift-giving. The concept of a gift, as it operates in ordinary life and in the practices of many cultures, is broad enough to encompass transfers motivated by strategy, obligation, or diplomacy, and not only those motivated by genuine affection. One might observe that the definition has idealised the concept, taking the loving gift as the paradigm and excluding the vast territory of gifts that are given from motives other than love — yet this territory constitutes a significant portion of what we ordinarily call gift-giving.

definition: Two persons A and B are friends if and only if (i) A and B have a mutual affection for one another, (ii) A and B regularly spend time together, and (iii) A and B confide personal matters to each other.
conditions: A and B have mutual affection
A and B regularly spend time together
A and B confide personal matters to each other
counterexample: Consider two soldiers who served together in the same regiment during a war, shared hardships and dangers over several years, and developed a profound bond of loyalty and trust. After the war, they returned to their separate countries and never met again. They do not regularly spend time together — indeed, they spend no time together at all. They do not confide personal matters, for they have lost contact. Yet each would, if asked, describe the other as a friend — one of the truest friends he ever had. The mutual affection persists across decades of separation, and the friendship is no less real for the absence of regular contact and mutual confidence.
missing_condition: The definition requires regular contact and mutual confiding, but many of the most enduring friendships persist across long separations without either, sustained by shared history and continuing affection rather than by ongoing interaction.
passage: One might propose that two persons are friends if and only if they have mutual affection, regularly spend time together, and confide personal matters to each other. The definition has a reassuring solidity. It captures the friendship of neighbours who visit one another weekly, share their concerns over tea, and take a genuine interest in each other's welfare. It captures the friendship of colleagues who lunch together daily and discuss the difficulties of their lives. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the friendships forged in adversity and preserved across distance. Consider two soldiers who served together in the same regiment during a protracted war. They shared the hardships of the march, the terrors of battle, the discomforts of the camp. Over the course of several years they developed a bond of loyalty, trust, and mutual affection as deep as any human connection. When the war ended, they returned to their respective countries — one to England, the other to New Zealand — and never met again. They exchanged a few letters in the first years, then lost contact entirely. They do not regularly spend time together; they have not seen each other in decades. They do not confide personal matters; they have no means of communication. The second and third conditions of the definition fail. Yet each man, if asked, would name the other as one of the truest friends of his life. The affection has not diminished; the bond has not dissolved. One might observe that the definition has modelled friendship upon the pattern of close, ongoing companionship and has made regular contact and mutual confidence necessary conditions, overlooking the deep friendships that endure across decades of separation, sustained not by continued interaction but by the indelible memory of shared experience and the persistence of mutual regard.

definition: An utterance U by a person A constitutes a promise if and only if (i) U is a declaration by A that A will perform some future action, (ii) U is addressed to a specific person B who understands and accepts the declaration, and (iii) A intends, at the time of uttering U, to perform the action declared.
conditions: U is a declaration that A will perform a future action
U is addressed to a specific person who understands and accepts it
A intends at the time of utterance to perform the declared action
counterexample: Consider a political candidate who, at a public rally, declares that he will reduce taxation if elected. The declaration is made not to a specific individual but to a crowd of thousands, and through the press to millions more. No single person is the addressee of the promise, and the audience does not accept the declaration in the way that an individual accepts a personal undertaking. The second condition fails. Yet the electorate treats the candidate's declaration as a promise and will hold him to it if elected. The concept of a promise, in ordinary usage, extends to public declarations addressed to no one in particular.
missing_condition: The definition requires a specific addressee, but many promises — political pledges, public vows, declarations of intent — are made to large groups or to the public at large, with no single identifiable person as the recipient.
passage: One might define a promise as an utterance in which a person declares that he will perform some future action, addresses the declaration to a specific person who understands and accepts it, and intends at the time of utterance to carry out what he has declared. The definition captures the intimate promise between individuals: a father promises his child that he will attend the school play; a friend promises to return a borrowed book by Friday. In each case the declaration is made, a specific person receives and accepts it, and the speaker intends to fulfil his word. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the promises of public life. Consider a political candidate who stands before a rally of several thousand supporters and declares, with evident solemnity, that he will reduce taxation if he is elected. The crowd cheers; the newspapers report the pledge; the electorate takes note. The declaration is plainly a promise — the candidate himself calls it a promise, his supporters hold him to it, and his opponents reproach him if he fails to honour it. But to whom has the promise been made? Not to any specific person. The audience is a crowd of thousands, and beyond them, through the press, millions more. No single individual has been addressed, and no single individual has accepted the declaration in the way that a friend accepts a personal undertaking. The second condition of the definition fails. One might observe that the definition has been modelled upon the private, face-to-face promise and has made the existence of a specific addressee a necessary condition, overlooking the large and important class of promises that are made publicly, to groups, to institutions, or to the world at large — promises that are no less binding for lacking an identifiable individual recipient.

definition: A statement S by a person A constitutes a lie if and only if (i) A asserts S, (ii) S is false, and (iii) A believes, at the time of assertion, that S is false.
conditions: A asserts S
S is false
A believes S to be false at the time of assertion
counterexample: Consider a person who is asked at a dinner party whether he enjoyed the meal. He replies that it was delicious, when in fact he found it barely tolerable. He asserts the statement, the statement is false, and he believes it to be false. All three conditions are met, and the definition classifies this as a lie. Yet many people would hesitate to call this a lie; they would call it a polite fiction, a social courtesy, or a white lie — something categorically different from the mendacity of a swindler or a perjurer. The definition draws no distinction between malicious deceit and the harmless courtesies that lubricate social intercourse.
missing_condition: The definition neglects the intent to deceive in a manner that may cause harm or that violates the reasonable expectations of the hearer, grouping together malicious falsehoods and the innocent courtesies of social life under a single category.
passage: One might hold that a statement constitutes a lie if and only if the speaker asserts it, the statement is false, and the speaker believes it to be false at the time of assertion. This definition has the virtue of simplicity: it requires only an assertion, a falsehood, and awareness of the falsehood. It captures the paradigmatic cases of lying without difficulty. The confidence trickster who tells his victim that the shares will double in value, knowing this to be false, has lied. The witness who tells the court that he was at home, knowing he was at the scene, has lied. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the polite fictions of social life. A guest is asked, at the conclusion of a dinner party, whether he enjoyed the meal. The food was indifferent and the conversation dull, but the guest replies with a warm smile that the meal was delightful and the evening most enjoyable. He asserts a statement. The statement is false. He believes it to be false. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied, and the definition classifies his response as a lie. Yet we should hesitate to say that this man has lied. What he has done is to observe the ordinary courtesies of social life — to spare his host's feelings, to maintain the pleasantness of the occasion, to discharge the small obligations of civility that decent people everywhere acknowledge. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only assertion, falsity, and awareness of falsity, is unable to distinguish between the calculated deceit of the swindler and the harmless courtesy of the dinner guest. It groups together cases that are, in their moral character and in their social function, quite different, and thereby fails to capture the element of intent to deceive or to cause harm that is characteristic of lying in the full and proper sense.

definition: An event E in the life of a person A constitutes good luck if and only if (i) E is beneficial to A, (ii) E was not brought about by A's deliberate action, and (iii) E was not reasonably foreseeable by A.
conditions: E is beneficial to A
E was not brought about by A's deliberate action
E was not reasonably foreseeable by A
counterexample: Consider a farmer who plants his crops in the usual way and at the usual time. A drought strikes the region and destroys the harvest of every farm in the county — except his, because his fields happen to sit above an underground aquifer that keeps the soil moist. The survival of his crop is beneficial to him. It was not brought about by his deliberate action — he did not know about the aquifer and did not plant his crops there for that reason. And it was not reasonably foreseeable — he had no way of predicting the drought or the presence of the aquifer. All three conditions are met, and we would call this good luck. Yet the farmer's neighbours might hesitate to attribute his good fortune entirely to luck; they might suspect that his choice of land, though not made with the aquifer in mind, reflected some wisdom or familiarity with the terrain that gave him an advantage. The boundary between luck and unrecognised competence is blurred in a way the definition does not address.
missing_condition: The definition does not account for cases in which a beneficial outcome, though unintended and unforeseeable, is partly attributable to the agent's competence, character, or positioning, so that the line between luck and unrecognised skill cannot be drawn as sharply as the definition implies.
passage: One might propose that an event constitutes good luck for a person if it is beneficial to him, was not brought about by his deliberate action, and was not reasonably foreseeable. This definition captures the standard cases. A man walking down a street finds a banknote on the pavement: the event is beneficial, he did not deliberately cause it, and he could not have foreseen it. A traveller misses a train that later derails: the narrow escape is beneficial, unintended, and unforeseeable. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis concerns the frequent entanglement of luck with unrecognised competence. Consider a farmer who plants his crops at the customary time and in the customary manner, paying no special attention to the composition of his soil. A severe drought strikes the region, and every farm in the county loses its harvest — except his, because his fields happen to sit above an underground aquifer of which he was entirely unaware. The survival of his crop is beneficial; it was not brought about by deliberate action, for he did not know about the aquifer; and it was not foreseeable, for he had no reason to expect either the drought or the aquifer. All three conditions are met, and we would call this good luck. But one might observe that the farmer's choice of land, though not made with the aquifer in mind, was not entirely random. Perhaps he settled on those fields because the soil looked rich, because the grass grew thick, because some inarticulate instinct told him the ground was favourable. His good fortune may be partly attributable to a form of unrecognised competence — a sensitivity to signs of fertile ground that he could not have articulated but that guided his choice nonetheless. The definition draws a sharp line between luck and deliberate action, but the cases that matter most in life often fall in the grey region where fortune and unacknowledged skill are inextricably intertwined.

definition: A person A owns an object O if and only if (i) A has the right to use O as A sees fit, (ii) A has the right to exclude others from using O, and (iii) A acquired O through a legitimate transfer or through A's own labour.
conditions: A has the right to use O as A sees fit
A has the right to exclude others from using O
A acquired O through legitimate transfer or own labour
counterexample: Consider a person who has inherited a building that has been designated as a protected historical monument. She owns the building — the deed is in her name, and the law recognises her as its owner. She has the right to exclude others from entering it. She acquired it through legitimate transfer, namely inheritance. But she does not have the right to use it as she sees fit: she cannot demolish it, alter its facade, or convert it to a purpose incompatible with its historical character. The law imposes severe restrictions on what she may do with her own property. The first condition fails, yet she is unquestionably the owner.
missing_condition: The definition requires unrestricted use, but ownership in practice is always subject to legal, social, and moral constraints, so that the right to use as one sees fit is never absolute and should not be made a necessary condition of ownership.
passage: One might hold that a person owns an object if and only if she has the right to use it as she sees fit, the right to exclude others from using it, and acquired it through legitimate transfer or her own labour. The definition captures the simplest cases of ownership. A craftsman who has made a chair from timber he purchased has the right to sit in it, to sell it, to burn it if he chooses; he can exclude others from using it; and he acquired it through his own labour. All three conditions are satisfied, and the ownership is clear. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the ownership of protected historical buildings. Consider a woman who has inherited a Tudor manor house that has been designated as a listed building by the relevant authority. She is the legal owner: the deed is in her name, the inheritance was legitimate, and no one disputes her title. She has the right to exclude trespassers. But she does not have the right to use the building as she sees fit. She may not demolish it. She may not alter its external appearance without official permission. She may not convert it to certain commercial uses. The law imposes constraints upon her use that are severe and comprehensive. The first condition of the definition — the right to use the object as one sees fit — is not satisfied. Yet she is the owner in every legally and socially recognised sense. One might observe that the definition has idealised ownership, treating the unlimited right of use as a necessary condition, when in fact ownership in every known legal system is hedged about with restrictions — building codes, environmental regulations, the rights of neighbours, the claims of the public interest — so that the right to use as one sees fit is an abstraction that corresponds to no actual instance of ownership.

definition: A place P is a person A's home if and only if (i) A resides in P on a permanent basis, (ii) A has a legal right to occupy P, and (iii) P provides A with shelter and the facilities necessary for daily life.
conditions: A resides in P permanently
A has a legal right to occupy P
P provides shelter and facilities for daily life
counterexample: Consider a nomadic herder who moves with his flock across a vast steppe, never staying in any one place for more than a few weeks. He has no permanent residence, no fixed abode, and no legal right to occupy any particular patch of ground. Yet he would say without hesitation that the steppe is his home — that the wide grasslands, the seasonal camps, the familiar routes of migration constitute home in the fullest sense. The first and second conditions fail entirely, yet the concept of home applies.
missing_condition: The definition requires permanent residence and legal occupancy, but for nomadic peoples and for many others whose sense of home is tied to a landscape, a community, or a way of life rather than to a fixed address, home is constituted by belonging and familiarity rather than by legal tenure and settled habitation.
passage: One might propose that a place is a person's home if the person resides there permanently, has a legal right to occupy it, and it provides shelter and the facilities for daily life. The definition captures the settled domestic arrangements that most people in modern societies enjoy. A family living in a house they own or rent, with kitchen, bedrooms, and plumbing, occupies a home in the fullest sense, and the three conditions are plainly met. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the lives of nomadic peoples. Consider a herder who moves with his flock across the grasslands of Central Asia, following routes that his ancestors have followed for centuries. He never stays in one place for more than a few weeks. He has no permanent residence; each camp is temporary, erected and dismantled according to the needs of the season and the flock. He has no legal right to occupy any particular patch of ground, for the steppe is common land, and his presence in any given spot is transient. The first and second conditions of the definition fail. Yet if one were to ask this man where his home is, he would gesture at the wide steppe — the grasslands, the rivers, the mountain passes that define his annual circuit — and say that this is his home, that he belongs to this land and this land to him. His sense of home is no less genuine, no less deeply felt, than that of the urban householder with a deed of ownership. One might observe that the definition has modelled the concept of home upon the fixed domestic arrangements of settled societies and has made permanence and legal right necessary conditions, overlooking the possibility that home may be constituted not by tenure and fixity but by belonging, familiarity, and the habits of a life lived in a particular landscape.

definition: An occasion of eating constitutes a meal if and only if (i) the food consumed has been deliberately prepared or arranged, (ii) the eating occurs at a customary time — morning, midday, or evening, and (iii) the food is consumed while seated at a table or designated eating place.
conditions: The food has been deliberately prepared or arranged
The eating occurs at a customary time
The food is consumed while seated at a table or designated eating place
counterexample: Consider a group of mountaineers who, at two o'clock in the afternoon during an ascent, stop on a narrow ledge and eat bread, cheese, and dried fruit that they have brought in their rucksacks. The food has been deliberately prepared and packed. But the eating does not occur at a customary mealtime — two in the afternoon is neither luncheon nor dinner. And the food is not consumed at a table or designated eating place — the mountaineers sit on a rock ledge hundreds of metres above the valley floor. The second and third conditions fail. Yet the mountaineers would call this a meal, and rightly so; it sustains them for the exertions ahead and serves the social and nutritional functions that meals characteristically serve.
missing_condition: The definition ties the concept of a meal to the conventions of settled, domestic life — customary times and designated places — but many genuine meals occur outside these conventions, being defined instead by their role in sustaining the eater and punctuating the activities of the day.
passage: One might hold that an occasion of eating constitutes a meal if the food has been deliberately prepared, the eating occurs at a customary time, and the food is consumed while seated at a table or designated eating place. This definition captures the meals of settled domestic life: breakfast taken at the kitchen table in the morning, luncheon at the dining table at midday, dinner at the table in the evening. Each involves prepared food, a customary hour, and a designated place. The three conditions are met without difficulty. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the meals of those whose activities do not conform to the rhythms of the household. Consider a party of mountaineers engaged in a long ascent. At two o'clock in the afternoon — an hour that is neither lunch nor dinner by any domestic convention — they halt on a narrow ledge of rock and unpack bread, cheese, dried fruit, and a flask of tea from their rucksacks. The food has been prepared and packed with care. But the hour is unconventional, and the eating place is a rock ledge on the face of a cliff, with no table, no chairs, no domestic apparatus of any kind. The second and third conditions of the definition fail. Yet the mountaineers would call this a meal without hesitation, and they would be right to do so. It serves the purposes that meals serve: it nourishes, it provides a pause in the day's labour, it brings the party together for a few minutes of shared rest and sustenance. One might observe that the definition has taken the conventions of the bourgeois dining room as necessary features of the concept and has overlooked the many contexts in which meals are taken at irregular hours, in improvised settings, and without the furnishings of domestic life, yet are meals in every sense that matters.

definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is governed by a set of rules that the participants voluntarily accept, (ii) the outcome of A is uncertain at the outset, and (iii) A is undertaken for the purpose of amusement or recreation.
conditions: A is governed by a set of rules that the participants voluntarily accept
The outcome of A is uncertain at the outset
A is undertaken for the purpose of amusement or recreation
counterexample: Consider a professional chess player who competes in a tournament for a large monetary prize. He accepts the rules of chess voluntarily, and the outcome of each match is uncertain. But he does not play for amusement or recreation; he plays as his livelihood, with the intense seriousness and anxiety that attend any form of professional labour. The third condition fails. Yet it would be perverse to deny that what the professional chess player is doing is playing a game. Chess does not cease to be a game merely because the player's motive is financial rather than recreational.
missing_condition: The definition wrongly makes recreational purpose essential, failing to recognise that an activity may remain a game regardless of the motives for which it is undertaken — professional competition, pedagogical exercise, or scientific research — provided its structural features are preserved.
passage: One might define a game as an activity governed by rules voluntarily accepted, whose outcome is uncertain, and which is undertaken for amusement or recreation. This definition captures the familiar cases readily enough: a group of children playing tag in the garden accept the rules freely, cannot predict who will be caught, and participate for the sheer fun of it. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by professional sport. Consider a chess grandmaster competing in an international tournament. He accepts the rules of chess; the outcome of each game is uncertain; but his purpose is not amusement. He plays as a profession, for prize money and ranking points, with a seriousness and competitive intensity that leave no room for the lighthearted spirit of recreation. His preparation is gruelling — hours of opening analysis, physical training, psychological conditioning. One might observe that the anxiety and pressure he experiences during a crucial match are the very antithesis of amusement. The third condition of the definition is not satisfied. Yet it appears manifestly absurd to conclude that the grandmaster is not playing a game. Chess is a game whether it is played by children in a garden or by professionals in a tournament hall. The definition, by making recreational purpose essential, confuses the motives for which a game is played with the structural features that make it a game.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) U expresses the speaker's commitment to perform some future action, (ii) the speaker intends to perform the action at the time of utterance, and (iii) the addressee desires or has an interest in the performance of the action.
conditions: U expresses the speaker's commitment to perform some future action
The speaker intends to perform the action at the time of utterance
The addressee desires or has an interest in the performance of the action
counterexample: Consider a man who promises his dying father that he will never sell the family estate. The father dies moments later. The man has expressed a commitment to a future action, and at the moment of utterance he sincerely intends to honour it. But the addressee — the father — is now dead and can no longer be said to desire or have an interest in anything. The third condition fails from the moment the father expires. Yet we should not wish to say that the promise ceased to be a promise at the instant of the father's death. The man remains bound by it, and the moral force of the commitment persists.
missing_condition: The definition makes the addressee's continuing interest a necessary condition, but fails to recognise that a promise may remain binding even when the addressee can no longer take an interest in its fulfilment, as when the addressee dies or becomes permanently unable to care about the outcome.
passage: A plausible analysis of promising holds that an utterance constitutes a promise if and only if it expresses the speaker's commitment to a future action, the speaker sincerely intends to perform the action, and the addressee has an interest in its performance. This analysis captures the ordinary case well: I promise to return your book, I intend to do so, and you have an interest in receiving it back. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is the deathbed promise. A man sits beside his dying father and promises that he will never sell the family estate. At the moment of utterance, all three conditions are satisfied. The man expresses his commitment; he intends to honour it; and the father, still alive, has a deep interest in the preservation of the estate. But the father dies moments later. One might observe that the third condition now fails — the father is dead and can no longer desire or take an interest in anything. If the addressee's interest is a necessary condition for the existence of a promise, then it appears that the promise ceased to be a promise at the instant of the father's death. Yet this conclusion is plainly unacceptable. The man feels himself bound by the promise, and ordinary moral sentiment supports his feeling. We should say that the promise persists and retains its force, even though the person to whom it was made can no longer care whether it is kept. The definition, by requiring the addressee's continuing interest, makes a promise's existence depend upon a condition that may be extinguished by circumstances entirely beyond the speaker's control.

definition: A statement S is a lie if and only if (i) S is asserted by a speaker who believes S to be false, (ii) the speaker intends to deceive the audience by asserting S, and (iii) the audience is unaware that S is false.
conditions: S is asserted by a speaker who believes S to be false
The speaker intends to deceive the audience by asserting S
The audience is unaware that S is false
counterexample: Consider a poker player who bluffs — he bets heavily whilst holding a weak hand, thereby representing to the other players that his hand is strong. He believes his representation to be false, and he intends the other players to be deceived by it. The other players are (at the moment of the bluff) unaware that his hand is weak. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one would hesitate to call the bluff a lie. Bluffing is an accepted and expected part of the game; the other players know that bluffs may occur and participate with that understanding. To call every bluff a lie would be to mischaracterise the nature of the activity.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the assertion be made in a context where sincerity is the operative norm, thereby including cases — such as bluffing in games, acting on stage, or telling tall tales at a dinner party — where the audience has implicitly consented to the possibility of non-sincere assertion.
passage: One might define a lie as a statement asserted by someone who believes it false, with the intention of deceiving the audience, where the audience is unaware of its falsity. This definition captures paradigmatic cases of mendacity: the witness who testifies falsely in court, the child who denies eating the biscuits, the politician who misrepresents the facts of a matter. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is the commonplace practice of bluffing in card games. A poker player who bets heavily on a weak hand represents to his opponents that he holds strong cards. He believes this representation to be false. He intends his opponents to be deceived — that is the very point of the bluff. And his opponents are, at the moment, unaware that his hand is weak. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet it appears that we should hesitate to call the poker player's bluff a lie. The other players have entered into the game with the understanding that bluffing is a legitimate and expected tactic. They have, as it were, consented to the possibility that their opponents will misrepresent their hands. One might observe that lying presupposes a context in which sincerity is the operative norm, and that a bluff occurs in a context where that norm has been suspended by mutual agreement. The definition, by attending only to the speaker's belief, intention, and the audience's ignorance, takes no account of the normative context within which the assertion is made.

definition: An object O is a tool if and only if (i) O is used by an agent to accomplish a task that the agent could not accomplish, or could not accomplish as efficiently, with bare hands, (ii) O is a physical artefact — a material thing deliberately fashioned for the purpose, and (iii) O retains its functional integrity across multiple uses.
conditions: O is used by an agent to accomplish a task not efficiently accomplishable by bare hands
O is a physical artefact deliberately fashioned for its purpose
O retains its functional integrity across multiple uses
counterexample: Consider a man stranded on a beach who picks up a sharp stone and uses it to cut a length of rope. The stone enables him to accomplish a task he could not manage with his bare hands. It is a physical object. And it retains its functional integrity — the stone is as sharp after the cut as before, and could be used again. But the stone is not a physical artefact; it was not deliberately fashioned by anyone. It is a natural object pressed into service. The second condition fails. Yet it seems clear that the man has used the stone as a tool, and indeed we speak naturally of 'tools' found in nature, as when primatologists describe chimpanzees using sticks to extract termites.
missing_condition: The definition wrongly restricts tools to deliberately fashioned artefacts, failing to accommodate the widespread and perfectly natural use of found objects — stones, sticks, bones — as tools, both by humans and by other animals.
passage: It is natural to define a tool as a physical artefact used by an agent to accomplish a task that bare hands could not accomplish as efficiently, and that retains its functional integrity across repeated uses. This definition captures the paradigmatic cases: a hammer, a saw, a screwdriver — each is a deliberately fashioned object, used to accomplish specific tasks, and capable of repeated service. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is near at hand. Imagine a man stranded upon a beach who discovers that he must cut a length of rope. Having no knife, he casts about and finds a stone with a naturally sharp edge. He uses the stone to saw through the rope, and succeeds admirably. The stone meets the first and third conditions: it accomplishes a task the man could not manage with his bare hands, and it retains its sharpness across uses. But it fails the second condition, for the stone is not an artefact. No one fashioned it for the purpose of cutting; it is a natural object, shaped by geological processes, which the man has pressed into service. One might observe, however, that we should unhesitatingly call the stone a tool in this context. The primatologist who watches a chimpanzee use a stick to extract termites from a mound does not hesitate to call the stick a tool, despite the fact that no one has fashioned it. The definition, by insisting upon deliberate manufacture, excludes a class of objects that function as tools in every respect that matters.

definition: A transfer of an object O from person A to person B constitutes a gift if and only if (i) A voluntarily relinquishes possession of O to B, (ii) A expects no material return from B in exchange for O, and (iii) the transfer is motivated by A's goodwill toward B.
conditions: A voluntarily relinquishes possession of O to B
A expects no material return from B in exchange
The transfer is motivated by A's goodwill toward B
counterexample: Consider a man who gives an expensive watch to his business rival, not out of goodwill but as a calculated gesture designed to embarrass the rival, who will feel obligated to reciprocate with an equally expensive gift he cannot afford. The man voluntarily relinquishes the watch, and he expects no material return. But the third condition fails, for his motive is not goodwill but social manipulation. Yet the watch is wrapped in paper, presented with a card, and received by the rival as a gift. The social form and the legal consequences are those of a gift. It appears that whatever we think of the giver's motives, the transfer is nonetheless a gift.
missing_condition: The definition imposes a requirement of benevolent motive that, while characteristic of the ideal case, is not essential to the concept, for a transfer may function as a gift in its social form, legal status, and effects upon the recipient regardless of the donor's underlying intentions.
passage: One might propose that a gift is a voluntary transfer of an object from one person to another, made without expectation of material return and motivated by goodwill. This characterisation captures the central cases well: the birthday present given to a friend, the charitable donation made to a stranger in need. In each case, the transfer is voluntary, no return is expected, and the motive is benevolent. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when one considers the strategic gift. Imagine a man who presents an expensive watch to his business rival. He has no goodwill toward the rival; on the contrary, the gift is a calculated manoeuvre designed to place the rival under a social obligation that he will find burdensome to discharge. The giver expects no material return — the strategic value of the gesture lies precisely in the rival's inability to reciprocate adequately. He voluntarily relinquishes the watch. The first two conditions are met, but the third is not: the motive is manipulation, not goodwill. Yet one might observe that the transfer possesses every external mark of a gift. It is wrapped in decorative paper, presented with a card, and received as a gift by the rival and by all who witness the exchange. In law, it would be treated as a gift. The definition, by making goodwill a necessary condition, excludes transfers that are gifts in every socially and legally relevant sense.

definition: An event E is a matter of luck for a person S if and only if (i) E is significant to S — it affects S's interests or welfare, (ii) E is not the result of S's deliberate action or planning, and (iii) E is improbable — S had no reasonable expectation that E would occur.
conditions: E is significant to S, affecting S's interests or welfare
E is not the result of S's deliberate action or planning
E is improbable — S had no reasonable expectation it would occur
counterexample: Consider a pedestrian who walks to work along her usual route and arrives safely. Her safe arrival is significant to her — it affects her welfare profoundly. It is not the result of any special planning or precaution beyond her ordinary habit. But it is not improbable; on the contrary, she arrives safely on the vast majority of days, and she has every reasonable expectation of doing so. The third condition fails. Yet if, on this particular morning, a lorry ran a red light and missed her by inches, we should wish to say that she was lucky to arrive safely. The improbability of the outcome to her is not what makes it lucky; rather, it is the proximity to a bad alternative that confers the character of luck.
missing_condition: The definition fails to capture the modal dimension of luck — the closeness of the actual outcome to a significantly worse alternative — by focusing on the improbability of the event itself rather than on how easily things might have gone otherwise.
passage: A plausible analysis of luck holds that an event is a matter of luck for a person if and only if it is significant to her, is not the result of her deliberate planning, and is improbable. This analysis captures many familiar cases of good fortune: the lottery winner did not plan her win, the outcome was enormously improbable, and it is certainly significant to her welfare. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the most ordinary circumstances. Consider a woman who walks to work along her usual route and arrives safely, as she does on nearly every working day. Her safe arrival is plainly significant to her. It is not the result of any special planning — she merely follows her habitual path. But it is not improbable; indeed, it is overwhelmingly probable, and she has every reasonable expectation that she will arrive without incident.
