The third condition of the definition is not satisfied. Yet suppose that, on this particular morning, a lorry ran a red light at a crossing she had just passed, missing her by a matter of inches. We should wish to say, and she would certainly feel, that she was lucky to arrive safely. One might observe that what makes her arrival a matter of luck is not its improbability but the closeness of the actual outcome to a very different and far worse one. The lorry nearly struck her; a second's difference in her walking pace would have placed her in its path. The definition, by requiring improbability, cannot accommodate this modal sense of luck — the sense in which one is lucky when things might very easily have gone badly.

definition: A dwelling D is a home for a person S if and only if (i) S resides in D on a regular and continuing basis, (ii) S has a legal right to occupy D, and (iii) S regards D as her primary place of habitation.
conditions: S resides in D on a regular and continuing basis
S has a legal right to occupy D
S regards D as her primary place of habitation
counterexample: Consider a vagrant who, for want of any other shelter, sleeps each night beneath a particular railway bridge. He resides there on a regular and continuing basis; he regards the spot as his primary place of habitation — the place where he keeps his few possessions, to which he returns each evening. But he has no legal right to occupy the space. The second condition fails. Yet one might observe that it would be callous and philosophically confused to deny that the railway bridge is his home. He calls it home; his behaviour toward it exhibits all the marks of dwelling — habitual return, storage of possessions, a sense of territorial familiarity. The definition, by requiring legal right, excludes those whose homelessness is defined precisely by their lack of such a right, and thereby imports a legal concept into what is essentially a human and phenomenological one.
missing_condition: The definition makes legal right essential to the concept of home, but fails to recognise that the concept is fundamentally phenomenological — a matter of habitual dwelling, attachment, and subjective identification — and may obtain in the absence of any legal entitlement.
passage: One might define a home as a dwelling in which a person resides regularly, to which she has a legal right, and which she regards as her primary habitation. This definition captures the comfortable case: a family that lives in a house they own, returning to it daily, regarding it as the centre of their domestic life. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is the situation of the homeless person who has nonetheless found a place of habitual shelter. Consider a vagrant who sleeps each night beneath a particular railway bridge. He returns to this spot every evening; he keeps his few possessions there; he has established a routine of habitation around it. He resides there regularly, and he regards it as his primary place — indeed his only place — of habitation. But he has no legal right to occupy the space beneath the bridge. The local authority could move him on at any time. The second condition of the definition therefore fails. Yet it appears that we should not deny that the railway bridge is, for this man, his home. It is the place to which he returns, the place where he feels he belongs, the place from which he ventures out and to which he comes back. One might observe that the concept of home is at bottom a phenomenological one — it concerns a person's relation of dwelling, attachment, and habitual return to a place — and that importing a legal requirement excludes the very cases in which the concept of home is most poignant and most needed.

definition: A relationship between persons A and B is a friendship if and only if (i) A and B have mutual affection for each other, (ii) A and B voluntarily seek each other's company, and (iii) A and B have substantial shared interests or activities.
conditions: A and B have mutual affection for each other
A and B voluntarily seek each other's company
A and B have substantial shared interests or activities
counterexample: Consider two soldiers who serve together in a prolonged military campaign. They develop a deep bond forged by shared danger, mutual dependence, and the intimacy of life in the field. Each would readily risk his life for the other. Yet their temperaments, interests, and backgrounds are entirely different: one is a scholar of classics, the other a mechanic with no interest in books. They share no activities outside the demands of military service. The third condition fails. Yet one might observe that the bond between these men is as genuine a friendship as any that exists, and indeed possesses a depth and intensity that friendships founded on shared hobbies rarely achieve.
missing_condition: The definition requires shared interests or activities, but fails to recognise that friendship may be grounded in shared experience, mutual dependence, or reciprocal loyalty rather than in common pursuits, and that some of the deepest friendships arise precisely where interests diverge.
passage: One might analyse friendship as a relationship characterised by mutual affection, voluntary companionship, and substantial shared interests or activities. This analysis captures many familiar friendships: two colleagues who enjoy each other's company, share a passion for hillwalking, and meet regularly to pursue it. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is the bond formed between comrades in arms. Consider two soldiers who serve together through a prolonged and dangerous campaign. They develop a bond of extraordinary depth — each trusts the other with his life, each is willing to sacrifice his own safety for the other's welfare. Their mutual affection is beyond question. And they seek each other's company, though the circumstances of military service mean that this companionship is not always voluntary in the ordinary sense. But they share no interests or activities outside the demands of their service. One is a scholar, the other a mechanic; their temperaments are different, their backgrounds are different, their peacetime pursuits have nothing in common. The third condition of the definition fails. Yet it appears that the relationship between these men is a friendship of the most genuine and profound kind. One might observe that friendship may be grounded not in shared interests but in shared experience — in the mutual dependence, trust, and loyalty that arise when two people face danger together. The definition, by requiring shared interests, cannot accommodate friendships of this important and distinctive type.

definition: An occasion of eating E is a meal if and only if (i) E involves the consumption of a quantity of food sufficient to constitute nourishment, (ii) E takes place at a socially recognised time — breakfast, luncheon, dinner, or supper, and (iii) E is consumed in a seated position at a table or similar surface.
conditions: E involves sufficient food to constitute nourishment
E takes place at a socially recognised mealtime
E is consumed seated at a table or similar surface
counterexample: Consider a labourer who eats his sandwiches standing in a field at half past twelve. He consumes a substantial quantity of food — bread, cheese, ham, an apple — amounting to genuine nourishment. He eats at lunchtime, a socially recognised mealtime. But he eats standing up, with no table, holding his food in his hands. The third condition fails. Yet it would be absurd to deny that the labourer is eating his meal. He calls it lunch; his colleagues call it lunch; his employer allows him a lunch break specifically for this purpose.
missing_condition: The definition imposes a requirement of formal setting — a seated position at a table — that belongs to the customs of the middle classes and does not capture the concept as it is actually deployed across different social contexts and working conditions.
passage: A natural characterisation of a meal holds that it is an occasion of eating that involves sufficient food for nourishment, takes place at a socially recognised time, and is consumed in a seated position at a table. This characterisation captures the formal dinner and the family breakfast admirably. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the eating habits of working people. A labourer who takes his lunch in a field at midday consumes a substantial quantity of food — enough to sustain him through the afternoon's work. He eats at the recognised hour of lunchtime. But he eats standing, holding his sandwiches in his hands, with no table in sight. The third condition of the definition is not satisfied. One might observe, however, that the labourer is plainly eating a meal. His employer grants him a break for this very purpose; his colleagues describe the occasion as lunch; he himself would be puzzled by the suggestion that he was not eating a meal. The definition, by requiring a table and a seated position, imports a class-specific norm of dining into a concept that is in fact far more flexible. Nourishment taken at a recognised time, in a quantity sufficient to sustain the body, constitutes a meal regardless of the formality of the setting.

definition: A person S owns an object O if and only if (i) S acquired O through a legitimate process — purchase, production, gift, or inheritance, (ii) S has the right to use O as she sees fit, and (iii) S has the right to exclude others from the use of O.
conditions: S acquired O through a legitimate process
S has the right to use O as she sees fit
S has the right to exclude others from its use
counterexample: Consider a farmer who owns a field under the ordinary understanding of ownership. She acquired it by purchase, she has the right to exclude trespassers, and she farms it as she sees fit. But planning regulations restrict her from building a house upon it, environmental regulations forbid her from draining a wetland portion, and a public footpath crosses the corner, which she cannot obstruct. The second condition — the right to use the field as she sees fit — fails in multiple respects. Yet it would be absurd to deny that she owns the field. Ownership does not require unlimited discretion in use; it is compatible with extensive regulation and limitation.
missing_condition: The definition requires unrestricted use-rights, but fails to recognise that ownership in practice is always subject to legal, social, and moral limitations, and that the concept must accommodate the difference between owning something and having absolute dominion over it.
passage: One might define ownership as the condition in which a person has acquired an object through a legitimate process, has the right to use it as she sees fit, and has the right to exclude others from its use. This definition captures the ideal case: a person who buys a tool, uses it for whatever purpose she chooses, and keeps it locked in her shed. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is the ownership of land. Consider a farmer who purchases a field. She has acquired it through a paradigmatically legitimate process. She can exclude trespassers — the law is clear on this point. But her right to use the field as she sees fit is hedged about with restrictions of every kind. Planning regulations forbid her from erecting buildings without permission. Environmental regulations prohibit her from draining a wetland area that supports protected species. A public footpath crosses the corner of the field, and she cannot obstruct it. One might observe that the second condition of the definition is not satisfied, for the farmer manifestly cannot use the field as she sees fit. Yet she owns the field; this is not in dispute. She pays taxes on it, she can sell it, she can bequeath it to her children. The definition, by requiring an unrestricted right of use, sets the bar too high. Ownership in any actual legal system is always bounded by regulations, and a definition that demands unlimited discretion confuses ownership with absolute dominion.

definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is governed by a system of explicit rules, (ii) A involves competition between two or more participants, and (iii) A is pursued for the sake of amusement rather than material gain.
conditions: A is governed by a system of explicit rules
A involves competition between two or more participants
A is pursued for the sake of amusement rather than material gain
counterexample: Consider a child playing at tossing a ball into the air and catching it. We should naturally call this a game. Yet the activity is governed by no explicit rules — the child invents and modifies the activity as it proceeds. There is no competition, for the child plays alone. And while the child is amused, the absence of the other two conditions does not prevent us from applying the word 'game.' All three conditions fail, and yet the application of the concept seems correct.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate solitary, non-competitive, and rule-free activities that are nonetheless naturally and correctly described as games, suggesting that the concept cannot be captured by a conjunction of necessary conditions.
passage: One might attempt to define a game as an activity governed by explicit rules, involving competition between participants, and pursued for amusement rather than material gain. This definition captures many paradigmatic games — chess, football, card games — with reasonable fidelity. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be found in the simplest forms of play. Consider a child who tosses a ball into the air and catches it, repeating the action with evident delight. We should naturally say that the child is playing a game. Yet the activity is governed by no explicit rules; the child invents and modifies the game as it pleases, sometimes tossing higher, sometimes lower, sometimes with the left hand, sometimes with the right. There is no competition, for the child plays alone. One might observe that the absence of rules and competition does not in the least incline us to deny that this is a game. It appears that the concept of a game is more various and less susceptible to strict definition than the proposal suggests, and that the definition, by insisting upon rules and competition, excludes much of what falls naturally under the concept.

definition: An artefact X is a chair if and only if (i) X has a seat, a back, and a base that supports it above the ground, (ii) X is designed for a single person to sit upon, and (iii) X is a moveable piece of furniture.
conditions: X has a seat, a back, and a base that supports it above the ground
X is designed for a single person to sit upon
X is a moveable piece of furniture
counterexample: Consider a large flat rock in a garden, positioned at a comfortable height and used daily by the owner as a place to sit. The rock has a roughly flat surface that functions as a seat, the garden wall behind it serves as a back of sorts, and it is elevated above the ground. It accommodates a single sitter. Yet it was not designed at all — it is a natural object — and it is not a piece of furniture, let alone a moveable one. Nevertheless, one might observe that the owner and her visitors consistently refer to it as 'my chair in the garden.'
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the fact that ordinary usage extends the concept of a chair to objects that are neither designed artefacts nor pieces of furniture, provided they serve the functional role of a chair in a sustained and recognised manner.
passage: A natural definition of a chair holds that it is an artefact with a seat, a back, and a supporting base, designed for a single person to sit upon, and constituting a moveable piece of furniture. This definition captures the prototypical chair with some precision: the kitchen chair, the office chair, the dining chair all conform to this description. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in everyday life. Consider a large flat rock in a garden, situated at a comfortable sitting height, with the garden wall rising behind it to provide support for the sitter's back. The owner of the garden sits on this rock every morning with her tea and refers to it, without the least affectation, as her chair. Visitors do the same. The rock serves every functional purpose of a chair. Yet it was not designed — it is a natural object, placed where it is by geological happenstance. And it is not a piece of furniture, moveable or otherwise. One might observe that ordinary usage does not confine the concept of a chair to designed artefacts; the concept extends to any object that reliably serves the sitting function in a recognised and sustained manner. It appears that the definition, by insisting upon design and artefactuality, fails to capture the full breadth of the concept as it operates in ordinary language.

definition: An object O is a work of art if and only if (i) O is produced by a human agent with the intention of creating something aesthetically valuable, (ii) O exhibits formal properties — such as harmony, balance, or expressive power — that reward aesthetic contemplation, and (iii) O belongs to a recognised artistic medium such as painting, sculpture, music, or literature.
conditions: O is produced by a human agent with the intention of creating something aesthetically valuable
O exhibits formal properties — such as harmony, balance, or expressive power — that reward aesthetic contemplation
O belongs to a recognised artistic medium such as painting, sculpture, music, or literature
counterexample: Consider Marcel Duchamp's 'Fountain' — a mass-produced porcelain urinal submitted to an exhibition in 1917. Duchamp did not produce the urinal; it was manufactured industrially. It is not obvious that the object exhibits harmony, balance, or expressive power in the traditional sense. And its medium — a plumbing fixture — is not a recognised artistic medium. Yet 'Fountain' is widely regarded as one of the most significant works of art of the twentieth century. All three conditions fail, and yet the status of the object as art is scarcely in doubt.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for works of art that achieve their status through institutional recognition, conceptual provocation, or the recontextualisation of ordinary objects, rather than through the traditional qualities of aesthetic production.
passage: One might propose that a work of art is an object produced with aesthetic intention, exhibiting formal properties that reward contemplation, and belonging to a recognised artistic medium. This definition captures the great majority of the works that populate our galleries and concert halls: the paintings of Vermeer, the symphonies of Beethoven, the novels of Austen all satisfy it without difficulty. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the history of twentieth-century art. Consider the object submitted by Marcel Duchamp to the Society of Independent Artists in 1917 under the title 'Fountain.' The object was a mass-produced porcelain urinal, purchased from a plumbing supplier and signed with a pseudonym. Duchamp did not produce it; he selected it. It does not obviously exhibit harmony, balance, or expressive power. And a urinal is not a recognised artistic medium. All three conditions fail. Yet 'Fountain' is today regarded as one of the most consequential works of art of the twentieth century — it challenged the very presuppositions that the definition encodes. It appears that the definition, by confining art to the intentional production of aesthetically rewarding objects in traditional media, cannot accommodate works that achieve artistic significance through conceptual provocation and the recontextualisation of the ordinary.

definition: A statement S uttered by a person P is a lie if and only if (i) P asserts S, (ii) S is false, and (iii) P believes that S is false at the time of assertion.
conditions: P asserts S
S is false
P believes that S is false at the time of assertion
counterexample: Consider a confidence trickster who tells his victim, 'There is a gold mine in the Yukon that will make you rich.' Suppose that, entirely by coincidence, there is indeed a gold mine in the Yukon that would make the victim rich were she to invest. The statement is true. The trickster believes it to be false — he intends to deceive. He asserts it sincerely. The second condition fails: the statement is true. Yet one might observe that the trickster has lied, for his intention was wholly deceptive and his disregard for the truth was complete.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P's intention to deceive be sufficient for lying regardless of the accidental truth of the statement, so that a speaker who asserts what he believes to be false with deceptive intent is not classified as a liar when the statement turns out, by sheer chance, to be true.
passage: It is natural to define a lie as a false statement asserted by someone who believes it to be false. This definition captures the core of our ordinary concept: the liar says what is not so, and does so in the awareness that it is not so. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition may be found in the practices of deception. Consider a confidence trickster who tells his victim that there is a gold mine in the Yukon that will make her rich. The trickster has no information about Yukon gold mines; he fabricates the claim entirely. He asserts it with every appearance of sincerity. He believes it to be false. Yet suppose that, by extraordinary coincidence, there is in fact such a gold mine, and it would indeed make the victim rich. The statement is true. The second condition of the definition therefore fails. Nevertheless, one might observe that the trickster has lied — his intention was to deceive, his disregard for the truth was absolute, and the accidental truth of his statement does nothing to redeem his moral standing. It appears that the definition, by requiring that the statement actually be false, conflates lying with unsuccessful assertion and cannot accommodate cases in which the liar's intent is fully deceptive but the world happens to cooperate.

definition: An utterance U by a person P constitutes a promise if and only if (i) P expresses an intention to perform a future action A, (ii) P intends the utterance to create an obligation upon herself to perform A, and (iii) the person to whom U is addressed understands U as creating such an obligation.
conditions: P expresses an intention to perform a future action A
P intends the utterance to create an obligation upon herself to perform A
The person to whom U is addressed understands U as creating such an obligation
counterexample: Consider a politician who, at a public rally, declares, 'I promise to abolish all taxes within my first year in office.' The audience understands this as creating an obligation. The politician utters it with every outward mark of sincerity. Yet the politician privately has no intention whatever of abolishing taxes and makes the utterance solely for its rhetorical effect. All three conditions appear to be met if we take the politician's outward expression at face value. Yet one might observe that no genuine promise has been made, for the speaker's intention is wholly insincere.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P sincerely intends to perform A at the time of promising, so that an insincere or manipulative expression of intention — one that mimics the form of a promise without the accompanying commitment — may nonetheless qualify as a genuine promise.
passage: One might propose that a promise is an utterance in which the speaker expresses an intention to perform a future action, intends the utterance to create an obligation, and is understood by the addressee as doing so. This characterisation captures the communicative and normative structure of promising: a promise is not merely a prediction but a self-imposed obligation, and it requires the addressee's uptake. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal arises in the sphere of political rhetoric. Consider a politician who declares at a rally, 'I promise to abolish all taxes within my first year in office.' The audience understands this as a binding commitment and responds with enthusiasm. The utterance has all the outward marks of a promise. But suppose the politician has no intention whatever of abolishing taxes — the declaration is made purely for its electoral effect. If we take the politician's words at face value, all three conditions appear to be satisfied: an intention to act is expressed, the utterance is understood as creating an obligation, and the audience accepts it as such. Yet one might observe that no genuine promise has been made. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the expressed and perceived intention rather than to the speaker's actual state of mind, cannot distinguish sincere promises from their insincere counterfeits.

definition: An object X is a vehicle if and only if (i) X is a physical conveyance capable of transporting persons or goods from one location to another, (ii) X is propelled by an engine, motor, or other mechanical means, and (iii) X is designed to travel on roads, rails, water, or through the air.
conditions: X is a physical conveyance capable of transporting persons or goods from one location to another
X is propelled by an engine, motor, or other mechanical means
X is designed to travel on roads, rails, water, or through the air
counterexample: Consider a bicycle. A bicycle is a physical conveyance that transports a person from one place to another. It is designed to travel on roads. These conditions are satisfied. Yet a bicycle is not propelled by an engine, motor, or mechanical means in the relevant sense — it is propelled by the rider's muscular effort through a system of pedals and gears. Nevertheless, bicycles are classified as vehicles by traffic regulations in most jurisdictions and are called vehicles in ordinary speech.
missing_condition: The definition fails to include conveyances propelled by human muscular power, so that human-powered devices such as bicycles and rowing boats — which are ordinarily and legally classified as vehicles — are excluded.
passage: A natural definition of a vehicle holds that it is a physical conveyance propelled by engine or motor and designed to travel on roads, rails, water, or through the air. This definition captures motor cars, trains, ships, and aeroplanes with reasonable precision. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be found on any public road. Consider a bicycle. A bicycle is a physical conveyance capable of transporting a person from one location to another. It is designed to travel on roads. In these respects it satisfies the first and third conditions of the definition. Yet it is propelled not by an engine or motor but by the rider's muscular effort, transmitted through a system of pedals, chains, and gears. The second condition fails. Nevertheless, one might observe that bicycles are classified as vehicles by traffic legislation in virtually every jurisdiction, and ordinary speakers of English would unhesitatingly describe a bicycle as a vehicle. It appears that the definition, by requiring mechanical propulsion, draws the boundary of the concept too narrowly and excludes a large class of conveyances that both ordinary usage and legal practice recognise as vehicles.

definition: An object X is a tool if and only if (i) X is a physical artefact, (ii) X is used by a human agent to perform a task that would be more difficult or impossible without it, and (iii) X has been designed or manufactured for the purpose of performing that task.
conditions: X is a physical artefact
X is used by a human agent to perform a task that would be more difficult or impossible without it
X has been designed or manufactured for the purpose of performing that task
counterexample: Consider a chimpanzee that strips the leaves from a twig and inserts it into a termite mound to extract termites. The modified twig functions as a tool: it enables the chimpanzee to perform a task — extracting termites — that would be impossible without it. The twig has been deliberately modified for the purpose. Yet the chimpanzee is not a human agent, and the twig is not, in the strict sense, a manufactured artefact. The first and third conditions are only dubiously satisfied. Yet primatologists and ordinary observers alike describe the twig as a tool.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for tool use by non-human agents and for natural objects that are selected or modified for a purpose without being manufactured in the human sense, so that a broad and well-attested category of tool use is excluded.
passage: One might define a tool as a physical artefact designed for a purpose and used by a human agent to perform a task that would otherwise be more difficult. This definition captures hammers, screwdrivers, saws, and the implements of every trade. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency has been extensively documented in the field of ethology. Consider a chimpanzee that selects a twig, strips it of leaves, and inserts it into a termite mound to extract termites. The modified twig enables the chimpanzee to perform a task — the extraction of food — that would be impossible without it. The twig has been deliberately selected and modified for this purpose. Yet the chimpanzee is not a human agent, and the stripped twig is not, in any ordinary sense, a manufactured artefact. One might observe that primatologists describe this behaviour as tool use without the slightest hesitation, and that ordinary observers, shown a film of the behaviour, would readily agree. It appears that the definition, by restricting tool use to human agents and manufactured artefacts, reflects an anthropocentric prejudice rather than the actual boundaries of the concept, and that a more adequate characterisation must accommodate the purposeful use of objects by non-human animals.

definition: A system of communication L is a language if and only if (i) L employs a finite set of discrete symbols, (ii) L combines those symbols according to a system of grammatical rules, and (iii) the symbols of L bear their meanings by convention rather than by natural resemblance.
conditions: L employs a finite set of discrete symbols
L combines those symbols according to a system of grammatical rules
The symbols of L bear their meanings by convention rather than by natural resemblance
counterexample: Consider the communication system of honeybees, in which a forager performs a waggle dance to indicate the direction and distance of a food source. The dance employs a finite repertoire of discrete movements. These movements are combined in a structured manner — the angle of the dance indicates direction; its duration indicates distance. And the relation between movement and meaning is not one of natural resemblance. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet one might observe that we should hesitate to call the bee dance a language, for the bees cannot use their system to communicate about anything other than the location of food — the system lacks the open-ended generativity of human language.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that L possess the property of open-ended productivity — the capacity to generate an unbounded number of novel meaningful expressions — so that closed, finite communication systems may qualify as languages.
passage: A natural definition of language holds that it is a system of communication employing finite discrete symbols, combined according to grammatical rules, with meanings borne by convention. This definition captures the salient features of English, French, Mandarin, and every other human tongue. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be found in the natural world. The honeybee waggle dance is a system of communication that employs a finite set of discrete movements — waggles, turns, and vibrations. These movements are combined in a structured manner: the angle of the waggle run relative to the vertical indicates the direction of the food source relative to the sun; the duration of the waggle indicates the distance. The relation between movement and meaning is conventional in the sense that it is not one of natural resemblance — the dance does not look like the food source. All three conditions of the definition appear to be satisfied. Yet one might observe that the bee dance is not a language in any full sense of the word. It can communicate only about the location of food; it cannot express a wish, pose a question, or refer to the past. It appears that the definition, by requiring only symbols, grammar, and conventionality, cannot distinguish genuinely linguistic systems — with their open-ended capacity to generate novel expressions on any subject — from closed communication systems of fixed and limited scope.

definition: An activity A is artistic if and only if (i) A involves the deliberate arrangement of sensory materials — sounds, colours, shapes, words — into a structured form, (ii) the product of A is intended to be appreciated for its aesthetic qualities, and (iii) the practitioner of A possesses a degree of technical skill in the relevant medium.
conditions: A involves the deliberate arrangement of sensory materials — sounds, colours, shapes, words — into a structured form
The product of A is intended to be appreciated for its aesthetic qualities
The practitioner of A possesses a degree of technical skill in the relevant medium
counterexample: Consider the art brut or outsider art produced by persons with no formal training — for example, a patient in a psychiatric hospital who covers the walls of his room with elaborate, haunting drawings. The drawings involve the deliberate arrangement of visual materials into structured forms. They are, at least on a natural reading, intended for some form of appreciation. Yet the practitioner possesses no technical skill in the conventional sense — he has never studied drawing and may be unaware of the conventions of the medium. Nevertheless, the drawings have been exhibited in major galleries and are regarded by critics as possessing genuine artistic merit.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for artistic activities in which the absence of conventional technical skill is itself a source of aesthetic power, so that untrained or naive practitioners are excluded even when their work is of widely recognised artistic value.
passage: One might propose that an activity is artistic if it involves the deliberate arrangement of sensory materials into structured form, if its product is intended for aesthetic appreciation, and if the practitioner possesses technical skill in the medium. This characterisation captures the practice of the trained painter, the skilled musician, and the accomplished novelist. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the phenomenon of art brut or outsider art. Consider a patient in a psychiatric hospital who, without any formal training in drawing, covers the walls of his room with elaborate and haunting images — intricate patterns, distorted figures, densely layered compositions. The drawings involve the deliberate arrangement of visual materials. They are produced with apparent intentionality. Yet the patient has never studied art and possesses no technical skill in the conventional sense. One might observe that the absence of skill is, in this case, inseparable from the aesthetic power of the work — the images possess a raw directness that trained technique might extinguish. Major galleries have exhibited such works, and critics have recognised their genuine artistic merit. It appears that the definition, by requiring technical skill, excludes a category of artistic production whose value depends precisely upon the absence of conventional training.

definition: An object X qualifies as a hand tool if and only if (i) X is held and operated by the human hand, (ii) X amplifies or redirects the force applied by the user, and (iii) X is rigid and maintains its shape during use.
conditions: X is held and operated by the human hand
X amplifies or redirects the force applied by the user
X is rigid and maintains its shape during use
counterexample: Consider a paintbrush. A paintbrush is held and operated by the hand. Yet it does not amplify or redirect force in any significant sense — the force required to apply paint to a surface is negligible, and the brush functions primarily to distribute material rather than to multiply effort. Moreover, the bristles of the brush are deliberately flexible and change shape during use. The second and third conditions both fail. Yet one might observe that the paintbrush is, by any ordinary reckoning, a hand tool.
missing_condition: The definition fails to include tools whose function is not the amplification of force but the controlled distribution, application, or manipulation of materials, so that a large class of hand tools — brushes, pens, spatulas — is excluded.
passage: A natural characterisation of a hand tool holds that it is a rigid object held in the hand that amplifies or redirects the user's force. This captures the hammer, the lever, the screwdriver, and the wrench — the paradigmatic tools of the workshop. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the humbler implements of everyday work. A paintbrush is held and operated by the hand. No one would deny that it is a hand tool. Yet it does not amplify or redirect force: the force required to apply paint to a surface is slight, and the brush serves not to multiply effort but to distribute pigment in a controlled manner. Furthermore, the bristles of the brush are deliberately flexible — they splay and bend during each stroke and return to shape afterwards. The brush does not maintain rigid form during use. Both the second and third conditions of the definition fail. One might observe that the paintbrush is a tool whose function is not mechanical advantage but material distribution, and that its flexibility is essential to that function. It appears that the definition, by conceiving of tools in terms of force amplification and rigidity, reflects the bias of the workshop and cannot accommodate the broad range of implements whose function is the controlled manipulation of materials.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) the speaker S expresses an intention to perform some future act A, (ii) S intends that U place S under an obligation to perform A, and (iii) the hearer H understands U as placing S under such an obligation.
conditions: The speaker expresses an intention to perform a future act
The speaker intends the utterance to create an obligation
The hearer understands the utterance as creating an obligation
counterexample: Consider a stage actor who, in the course of a theatrical performance, solemnly declares to another character: 'I promise to return your fortune by sunrise.' The actor expresses an intention to perform a future act. The audience and the fellow actor understand this as a promise within the world of the play. Yet no one — neither the actor nor the audience — supposes that the actor has thereby placed himself under a genuine obligation to return anyone's fortune. All three conditions appear to be met in form, yet no promise has been made.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the utterance be made sincerely and in earnest rather than in a context — such as fiction, play-acting, or ceremonial recitation — where the conventions governing speech suspend the normal force of promissory language.
passage: One might analyse the concept of a promise as an utterance in which the speaker expresses an intention to perform a future act, intends thereby to place himself under an obligation, and is understood by the hearer as having done so. This analysis captures the ordinary case admirably: when one says 'I promise to meet you at three o'clock,' one expresses an intention, one means to bind oneself, and the hearer takes the utterance as binding. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is provided by the theatre. Suppose an actor upon the stage declares, in a tone of the utmost gravity, 'I promise to restore your inheritance before the sun rises.' The words express an intention to perform a future act. Within the conventions of the performance, the actor intends the audience to understand this as a promise. And the audience does so understand it — they recognise the dramatic significance of the moment. Yet no one supposes that the actor has made a genuine promise. He is not obligated to restore anyone's inheritance. The conventions of theatrical performance suspend the normal force of promissory language; the words are spoken in earnest only within the fiction. The definition, by requiring only expressed intention, intended obligation, and hearer understanding, cannot distinguish a genuine promise from a merely enacted one. A more adequate account would need to include a sincerity condition specifying that the utterance be made outside contexts in which the conventions of speech are suspended.

definition: A transfer of an object O from person A to person B constitutes a gift if and only if (i) A voluntarily relinquishes possession of O to B, (ii) A does not receive or expect any material return from B, and (iii) A intends the transfer to benefit B.
conditions: A voluntarily relinquishes possession to B
A does not receive or expect material return
A intends the transfer to benefit B
counterexample: Consider a landowner who makes a conspicuous donation of a large sum to a charitable institution. He voluntarily relinquishes the money. He receives no material return. He intends the transfer to benefit the institution. All three conditions are met. Yet his motive is not generosity but the desire to secure a favourable reputation in the community, which will in turn advance his commercial interests. The transfer functions as an investment in social standing rather than as a genuine gift. One might hesitate to call it a gift in the fullest sense, even though the definition admits it.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the donor's primary motive be generosity or benevolence rather than self-interested calculation, so that transfers made for strategic advantage satisfy all the stated conditions while lacking the spirit that distinguishes a genuine gift from a calculated benefaction.
passage: One might propose that a gift is a voluntary transfer of an object from one person to another, made without expectation of material return and with the intention of benefiting the recipient. This analysis captures the paradigmatic cases: a birthday present, a charitable donation given from the heart, a spontaneous act of generosity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is furnished by the phenomenon of strategic philanthropy. Suppose a wealthy landowner makes a conspicuous donation to a charitable institution. He parts with a considerable sum voluntarily. He receives nothing material in return. And he intends the donation to benefit the institution. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet his primary motive is not generosity but the desire to cultivate a reputation for benevolence, which he calculates will advance his commercial interests and secure him favourable terms in future dealings. The donation is, in effect, an investment in social capital. One might observe that while the definition correctly identifies the external features of a gift — voluntary transfer, absence of material return, beneficial intent — it cannot penetrate to the spirit of the transaction. A more adequate account would need to attend to the donor's motive, requiring that the transfer be animated by generosity or benevolence rather than by strategic self-interest.

definition: Two persons A and B are friends if and only if (i) A and B have positive feelings of affection towards one another, (ii) A and B voluntarily spend time in one another's company, and (iii) A and B are disposed to assist one another in times of difficulty.
conditions: A and B have mutual positive feelings of affection
A and B voluntarily spend time together
A and B are disposed to assist one another in difficulty
counterexample: Consider two soldiers in the same regiment who have fought side by side through a long campaign. They have developed deep mutual affection. They spend time together willingly when circumstances allow. Each is prepared to risk his life for the other. All three conditions are fully satisfied. Yet the soldiers may have been assigned to the same unit by orders they could not refuse, and the dangers they share create a bond quite unlike ordinary friendship — a bond forged by shared extremity, institutional obligation, and the peculiar intimacy of mortal peril. When the war ends and the institutional context dissolves, the bond may evaporate entirely, suggesting it was not friendship but comradeship — a distinct relation with its own character.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish friendship from other affectionate and mutually supportive relations — such as comradeship, collegial loyalty, or institutional solidarity — that arise from shared circumstances rather than from a voluntary mutual selection of one another as individuals valued for their own sake.
passage: One might analyse friendship as a relation between two persons who feel mutual affection, voluntarily spend time together, and are disposed to assist one another in difficulty. This analysis captures the essential warmth of friendship — the fondness, the chosen company, the readiness to help. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is furnished by the bond between soldiers in wartime. Consider two men assigned to the same regiment who fight side by side through a long and harrowing campaign. They develop deep mutual affection born of shared suffering. They spend their leisure hours together willingly. Each stands ready to lay down his life for the other. All three conditions are met in the most emphatic degree. Yet there is something about this bond that differs from ordinary friendship. The men did not choose one another; they were thrown together by military orders. Their affection was forged not by the gentle process of mutual discovery that characterises friendship but by the extremity of shared peril. When the war ends and the men return to civilian life, the bond may dissolve entirely, as though it could not survive outside the conditions that produced it. One might say that what obtained between them was comradeship rather than friendship — a relation that resembles friendship in its affective warmth and mutual support but differs in its dependence on institutional context and shared extremity.

definition: A statement S made by a speaker A to a hearer B is a lie if and only if (i) A asserts S, (ii) S is false, and (iii) A believes S to be false at the time of assertion.
conditions: A asserts S
S is false
A believes S to be false
counterexample: Consider a man who, wishing to deceive his wife about his whereabouts, tells her that he spent the afternoon at the library. He believes this to be false, as he intended to spend the afternoon elsewhere. Yet by a remarkable coincidence, he did in fact visit the library — he stopped in briefly on his way, having forgotten. The statement is true. He believes it to be false. All conditions except the second are met; the statement is not, in fact, a lie, since what he said happened to be true. Yet his intent was plainly dishonest, and one might feel that something very close to lying has occurred.
passage: One might define a lie as an assertion that is false and that the speaker believes to be false. This captures the central cases of lying with admirable economy. Yet the interplay between truth, falsity, and the speaker's beliefs yields cases that press upon the boundaries of this analysis. Consider a man who wishes to deceive his wife about how he spent the afternoon. He tells her he was at the library, believing this to be false — his plan had been to visit a friend instead. But by a coincidence he has forgotten, he did in fact stop at the library briefly on his way. The statement turns out to be true. The first condition is met — he made an assertion. The third is met — he believed the statement to be false. But the second condition fails — the statement is, as it happens, true. The definition correctly classifies this as not a lie, for the utterance is true. Yet one might observe that the man's conduct possesses all the moral features we associate with lying — the intent to deceive, the deliberate assertion of what one takes to be false, the attempt to create a false impression. The definition identifies the logical structure of lying accurately, but in doing so it reveals that our moral assessment of dishonest speech may track the speaker's intent rather than the truth-value of the statement. Whether this is a deficiency in the definition or a discovery about the concept is itself a question worth considering.

definition: A person P owns an object O if and only if (i) P has the right to use O as P sees fit, (ii) P has the right to exclude others from using O, and (iii) P acquired O through a legitimate process of transfer or creation.
conditions: P has the right to use O as P sees fit
P has the right to exclude others from O
P acquired O through legitimate transfer or creation
counterexample: Consider a tenant farmer who leases a tract of land for ninety-nine years. He has the right to use the land as he sees fit — to plough it, plant it, graze livestock upon it, or leave it fallow. He has the right to exclude others from the land — trespassers may be removed. And the lease was acquired through a legitimate process of agreement. All three conditions are met. Yet the farmer does not own the land; he is a leaseholder, and when the lease expires the land reverts to the landlord. Ownership requires something more than the rights the definition specifies.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P's rights over O be permanent and unconditional — that they do not expire at a fixed date or depend upon the continuing consent of another party — so that long-term but terminable rights of use and exclusion are mistaken for ownership.
passage: One might propose that a person owns an object if he has the right to use it as he pleases, the right to exclude others from it, and acquired it through a legitimate process. This analysis captures the central features of ownership: the freedom of use, the power of exclusion, and the legitimacy of acquisition. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is furnished by the institution of the long lease. Consider a tenant farmer who holds a ninety-nine-year lease on a tract of productive land. He may use the land as he sees fit — he may plough it, plant it, let it lie fallow, or graze his cattle upon it. He may exclude trespassers. And the lease was acquired through a perfectly legitimate process of agreement with the landlord. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the farmer does not own the land. When the lease expires — or in certain circumstances even before — the land reverts to the landlord. The farmer's rights, though extensive and long-lasting, are temporary and conditional. Ownership, it appears, involves a permanence and unconditionality that the definition fails to capture. A more adequate account would need to distinguish the full bundle of rights that constitutes ownership from the partial and temporary bundle that constitutes a lease, even when the two are difficult to tell apart in practice.

definition: An activity A counts as work if and only if (i) A requires sustained effort, (ii) A is directed towards a productive end, and (iii) A is performed in exchange for remuneration.
conditions: A requires sustained effort
A is directed towards a productive end
A is performed in exchange for remuneration
counterexample: Consider a woman who tends a large garden, cultivating vegetables, pruning fruit trees, and maintaining flower beds, entirely for her own satisfaction and for the benefit of her household. The activity requires sustained physical and mental effort. It is directed towards a productive end — the garden yields food and beauty. But she receives no remuneration whatever. Yet the activity seems plainly to be work: it is laborious, productive, and purposeful. The definition, by requiring remuneration, excludes it.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate unpaid labour — domestic work, volunteer service, subsistence farming — by treating remuneration as a necessary condition of work rather than as a frequent but dispensable accompaniment.
passage: One might analyse the concept of work as an activity requiring sustained effort, directed towards a productive end, and performed in exchange for remuneration. This analysis captures the paradigmatic case of paid employment: the factory worker, the office clerk, the professional. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by domestic labour. Consider a woman who devotes her days to tending a large garden — digging beds, planting seeds, pruning fruit trees, weeding, watering, and harvesting. The activity requires sustained and often strenuous effort. It is directed towards a manifestly productive end: the garden yields food for the household and beauty for the neighbourhood. Yet she receives no payment for this labour. No wage is offered; no fee is charged. The definition, applied strictly, excludes her activity from the category of work. Yet it would be remarkable to deny that what she does is work. The gardener labours as diligently as any employee, produces tangible results, and expends effort that would command a wage if performed in a commercial setting. It appears that remuneration, while a common feature of work, is not essential to it. A more adequate definition would need to treat payment as a frequent accompaniment rather than a necessary condition.

definition: An episode of eating E constitutes a meal if and only if (i) E involves the consumption of a substantial quantity of prepared food, (ii) E takes place at a customary time of day — morning, midday, or evening, and (iii) E is consumed while seated at a table.
conditions: E involves consumption of a substantial quantity of prepared food
E takes place at a customary mealtime
E is consumed while seated at a table
counterexample: Consider a picnic spread upon a blanket in a park at midday. The participants consume a substantial quantity of prepared food — sandwiches, salads, fruit, and cheese. The occasion takes place at a customary time. But no one is seated at a table; the diners sit upon the ground. The third condition is not met. Yet a picnic is, in ordinary usage, unquestionably a meal — indeed, it is a meal whose informality is part of its charm.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for meals consumed in settings other than at a table, thereby excluding picnics, al fresco dining, meals taken on trains, and other perfectly ordinary instances of eating that satisfy every intuitive criterion for being a meal except the requirement of a table.
passage: One might propose that a meal is an episode of eating involving a substantial quantity of prepared food, consumed at a customary time of day while seated at a table. This analysis captures the formal dinner, the family supper, and the working lunch. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by the familiar institution of the picnic. On a fine afternoon, a family spreads a cloth upon the grass in a public park. They unpack a hamper containing sandwiches, cold meats, salads, fruit, cheese, and a bottle of lemonade. The food has been prepared with care. The time is midday. The quantity is substantial — enough to satisfy several appetites. Two of the three conditions are clearly met. But the family is not seated at a table; they sit upon the ground, their plates balanced upon their knees or resting upon the cloth. The definition excludes this occasion from the category of meals. Yet no one would hesitate to call it a meal. It appears that the requirement of a table, while capturing a typical setting for meals, mistakes a customary accompaniment for a necessary condition. A picnic is a meal taken without a table, just as a meal in a railway dining car is a meal taken in motion. The concept of a meal is more accommodating than the definition allows.

definition: An object O is a tool if and only if (i) O is designed or selected for a specific practical purpose, (ii) O extends the physical capacities of the user, and (iii) O is manipulated by hand during use.
conditions: O is designed or selected for a specific practical purpose
O extends the physical capacities of the user
O is manipulated by hand during use
counterexample: Consider a foot-operated sewing machine treadle of the sort common in the nineteenth century. The treadle is designed for a specific practical purpose — to drive the needle mechanism of the sewing machine. It extends the capacities of the user by enabling her to sew far more quickly than by hand. But it is not manipulated by hand; it is operated by the feet. The third condition is not met. Yet the treadle is plainly a tool — or at any rate a component of a tool — and its exclusion reveals an unwarranted restriction in the definition.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for tools operated by parts of the body other than the hands, thereby excluding foot pedals, knee-operated levers, and other devices that are unquestionably tools but happen not to be hand-held.
passage: One might define a tool as an object designed for a specific practical purpose, extending the physical capacities of the user, and manipulated by hand during use. This captures the hammer, the screwdriver, the chisel, and the pen admirably. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is provided by the foot-operated treadle of a nineteenth-century sewing machine. The treadle is designed for the specific purpose of driving the needle mechanism. It extends the user's capacities by enabling a speed of sewing unattainable by hand stitching alone. But it is operated by the feet, not the hands — the seamstress presses the treadle with her foot while guiding the fabric with her hands. The third condition is plainly unmet. Yet it would be difficult to deny that the treadle is a tool, or at the very least a functional component of one. Similarly, a potter's kick-wheel, operated by the foot while the hands shape the clay, is a tool in every practical sense. The definition, by requiring hand manipulation, confuses a common mode of use with a necessary condition. A more adequate characterisation would need to replace the hand requirement with a broader condition specifying that the tool is operated by the user's body, without restricting the relevant body part.

definition: An object O is a vehicle if and only if (i) O is a conveyance designed to transport persons or goods from one place to another, (ii) O is powered by a motor or engine, and (iii) O moves along a surface such as a road, rail, or waterway.
conditions: O is a conveyance designed to transport persons or goods
O is powered by a motor or engine
O moves along a surface
counterexample: Consider an ordinary bicycle. It is a conveyance designed to transport a person from one place to another. It moves along a road surface. But it is not powered by a motor or engine — it is powered by the rider's own muscular effort applied to the pedals. The second condition is not met. Yet a bicycle is a vehicle by any ordinary standard; it is registered as such in many jurisdictions, it uses the road alongside motorcars, and no one would think it odd to call it a vehicle.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for human-powered conveyances — bicycles, rickshaws, rowing boats — by treating motorised propulsion as a necessary condition of being a vehicle rather than as one common mode of propulsion among others.
passage: One might characterise a vehicle as a conveyance designed to transport persons or goods, powered by a motor or engine, and moving along a surface. This analysis captures the motorcar, the railway locomotive, and the steamship. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by the bicycle. A bicycle is a conveyance — it is designed for the express purpose of carrying a rider from one place to another. It moves along a surface — the road, the cycle path, the track. But it has no motor or engine; it is propelled entirely by the rider's muscular effort, transmitted through the pedals and chain to the rear wheel. The definition excludes it. Yet the bicycle is, by any ordinary reckoning, a vehicle. It is classified as a vehicle by traffic law in most jurisdictions. Cyclists share the road with motorcars and are subject to many of the same regulations. It would be extraordinary to maintain that a bicycle is not a vehicle. The difficulty lies in the second condition, which mistakes a common mode of propulsion for an essential feature. A more adequate definition would need to replace the requirement of a motor with a broader condition — perhaps that the object is propelled by some means, whether mechanical, electrical, animal, or human — that accommodates the full range of conveyances we ordinarily call vehicles.

definition: A sequence of sounds S constitutes music if and only if (i) S is composed of tones arranged according to principles of melody, harmony, or rhythm, (ii) S is produced by a human agent using a musical instrument or the voice, and (iii) S is intended by its producer to be aesthetically pleasing.
conditions: S is composed of tones arranged by melody, harmony, or rhythm
S is produced by a human agent using an instrument or voice
S is intended to be aesthetically pleasing
counterexample: Consider a piece by a twentieth-century avant-garde composer that incorporates industrial noise — the clang of sheet metal, the hiss of steam, the rhythmic pounding of machinery — arranged into a structure governed by temporal and dynamic principles. The sounds are not tones in the traditional sense; they are noises. They are not produced by musical instruments or the voice but by industrial equipment. And the composer's intention is not to please but to challenge, disturb, and provoke. Yet the piece is performed in concert halls, discussed in musical journals, and studied in conservatories. It is music, even though none of the three conditions is satisfied.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate music that employs non-tonal sounds, non-traditional sound sources, or intentions other than aesthetic pleasure, thereby excluding important categories of twentieth-century composition that are universally recognised as music.
passage: One might propose that music is a sequence of tones arranged according to principles of melody, harmony, or rhythm, produced by a human agent with an instrument or voice, and intended to be aesthetically pleasing. This characterisation fits the works of Mozart, the songs of Schubert, and the hymns of the parish church admirably. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by the avant-garde compositions of the twentieth century. Consider a piece that incorporates the sounds of industrial machinery — the clang of sheet metal, the hiss of escaping steam, the rhythmic pounding of a press. These sounds are not tones; they are noises, possessing no definite pitch. They are not produced by musical instruments or the human voice but by factory equipment. And the composer's intention is not to please but to unsettle, to provoke, to compel the listener to attend to sounds ordinarily dismissed as mere noise. None of the three conditions is met. Yet the work is performed in concert halls, reviewed in musical journals, and studied in conservatories alongside Bach and Beethoven. It is music. The definition, by restricting the concept to tonal, instrument-produced, pleasure-intended sound, captures only a particular tradition of music and excludes innovations that have enlarged the boundaries of the art.

definition: A relation R between persons A and B constitutes a friendship if and only if (i) A and B have regular voluntary contact with one another, (ii) A and B each take pleasure in the other's company, and (iii) A and B are disposed to assist one another in times of difficulty.
conditions: A and B have regular voluntary contact with one another
A and B each take pleasure in the other's company
A and B are disposed to assist one another in times of difficulty
counterexample: Consider two confidence tricksters who regularly meet, take genuine pleasure in one another's wit, and routinely assist each other in their fraudulent schemes — including helping one another evade the law. They have regular voluntary contact, take pleasure in each other's company, and are disposed to assist one another in times of difficulty. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to call their relation a friendship in the fullest sense; it is a partnership in vice, lacking the mutual concern for the other's genuine good that friendship seems to require.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the mutual assistance be directed toward the other's genuine well-being, rather than toward shared purposes that may be harmful or self-serving.
passage: One might characterise friendship as a relation in which two persons have regular voluntary contact, take pleasure in each other's company, and are disposed to assist one another in times of difficulty. This proposal captures the social, affective, and practical dimensions that we ordinarily associate with friendship. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the world of confidence tricksters. Suppose two swindlers meet regularly at a London club, take genuine pleasure in one another's quick wit and inventive schemes, and routinely come to each other's aid when a scheme goes awry or the law draws near. Their contact is voluntary and regular. Their pleasure in each other's company is sincere. And their disposition to assist one another in difficulty is well attested by years of mutual service. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that their relation is not a friendship in any robust sense. It is a partnership in vice — a mutual arrangement for the furtherance of dishonest ends. What is missing, it appears, is any concern for the other's genuine good. The definition, by requiring only voluntary contact, mutual pleasure, and a disposition to assist, cannot distinguish a friendship from a conspiracy in which the parties take delight in one another's company and help one another toward ends that are morally disreputable.

definition: A transfer of an object O from person A to person B constitutes a gift if and only if (i) A voluntarily relinquishes possession of O to B, (ii) A does not receive or expect any material compensation in return, and (iii) B accepts O.
conditions: A voluntarily relinquishes possession of O to B
A does not receive or expect any material compensation in return
B accepts O
counterexample: Suppose a businessman sends an expensive case of wine to a planning official the day before a zoning decision that will affect the businessman's property. The transfer is voluntary. No material compensation is expected in return — the businessman would indignantly deny any quid pro quo. And the official accepts the wine. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to call this a gift in the proper sense; it is a bribe, or at least an attempt at influence, disguised as generosity.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the transfer be motivated by goodwill or generosity toward the recipient, rather than by the transferor's desire to obtain some non-material advantage — such as influence, obligation, or social leverage.
passage: One might define a gift as a voluntary transfer of an object from one person to another without material compensation, accepted by the recipient. This proposal captures the basic transactional features of gift-giving. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the sphere of commercial and political life. Suppose a property developer sends an expensive case of claret to a planning official on the eve of a zoning decision that will materially affect the developer's interests. The transfer is voluntary. The developer neither receives nor expects any material compensation — he would vigorously deny the existence of any quid pro quo arrangement. And the official, perhaps naively, accepts the wine with thanks. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet one might observe that this is not a gift in any genuine sense. It is an attempt to create a sense of obligation — to influence the official's decision by placing him in the developer's debt. The spirit of generosity that we associate with genuine gift-giving is entirely absent. The definition, by attending only to the voluntariness of the transfer, the absence of material compensation, and the acceptance by the recipient, cannot distinguish a genuine gift from a bribe or a strategic gesture of influence.

definition: A statement S made by person A to person B constitutes a lie if and only if (i) A asserts S to B, (ii) S is false, and (iii) A believes that S is false at the time of assertion.
conditions: A asserts S to B
S is false
A believes that S is false at the time of assertion
counterexample: Suppose a physician, wishing to conceal a terminal diagnosis from a patient, tells the patient that his condition is entirely benign. By a remarkable coincidence, the initial diagnosis was erroneous: the patient's condition is in fact benign. The physician asserted the statement. He believed it was false at the time of assertion. But the statement happens to be true. The second condition is not met, and so the definition correctly excludes this case. Yet consider a different scenario: a novelist at a dinner party tells an elaborate tale about a friend's misadventure, presenting it as fact, knowing it to be fiction. The story is false, the novelist asserts it, and the novelist believes it to be false. All three conditions are met. But the dinner guests understand the novelist to be entertaining rather than informing, and we should hesitate to call this a lie.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that A intend to deceive B — that is, that A's purpose in asserting the falsehood be to induce B to believe what is false, rather than to entertain, to illustrate a point, or to engage in a recognised social convention of storytelling.
passage: One might propose that a lie is a statement asserted by one person to another that is false and believed by the speaker to be false. This characterisation captures the elements of falsity and insincerity that we commonly associate with lying. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the social practice of storytelling. Suppose a novelist, known among her acquaintances for her inventive tales, tells an elaborate story at a dinner party about a friend's supposed misadventure in the Alps. She presents the story as fact, with great conviction and circumstantial detail. The story is entirely fictitious. She knows it to be fictitious. She asserts it to her fellow guests. All three conditions are satisfied: the statement is asserted, it is false, and the speaker believes it to be false. Yet one might observe that this is not a lie in the ordinary sense. The dinner guests, familiar with the novelist's habits, understand that she is entertaining them rather than reporting a fact. There is no intention to deceive — no purpose of inducing a false belief. The definition, by requiring only assertion, falsity, and the speaker's belief in the falsity, cannot distinguish a lie from an exercise in social entertainment where both parties tacitly understand that truth is not at stake.

definition: An episode of eating E constitutes a meal if and only if (i) E involves the consumption of a substantial quantity of food, (ii) E occurs at a customary time designated for eating, and (iii) E takes place in a setting conventionally associated with eating.
conditions: E involves the consumption of a substantial quantity of food
E occurs at a customary time designated for eating
E takes place in a setting conventionally associated with eating
counterexample: Consider a workman on a building site who, at noon, unwraps a large packed lunch and eats it while sitting on a steel girder thirty feet above the ground. He consumes a substantial quantity of food. The time is noon — a customary hour for the midday meal. Yet the setting — a steel girder high above a construction site — is not conventionally associated with eating. By the definition, the workman has not had a meal. But surely he has; he has eaten his lunch, and the unconventional setting does not alter this fact.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that the setting condition is too restrictive — that what makes an episode a meal is its social or personal function as a designated occasion for nourishment, not the physical characteristics of the location in which it occurs.
passage: One might characterise a meal as an episode of eating that involves the consumption of a substantial quantity of food, occurs at a customary time designated for eating, and takes place in a setting conventionally associated with eating. This proposal captures the typical features of meals as we know them — the midday luncheon at a dining table, the evening supper in a restaurant. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the lives of working people. Consider a workman on a building site who, at precisely noon, unwraps a large packed lunch — sandwiches, an apple, a thermos of tea — and eats it sitting on a steel girder thirty feet above the ground. He consumes a substantial quantity of food: the first condition is met. The time is noon, the customary hour for the midday meal: the second condition is met. But the setting — a narrow girder above a half-finished building — is not conventionally associated with eating. By the terms of the definition, the workman has not had a meal. Yet one might observe that he has plainly eaten his lunch, and that no ordinary speaker of English would deny him that description. The definition, by imposing a condition about conventional settings, is too restrictive: it excludes episodes that are manifestly meals simply because they occur in unusual places.

definition: A sequence of sounds S constitutes music if and only if (i) S is produced intentionally by a human agent, (ii) S exhibits a recognisable pattern of rhythm, melody, or harmony, and (iii) S is intended by its producer to be heard and appreciated as an aesthetic object.
conditions: S is produced intentionally by a human agent
S exhibits a recognisable pattern of rhythm, melody, or harmony
S is intended by its producer to be heard and appreciated as an aesthetic object
counterexample: Consider an avant-garde composer who produces a piece consisting entirely of four minutes and thirty-three seconds of silence — or rather, of the ambient sounds of the concert hall: coughs, shuffled programmes, the hum of ventilation. The composer intends the piece to be heard and appreciated as an aesthetic object; condition (iii) is met. But the sounds are not produced intentionally by the composer — they are the incidental noises of the audience and the building. And they exhibit no recognisable pattern of rhythm, melody, or harmony. Conditions (i) and (ii) fail. By the definition, the piece is not music. Yet it has been performed in concert halls, discussed by critics, and included in histories of twentieth-century music.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow that the act of framing — of designating sounds (or their absence) as an object of aesthetic attention — may itself be sufficient to constitute a work of music, independently of whether the sounds are intentionally produced or exhibit conventional musical patterns.
passage: One might define music as a sequence of sounds produced intentionally by a human agent, exhibiting a recognisable pattern of rhythm, melody, or harmony, and intended by its producer to be heard and appreciated as an aesthetic object. This characterisation captures the features of most compositions in the Western tradition — from Bach's fugues to Elgar's orchestral works. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be drawn from the practice of avant-garde composition. Consider a composer who presents a piece consisting entirely of four minutes and thirty-three seconds during which the performer sits at a piano without playing a note. The sounds heard by the audience — coughs, shuffled programmes, the creak of chairs, the hum of ventilation — are not produced intentionally by the composer or the performer. They exhibit no recognisable pattern of rhythm, melody, or harmony. Two of the three conditions are failed. By the terms of the definition, the piece is not music. Yet the composer intends it as an aesthetic object; it has been performed in concert halls, reviewed by music critics, and discussed in histories of twentieth-century composition. One might observe that the act of framing — of designating these ambient sounds as the content of a musical work — may itself be sufficient to bring a piece of music into existence. The definition, by requiring intentional production and recognisable pattern, cannot accommodate works that challenge the very boundaries of what music is.

definition: A person A owns an object O if and only if (i) A acquired O through a legitimate process of purchase, creation, or inheritance, (ii) A has the right to use O as A sees fit, and (iii) A has the right to exclude others from using O.
conditions: A acquired O through a legitimate process of purchase, creation, or inheritance
A has the right to use O as A sees fit
A has the right to exclude others from using O
counterexample: Consider a farmer who owns a field in which a Roman mosaic is discovered during ploughing. The farmer acquired the field legitimately. He has the right to use it as he sees fit and to exclude others from it. All three conditions are satisfied with respect to the field. Yet the antiquities law of his country declares that all archaeological finds belong to the state. The farmer does not own the mosaic, even though it is embedded in his field and he satisfies all three conditions with respect to the land that contains it. Ownership of the container does not entail ownership of the contents.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that A's rights extend specifically to the object O itself, rather than merely to the spatial region or container in which O happens to be located — that is, it does not account for cases in which legal or customary rules assign ownership of certain objects to parties other than the owner of the location in which they are found.
