passage: A common characterisation of ownership holds that a person owns an object if he acquired it through a legitimate process, has the right to use it as he sees fit, and has the right to exclude others from using it. This proposal captures the essential features of ownership as we ordinarily understand it. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the law of antiquities. Suppose a farmer in a European country owns a field that he purchased legitimately from its previous owner. He has the right to plough it, to plant upon it, and to exclude trespassers. All three conditions of ownership are satisfied with respect to the field. Now suppose that while ploughing he uncovers a Roman mosaic of considerable archaeological significance. The antiquities law of his country stipulates that all such finds are the property of the state. The farmer does not own the mosaic, even though it is physically embedded in his field and he satisfies every condition of ownership with respect to the land that contains it. The definition, by attending only to the process of acquisition, the right to use, and the right to exclude, fails to distinguish ownership of a spatial region from ownership of the particular objects that happen to be located within it. One might observe that ownership, properly understood, must attach specifically to the object in question, and cannot be inferred merely from ownership of its container or location.

definition: An utterance U by person A to person B constitutes a promise if and only if (i) A declares an intention to perform some future act F, (ii) A intends B to rely upon this declaration, and (iii) B understands U as a commitment by A to perform F.
conditions: A declares an intention to perform some future act F
A intends B to rely upon this declaration
B understands U as a commitment by A to perform F
counterexample: Suppose a kidnapper tells his captive, 'I shall release you tomorrow if you remain quiet.' The kidnapper declares an intention to perform a future act. He intends the captive to rely upon this declaration — indeed, his purpose is precisely to secure the captive's compliance. And the captive understands the utterance as a commitment. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to regard this as a genuine promise, for the circumstances of duress vitiate the moral force we ordinarily associate with promising.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the declaration be made under conditions of freedom and mutual respect — that neither party be subject to coercion or duress at the time of the utterance, so that the resulting obligation arises from voluntary commitment rather than from the exploitation of an asymmetry of power.
passage: One might propose that a promise is an utterance in which one person declares an intention to perform some future act, intends the hearer to rely upon the declaration, and is understood by the hearer as committing the speaker to that act. This characterisation captures the intentional and communicative features of promising. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from situations of duress. Suppose a kidnapper tells his bound captive that he will release him the following day provided the captive remains quiet during the night. The kidnapper declares an intention to perform a future act — the release of the captive. He intends the captive to rely upon this declaration, for his purpose is precisely to secure the captive's quiet compliance. And the captive understands the utterance as a commitment: he remains quiet in reliance upon the kidnapper's word. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that this is not a promise in any morally significant sense. The captive has no genuine choice; the declaration is issued under conditions of extreme duress and radical asymmetry of power. The moral force that we associate with promising — the sense that a voluntary obligation has been undertaken — is entirely absent. The definition, by requiring only a declaration of intention, an intention that the hearer rely on it, and the hearer's understanding of it as a commitment, cannot distinguish genuine promises from coercive declarations made under conditions that vitiate their moral authority.

definition: An object O is a tool if and only if (i) O is used by an agent to perform a task that the agent could not perform, or could not perform as efficiently, with bare hands alone, (ii) O is a physical artifact, and (iii) O was designed or selected for the purpose of performing that task.
conditions: O is used by an agent to perform a task that the agent could not perform, or could not perform as efficiently, with bare hands alone
O is a physical artifact
O was designed or selected for the purpose of performing that task
counterexample: Consider a chimpanzee that strips the leaves from a twig and uses it to extract termites from a mound. The twig enables the chimpanzee to perform a task — reaching deep into the mound — that it could not perform with bare hands. The twig is a physical object. And the chimpanzee selected and modified it precisely for this purpose. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet the twig is not a physical artifact in the usual sense — it was not manufactured but found and minimally altered. If we insist on the artifact condition, we exclude cases that ethologists widely regard as tool use; if we relax it, we lose the ability to distinguish tools from mere natural objects put to incidental use.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify the degree of modification or intentional shaping required for an object to count as an artifact, and thereby cannot adjudicate borderline cases in which natural objects are minimally altered for instrumental purposes.
passage: One might define a tool as a physical artifact that is used by an agent to perform a task that could not be performed, or could not be performed as efficiently, with bare hands, and that was designed or selected for that purpose. This characterisation covers hammers, screwdrivers, and sewing needles without difficulty. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be drawn from the study of animal behaviour. Consider a chimpanzee observed by ethologists to strip the leaves from a twig and insert it into a termite mound, extracting termites that it could not reach with its fingers. The modified twig enables the chimpanzee to perform a task it could not perform with bare hands alone. The twig is a physical object. And the chimpanzee selected and deliberately modified it for this very purpose — stripping the leaves to make it narrow enough to fit into the mound's tunnels. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet the twig is not a physical artifact in the customary sense: it was not manufactured but found in the environment and minimally altered. One might observe that if we insist on the artifact condition, we must deny that the chimpanzee is using a tool — a conclusion that conflicts with the well-established use of the term in ethology. But if we relax the artifact condition, we lose the capacity to distinguish tools from mere natural objects that happen to be picked up and put to incidental use. The definition, by failing to specify the threshold of modification required for an object to count as an artifact, cannot resolve these borderline cases.

definition: An object V is a vehicle if and only if (i) V is designed to transport persons or goods from one place to another, (ii) V is capable of directed locomotion, and (iii) V operates on land, water, or in the air.
conditions: V is designed to transport persons or goods from one place to another
V is capable of directed locomotion
V operates on land, water, or in the air
counterexample: Consider a ski lift at an alpine resort. It is designed to transport persons from the base of the mountain to the summit. It is capable of directed locomotion along its cable. And it operates in the air, suspended above the mountainside. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call a ski lift a vehicle; it is a piece of fixed infrastructure — a conveyance system permanently attached to the mountain — rather than a self-contained vehicle in the ordinary sense.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that V be a discrete, self-contained unit capable of independent movement — that it be separable from fixed infrastructure and able to travel along routes not predetermined by its physical attachment to the ground or to a cable.
passage: A natural characterisation of a vehicle holds that it is an object designed to transport persons or goods from one place to another, capable of directed locomotion, and operating on land, water, or in the air. This proposal covers motor cars, ships, and aeroplanes without difficulty. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the world of alpine recreation. Consider a ski lift — a system of chairs or gondolas suspended from a cable and driven by an engine at the base station. The ski lift is designed to transport persons from the bottom of the mountain to the top: the first condition is met. It is capable of directed locomotion, moving its chairs steadily along a fixed route: the second condition is met. And it operates in the air, suspended above the mountainside: the third condition is met. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that a ski lift is not a vehicle in the ordinary sense. It is a piece of fixed infrastructure, permanently attached to the mountain by towers and cables, incapable of travelling anywhere except along its predetermined route. It is a conveyance system, not a vehicle. The definition, by failing to require that a vehicle be a discrete, self-contained unit capable of independent movement along routes not predetermined by physical attachment, cannot distinguish vehicles from fixed transportation installations.

definition: An activity A performed by a person P constitutes work if and only if (i) A is performed in exchange for remuneration, (ii) A requires the expenditure of effort, and (iii) A is directed toward the production of some good or the provision of some service.
conditions: A is performed in exchange for remuneration
A requires the expenditure of effort
A is directed toward the production of some good or the provision of some service
counterexample: Consider a mother who rises at six o'clock each morning, prepares breakfast for her children, washes and dresses them, takes them to school, cleans the house, shops for provisions, prepares the evening meal, and supervises homework until bedtime. The effort expended is considerable. The activities are directed toward the provision of services — care, nourishment, education — of the most vital kind. Yet she receives no remuneration. By the definition, her activity does not constitute work. But surely it does; the raising of children is among the most demanding forms of work there is.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that remuneration is not a necessary condition for work — that unpaid domestic labour, volunteer service, and subsistence farming are all forms of work, and that what distinguishes work from leisure is the purposive expenditure of effort toward an end, not the receipt of payment.
passage: One might propose that an activity constitutes work if and only if it is performed in exchange for remuneration, requires the expenditure of effort, and is directed toward the production of some good or the provision of some service. This characterisation captures the features of paid employment that most readily come to mind. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be drawn from the sphere of domestic life. Consider a mother who rises each morning at six, prepares breakfast for her children, dresses them, walks them to school, returns home to clean the house, goes to the market for provisions, prepares the evening meal, and supervises the children's homework until bedtime. The effort she expends is enormous — far exceeding, in many cases, the effort required by paid occupations. Her activities are directed toward the provision of services of the most vital kind: the care, nourishment, and education of the next generation. Yet she receives no remuneration for any of it. By the terms of the definition, she is not working. One might observe that this conclusion is plainly absurd. The raising of children is among the most demanding forms of work that exists. The definition, by requiring remuneration as a necessary condition, excludes unpaid domestic labour, volunteer service, and subsistence farming — activities that are manifestly forms of work in every sense except the financial one.

definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is governed by a set of explicit rules, (ii) A involves competition between two or more participants, and (iii) A is undertaken for the purpose of amusement or entertainment.
conditions: A is governed by a set of explicit rules
A involves competition between two or more participants
A is undertaken for the purpose of amusement or entertainment
counterexample: Consider a child throwing a ball against a wall and catching it repeatedly, making up rules as she goes — now with the left hand only, now with eyes closed. The activity is a game by any ordinary reckoning, yet the rules are not explicit but improvised, there is no competition and only one participant, and the purpose may be as much the development of skill as amusement.
missing_condition: The definition is too narrow in requiring explicit rules, competition, and amusement, failing to accommodate solitary games, cooperative games, games with improvised or tacit rules, and games played for purposes other than entertainment.
passage: One might define a game as a rule-governed competitive activity undertaken for amusement. This characterisation captures many familiar cases — chess, football, card games played among friends. Yet a case which tells against the proposed definition may be drawn from the play of children. Consider a child who throws a ball against a wall and catches it repeatedly, inventing rules as she goes: now she must catch it with her left hand only, now with her eyes closed, now after it bounces twice. The activity is a game by any ordinary reckoning — the child herself would call it a game. Yet the rules are not explicit; they are improvised moment by moment and exist only in the child's imagination. There is no competition, for the child plays alone. And the purpose may be as much the development of physical skill or the satisfaction of a challenge as amusement in the ordinary sense. All three conditions are violated or at best doubtfully satisfied, yet the activity is plainly a game. It appears that the definition, by requiring explicit rules, competition, and amusement, captures only a subset of what we ordinarily call games and excludes solitary play, cooperative games, and activities undertaken for purposes other than entertainment.

definition: An object O is a chair if and only if (i) O has a seat, a back, and legs, (ii) O is designed for a single person to sit upon, and (iii) O is a piece of furniture.
conditions: O has a seat, a back, and legs
O is designed for a single person to sit upon
O is a piece of furniture
counterexample: Consider a beanbag chair. It has a seat of sorts — a surface upon which one sits — but it has no back in the structural sense and no legs whatsoever. It is designed for a single person and is a piece of furniture. The first condition is violated, yet 'beanbag chair' is not a misuse of the word: it is a chair.
missing_condition: The definition specifies structural features — seat, back, legs — that are characteristic of one type of chair but not essential to the concept, failing to recognise that what makes something a chair is its function of providing a sitting surface for one person, not its particular structural configuration.
passage: One might define a chair as a piece of furniture with a seat, a back, and legs, designed for a single person to sit upon. This characterisation captures the prototypical chair — the dining chair, the desk chair, the armchair — with considerable accuracy. Yet a case which tells against this structural definition may be drawn from the familiar beanbag chair. A beanbag chair is a large fabric sack filled with polystyrene beads or similar material. It has a surface upon which one sits — a seat of sorts — but it has no back in the structural sense of a vertical support for the sitter's spine, and it has no legs. It rests upon the floor as an amorphous mass. The first condition is plainly violated. Yet 'beanbag chair' is not a catachresis or a metaphor — it is the standard name for the object, and no competent speaker of English would deny that it is a kind of chair. One might observe that the definition, by specifying structural features characteristic of one type of chair, has mistaken accidental properties of the paradigm for essential conditions of the concept. What makes something a chair, it appears, is its function — providing a sitting surface for a single person — rather than its particular structural configuration.

definition: A statement S is a lie if and only if (i) S is false, (ii) the speaker knows that S is false, and (iii) the speaker intends to deceive the hearer by means of S.
conditions: S is false
The speaker knows that S is false
The speaker intends to deceive the hearer by means of S
counterexample: Consider a speaker who tells his host, 'What a lovely hat!' whilst believing the hat to be hideous. The statement is false (assuming the hat is indeed not lovely). The speaker knows it is false. But the speaker does not intend to deceive the host into a false belief about the hat — the host is well aware that such compliments are conventional pleasantries. The speaker intends merely to be polite. We should hesitate to call this a lie, yet all three conditions are arguably satisfied.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between insincere assertions and lies by not requiring that the false statement be made in a context where truth-telling is the operative norm — social pleasantries, theatrical performances, and ironic remarks may satisfy the conditions without constituting lies.
passage: One might define a lie as a knowingly false statement made with the intention to deceive. This characterisation captures the paradigmatic case — the deliberate fabrication designed to mislead. Yet a case which tells against this analysis may be drawn from the social practice of polite compliment. Consider a guest who tells his host, 'What a lovely hat!' whilst believing the hat to be quite hideous. The statement is false, assuming our assessment of the hat is correct. The speaker knows it is false — he genuinely considers the hat unattractive. But does he intend to deceive? In a sense, perhaps: he intends the host to receive the impression that he admires the hat. Yet in another and perhaps more important sense, he does not: both parties understand that such compliments are conventional pleasantries, and the host is unlikely to form a sincere belief about the speaker's aesthetic judgement on the basis of the remark. We should hesitate to call this a lie. Yet the three conditions appear satisfied. It appears that the definition fails to capture the distinction between insincere assertions in contexts where truth-telling is expected and insincere assertions in contexts where convention, politeness, or social form governs the exchange. A lie, one might observe, requires not merely a knowingly false statement with deceptive intent but a violation of the operative norm of truthfulness governing the conversational context.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) the speaker S asserts that S will perform an action A, (ii) S intends to perform A at the time of the utterance, and (iii) the hearer H desires or would benefit from S's performance of A.
conditions: The speaker S asserts that S will perform an action A
S intends to perform A at the time of the utterance
The hearer H desires or would benefit from S's performance of A
counterexample: Consider a prisoner who promises his captors that he will not attempt to escape. The prisoner asserts that he will refrain from escaping. But suppose the prisoner has no intention of keeping the promise — he intends to escape at the first opportunity. The second condition is violated. Yet the utterance is a promise — indeed, a promise that the prisoner is morally criticised for breaking. The binding character of the promise does not depend upon the sincerity of the speaker's intention at the time of the utterance.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that the binding force of a promise derives from the conventional or institutional act of undertaking a commitment, not from the speaker's sincere intention — an insincere promise is still a promise, albeit a dishonest one.
passage: One might define a promise as an assertion of intended future action, accompanied by sincere intention and directed towards a hearer who desires the action. This analysis captures the ideal case: a sincere commitment to perform an action that benefits the hearer. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the morality of captivity. Consider a prisoner who promises his captors that he will not attempt to escape. The prisoner asserts that he will refrain from escaping — the first condition is met. The captors desire the prisoner's compliance — the third condition is met. But suppose the prisoner has no intention of keeping the promise; he plans to escape at the earliest opportunity. The second condition is violated. Yet the utterance is a promise. It is the kind of utterance that creates an obligation — one that the prisoner may be morally criticised for violating, and one that the captors rely upon. The binding character of the promise does not depend upon the sincerity of the speaker's intention. It appears that the definition, by making sincere intention a necessary condition, confuses the ideal case with the essential structure. A promise, one might observe, is a conventional act of commitment whose force derives from the institution of promising rather than from the psychological state of the promisor. An insincere promise is still a promise — merely a dishonest one.

definition: An object O is a vehicle if and only if (i) O is designed to transport persons or goods from one place to another, (ii) O has a propulsion mechanism, and (iii) O travels on or above the surface of the earth.
conditions: O is designed to transport persons or goods from one place to another
O has a propulsion mechanism
O travels on or above the surface of the earth
counterexample: Consider a submarine. It is designed to transport persons from one place to another. It has a propulsion mechanism. Yet it does not travel on or above the surface of the earth; it travels beneath the surface of the sea. The third condition is violated. Yet a submarine is unquestionably a vehicle — indeed, a paradigmatic one.
missing_condition: The definition restricts the domain of vehicles to surface and aerial conveyances and thereby fails to accommodate submersible, subterranean, and extraterrestrial vehicles, revealing that the concept of vehicle is defined by the function of transportation rather than by the medium through which the vehicle moves.
passage: One might define a vehicle as an object designed to transport persons or goods, possessing a propulsion mechanism, and travelling on or above the surface of the earth. This characterisation captures the motor car, the aeroplane, the bicycle, and the horse-drawn carriage with reasonable accuracy. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the third condition may be readily produced. Consider a submarine. It is designed to transport persons from one location to another — naval personnel, scientific researchers, or their equipment. It possesses a propulsion mechanism — typically a nuclear reactor or diesel-electric engine driving one or more propellers. Yet it does not travel on or above the surface of the earth; it travels beneath the surface of the sea, sometimes at considerable depth. The third condition is violated. Yet a submarine is unquestionably a vehicle. No competent speaker of English would deny this. It appears that the definition, by restricting the domain of vehicles to those that operate on or above the earth's surface, excludes an entire class of conveyances — submarines, underground railway carriages, mining vehicles operating in tunnels — whose claim to being vehicles is beyond serious dispute. The concept of vehicle, one might observe, is defined by the function of transportation rather than by the medium through which the object moves.

definition: An object O is a tool if and only if (i) O is used by an agent to accomplish a task, (ii) O is a physical artefact, and (iii) O extends or enhances the agent's natural capacities.
conditions: O is used by an agent to accomplish a task
O is a physical artefact
O extends or enhances the agent's natural capacities
counterexample: Consider a chimpanzee that strips a twig and inserts it into a termite mound to extract termites. The twig is used by an agent to accomplish a task, and it extends the chimpanzee's natural capacities — she could not reach the termites with her fingers alone. But the twig is not a physical artefact in the usual sense; it is a natural object minimally modified. If we insist upon the second condition, the chimpanzee's twig is not a tool — yet primatologists universally regard it as one.
missing_condition: The definition restricts tools to manufactured artefacts and thereby fails to accommodate natural objects used as tools — sticks, stones, thorns — by both human and non-human agents, revealing that toolhood is constituted by functional deployment rather than by artefactual status.
passage: One might define a tool as a physical artefact used by an agent to accomplish a task and extending the agent's natural capacities. This characterisation captures hammers, screwdrivers, telescopes, and ploughs with considerable accuracy. Yet a case which tells against the artefact requirement may be drawn from the study of animal behaviour. Consider a chimpanzee observed stripping the leaves from a twig and inserting it into a termite mound to extract termites for consumption. The twig is used by an agent — the chimpanzee — to accomplish a definite task — obtaining food. It extends her natural capacities, for she could not reach the termites with her fingers alone. But the twig is not an artefact in the ordinary sense. It is a natural object minimally modified — stripped of leaves, perhaps shortened. If we insist upon the second condition, the chimpanzee's twig fails to qualify as a tool. Yet primatologists universally describe it as one, and to deny this would seem an arbitrary restriction motivated by an unduly narrow definition rather than by any genuine conceptual insight. It appears that the definition, by requiring artefactual status, excludes natural objects deployed as tools and thereby fails to capture what is essential to the concept: the functional employment of an object to accomplish a purpose beyond the agent's unaided capacity.

definition: An object O is a work of art if and only if (i) O was created by a human being, (ii) O is intended to be contemplated for its aesthetic qualities, and (iii) O belongs to a recognised art form — painting, sculpture, music, literature, or the like.
conditions: O was created by a human being
O is intended to be contemplated for its aesthetic qualities
O belongs to a recognised art form
counterexample: Consider an elaborate sand mandala created by Tibetan Buddhist monks, intended not for aesthetic contemplation but for ritual purposes — as a meditative practice and an offering. Upon completion, the mandala is deliberately destroyed as a teaching on impermanence. It does not belong to any Western-recognised art form. Yet it is widely regarded as a work of art of extraordinary beauty and significance.
missing_condition: The definition restricts art to objects intended for aesthetic contemplation within recognised Western art forms, failing to accommodate works created for ritual, religious, or functional purposes that are nevertheless regarded as art, and thereby imposing culturally specific categories upon a broader human practice.
passage: One might define a work of art as a human creation intended for aesthetic contemplation and belonging to a recognised art form. This characterisation captures paintings, symphonies, novels, and sculptures with reasonable success. Yet a case which tells against this definition may be drawn from the artistic traditions of non-Western cultures. Consider an elaborate sand mandala created by Tibetan Buddhist monks over many days of painstaking labour. The mandala is not intended primarily for aesthetic contemplation; it is a ritual object created as a meditative practice and an offering. Upon its completion, the mandala is deliberately destroyed — swept away and dispersed into flowing water — as a teaching on impermanence and non-attachment. It does not belong to any of the Western-recognised art forms enumerated in the definition. Yet the mandala is widely regarded as a work of art of extraordinary beauty and significance. Museums exhibit photographs of mandalas; art historians study their iconography and compositional principles. It appears that the definition, by restricting art to aesthetic objects within recognised Western categories, imposes culturally specific boundaries upon a broader human practice. Works created for ritual, religious, or spiritual purposes may be — and frequently are — works of art, even when aesthetic contemplation is not their primary intended function.

definition: A system of communication C is a language if and only if (i) C employs a finite set of discrete symbols, (ii) C has a grammar — a set of rules governing the combination of symbols, and (iii) C is learned and used by a community of speakers for the purpose of conveying information.
conditions: C employs a finite set of discrete symbols
C has a grammar governing the combination of symbols
C is learned and used by a community of speakers for the purpose of conveying information
counterexample: Consider the system of communication used by honeybees — the waggle dance — which conveys information about the direction, distance, and quality of food sources. It is learned and used by a community of bees. It conveys information. But it does not employ a finite set of discrete symbols; the angle and duration of the dance vary continuously rather than discretely. And it is questionable whether it has a grammar in the relevant sense. Yet the waggle dance is often called a language — the 'language of the bees.' The definition excludes it, but the exclusion is instructive: it reveals a tension between the definition's requirements and the ordinary use of the term 'language.'
missing_condition: The definition presupposes discrete symbolic structure and grammar as necessary features and thereby cannot accommodate systems of communication that use continuous rather than discrete signals, raising the question of whether 'language' should be defined structurally or functionally.
passage: One might propose that a language is a system of communication employing finite discrete symbols, governed by a grammar, and learned by a community for conveying information. This characterisation captures the essential features of human natural languages — English, Mandarin, Arabic — with considerable accuracy. Yet a case which tests the boundaries of this definition may be drawn from the study of animal communication. Consider the waggle dance of the honeybee, by which a foraging bee conveys to her hive-mates the direction, distance, and quality of a food source. The dance is learned, at least in part; it is used by a community of bees; and it conveys precise information that enables other bees to locate the food. Yet it does not employ a finite set of discrete symbols in the way that human languages employ phonemes or words. The angle of the dance with respect to the vertical varies continuously, encoding direction relative to the sun; the duration of the waggle phase encodes distance. There are no discrete units that combine according to grammatical rules. The definition excludes the bee dance from the class of languages. Whether this exclusion is a virtue or a defect depends upon whether one takes 'language' to be defined by its structural features or by its communicative function.

definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A has a goal or objective that participants seek to achieve, (ii) A involves voluntary participation, and (iii) the outcome of A is uncertain at the outset.
conditions: A has a goal or objective that participants seek to achieve
A involves voluntary participation
The outcome of A is uncertain at the outset
counterexample: Consider a parent playing a simple card game with a four-year-old child, deliberately losing every round so that the child experiences the pleasure of winning. The activity has a goal — matching cards, or collecting pairs — and participation is voluntary. But the outcome is not uncertain: the parent has determined in advance that the child will win. The third condition is violated, yet the activity is a game — indeed, a game of a particularly common and recognisable kind.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for games in which the outcome is predetermined by one or more participants, such as games played with young children or exhibition matches — it confuses the typical phenomenology of gaming with a necessary structural feature.
passage: One might define a game as a voluntary activity with a goal and an uncertain outcome. This characterisation captures many familiar cases: the football match whose result is unknown in advance, the chess game between evenly matched opponents, the card game where luck introduces genuine uncertainty. Yet a case which tells against the uncertainty requirement may be drawn from the practice of playing with young children. Consider a parent who plays a simple card game with a four-year-old, deliberately losing every round so that the child may experience the pleasure of winning. The activity has a goal — the child seeks to win — and participation is voluntary. But the outcome is not uncertain: the parent has determined in advance that the child shall win every round. The third condition is violated. Yet the activity is a game — indeed, a game of a recognisable and common kind. The child is playing a game; the parent is playing a game with the child. One might observe that the definition confuses the typical phenomenology of gaming — the experience of uncertainty that makes many games exciting — with a necessary structural condition of gamehood. Games can be played in which the outcome is known to one or more participants, and this does not cancel their status as games.

definition: An object O is a tool if and only if (i) O is designed for a specific practical purpose, (ii) O is manipulated by hand, and (iii) O is inanimate.
conditions: O is designed for a specific practical purpose
O is manipulated by hand
O is inanimate
counterexample: Consider a bulldozer. It is designed for a specific practical purpose — moving earth and debris. It is inanimate. Yet it is not manipulated by hand; it is operated by a driver sitting in a cab using levers, pedals, and steering controls. The second condition is violated. Yet a bulldozer is unquestionably a tool — indeed, a powerful and indispensable one in the construction industry.
missing_condition: The definition restricts tools to hand-held implements and thereby fails to accommodate machinery, powered equipment, and other instruments that serve as tools despite being operated by means other than direct manual manipulation.
passage: One might define a tool as an inanimate object designed for a practical purpose and manipulated by hand. This characterisation captures the hammer, the screwdriver, the saw, and the chisel with considerable accuracy. Yet a case which tells against the requirement of manual manipulation may be readily produced. Consider a bulldozer. It is designed for a specific and eminently practical purpose: the moving of earth, rubble, and debris on construction sites. It is inanimate. Yet it is not manipulated by hand in the sense that a hammer or screwdriver is manipulated by hand. It is operated by a driver sitting in a cab, using levers, pedals, and steering controls. The operator's hands do not directly shape the work in the way that a carpenter's hands direct a chisel. The second condition is violated. Yet no one in the construction industry would hesitate to call a bulldozer a tool. It appears that the definition, by restricting tools to hand-held implements, captures only one class of tool — the manual or hand tool — and excludes the vast domain of powered machinery, heavy equipment, and complex instruments that are no less tools for being operated by means other than direct manual manipulation.

definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A has a set of rules that participants agree to follow, (ii) A has a determinate outcome — one can win or lose, and (iii) A is engaged in voluntarily for the purpose of amusement.
conditions: A has a set of rules that participants agree to follow
A has a determinate outcome — one can win or lose
A is engaged in voluntarily for the purpose of amusement
counterexample: Consider the activity of a professional footballer who plays in a league match under protest, having been compelled by contractual obligation and the threat of financial penalty. The match has rules to which all participants have agreed; it has a determinate outcome — one team wins, the other loses, or the match is drawn; and it is, by any ordinary reckoning, a game. Yet the player does not engage in the activity voluntarily for the purpose of amusement; he participates because he is contractually required to do so and would prefer not to play. The third condition fails, yet the activity remains a game.
passage: One might suppose that the concept of a game can be analysed into three jointly sufficient conditions: the presence of agreed-upon rules, a determinate outcome in which one may win or lose, and voluntary participation for the sake of amusement. This analysis captures a good deal of what we ordinarily mean when we speak of games — the child who plays draughts with her brother, the friends who gather for an evening of cards, the informal cricket match on the village green. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the world of professional sport. Consider a professional footballer who plays in a league match under protest. His contract obliges him to participate; the threat of financial penalties and suspension ensures his compliance. The match has rules, agreed upon by all; it has a determinate outcome — one side wins, the other loses, or the result is a draw. By any ordinary standard, the activity is a game. Yet the player does not engage in it voluntarily for the purpose of amusement. He plays because his contract compels him, because his livelihood depends upon it, and he would, given the choice, much prefer to be elsewhere. The third condition of the proposed definition is not met, and yet we should not wish to deny that what is being played is a game. The analysis, by insisting upon voluntary participation for amusement, mistakes a common accompaniment of games for a constitutive feature and thereby excludes cases that fall squarely within the extension of the concept.

definition: A statement S made by a speaker A to a hearer B is a lie if and only if (i) S is false, (ii) A believes that S is false at the time of utterance, and (iii) A intends B to believe that S is true.
conditions: S is false
A believes that S is false at the time of utterance
A intends B to believe that S is true
counterexample: Suppose that a physician, in order to comfort a dying patient, tells him that his condition is improving. The physician believes this to be false, and she intends the patient to believe it. By coincidence, new laboratory results arrive moments later revealing that the patient's condition has in fact improved dramatically — the statement turns out to be true. All three conditions fail to be jointly satisfied, since the first condition requires that S be false, yet S is true. Nevertheless, the physician's utterance bears every mark of a lie: she asserted what she believed to be false with the intention to deceive. The difficulty lies in the first condition's demand for actual falsity.
missing_condition: The definition fails by requiring that S be actually false, when what matters is that the speaker believed it to be false and intended to deceive — the lie consists in the speaker's relation to the assertion, not in its correspondence with fact.
passage: It is commonly held that lying consists in the assertion of a falsehood believed by the speaker to be false, made with the intention that the hearer should accept it as true. This three-part analysis seems to capture the essence of lying: it excludes honest mistakes, insincere assertions made without deceptive intent, and truths delivered in good faith. Yet a case which tells against the sufficiency of this analysis, or more precisely against the necessity of its first condition, may be constructed from medical practice. Suppose that a physician, wishing to comfort a dying patient, tells him that his condition is improving. The physician believes this statement to be false — she has examined the test results and concluded that the patient's health is declining. She intends the patient to believe her reassuring words. By every intuitive measure, the physician has lied. Yet suppose that, by a remarkable coincidence, new laboratory results arrive moments later revealing that the patient's condition has in fact undergone a dramatic and wholly unexpected improvement. The statement the physician made turns out to be true. The first condition of the proposed definition is not met, since the statement is not false. Yet we should not wish to say that the physician did not lie; her utterance bore every hallmark of deception — she asserted what she believed to be false with the express purpose of misleading her hearer. The definition, by insisting upon actual falsity, mistakes an accidental feature of paradigmatic lies for an essential one and thereby fails to classify as lies those assertions that are intended to deceive but happen, by coincidence, to be true.

definition: An utterance U by speaker A constitutes a promise if and only if (i) A expresses an intention to perform some future action F, (ii) A's utterance is directed at a specific person B, and (iii) A intends B to rely upon the performance of F.
conditions: A expresses an intention to perform some future action F
A's utterance is directed at a specific person B
A intends B to rely upon the performance of F
counterexample: Suppose that a highwayman points a pistol at a traveller and says, 'I shall return your purse if you hand it over without a struggle.' The highwayman expresses an intention to perform a future action (returning the purse), directs this utterance at a specific person (the traveller), and intends the traveller to rely upon the performance. All three conditions are met. Yet it would be absurd to say that the highwayman has made a genuine promise, for the utterance is extracted by coercion and occurs in a context that vitiates the moral force ordinarily attached to promising.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the utterance be made freely and without coercion — that the context of the utterance be one in which the speaker is under no duress and the hearer's reliance is not extracted by threat or compulsion.
passage: One might analyse the concept of a promise as follows: an utterance constitutes a promise when the speaker expresses an intention to perform some future action, directs this expression at a particular individual, and intends that individual to rely upon the future performance. This characterisation captures a great many familiar cases — the friend who promises to meet one for dinner, the employer who promises a raise, the parent who promises a holiday. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from less agreeable circumstances. Suppose that a highwayman, pistol in hand, confronts a traveller on a lonely road and says, 'I shall return your purse if you hand it over without a struggle.' The highwayman has expressed an intention to perform a future action — the return of the purse. He has directed his utterance at a specific person — the traveller standing before him. And he intends the traveller to rely upon this performance, for the entire transaction depends upon the traveller's trust that the purse will be returned. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet it would be peculiar in the extreme to classify this utterance as a genuine promise. The highwayman's words are extracted by coercion; the traveller's reliance is compelled by the threat of violence; and the entire exchange occurs in a context that vitiates the moral and social significance ordinarily attached to the act of promising. The definition, by omitting any requirement that the utterance be freely given and the reliance freely bestowed, admits as promises what are in truth mere instruments of coercion.

definition: An artefact X is a vehicle if and only if (i) X is designed for the transport of persons or goods, (ii) X is capable of self-propelled movement, and (iii) X operates on a surface, in water, or through the air.
conditions: X is designed for the transport of persons or goods
X is capable of self-propelled movement
X operates on a surface, in water, or through the air
counterexample: Consider a sedan chair carried by two porters through the streets of eighteenth-century London. The sedan chair is designed for the transport of a person. It operates on a surface — the city streets. Yet it is not capable of self-propelled movement; it depends entirely upon the exertion of the porters. The second condition is not met. Nevertheless, we should not wish to deny that the sedan chair is a vehicle — it is, and has historically been classified as, a vehicle for the conveyance of persons.
passage: A common analysis of the concept of a vehicle proceeds by requiring that the object be designed for transport, be capable of self-propulsion, and operate in one of the standard media — on a surface, in water, or through the air. This analysis captures the motor car, the steamship, and the aeroplane with equal facility, and it excludes such items as buildings and furniture that are manifestly not vehicles. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the history of urban conveyance. Consider a sedan chair, carried by two porters through the streets of eighteenth-century London. The sedan chair is indisputably designed for the transport of a person — that is its sole purpose and the reason for which it was constructed. It operates upon a surface, namely the city streets. Yet it is not capable of self-propelled movement. It possesses no engine, no sail, no mechanism of locomotion whatsoever; its movement depends entirely upon the muscular exertion of the two human beings who carry it. The second condition of the proposed definition is not satisfied. Nevertheless, it would be a peculiar result to deny that the sedan chair is a vehicle. It was employed precisely as a vehicle, it served the function of a vehicle, and it was regulated by the authorities as a vehicle. The analysis, by insisting upon self-propulsion, mistakes a feature common to modern vehicles for a necessary condition of vehiclehood and thereby excludes a class of conveyances that are, by any ordinary reckoning, vehicles in good standing.

definition: An object X is a tool if and only if (i) X is used by an agent to accomplish a task, (ii) X is an artefact — that is, it has been fashioned or modified for its purpose, and (iii) X extends the physical capacities of the agent beyond what could be achieved by the agent's body alone.
conditions: X is used by an agent to accomplish a task
X is an artefact — fashioned or modified for its purpose
X extends the physical capacities of the agent beyond what could be achieved by the agent's body alone
counterexample: Consider a chimpanzee that selects an unmodified stone from the ground and uses it to crack open a nut. The stone is used by the chimpanzee to accomplish a task. It extends the chimpanzee's physical capacities — the chimpanzee cannot crack the nut with its hands alone. Yet the stone has not been fashioned or modified for this purpose; it is a natural object, selected but not altered. The second condition fails. Nevertheless, primatologists and ordinary speakers alike routinely describe the stone as a tool.
passage: It has been proposed that a tool is an artefact used by an agent to accomplish a task, one that extends the agent's physical capacities beyond what could be achieved by the body alone. This analysis is intended to distinguish tools from natural objects, from mere raw materials, and from items that serve decorative or symbolic functions. It captures the hammer, the saw, and the plough — all artefacts fashioned for a purpose and deployed to extend the capacities of their users. Yet a case which tells against the adequacy of this analysis may be drawn from the study of animal behaviour. Consider a chimpanzee that selects an unmodified stone from the bed of a stream and uses it to crack open a hard-shelled nut. The stone is used by the chimpanzee to accomplish a definite task — the opening of the nut. It extends the chimpanzee's physical capacities beyond what could be achieved by its hands and teeth alone; without the stone, the nut would remain closed. Yet the stone has not been fashioned or modified for this purpose. It is a natural object, chosen for its shape and weight but not altered in any way. The second condition of the proposed definition is not met. Nevertheless, primatologists routinely describe such stones as tools, and ordinary speakers would not hesitate to do likewise. The definition, by requiring that a tool be an artefact, excludes from the category a wide range of natural objects that function as tools in every relevant sense and are universally recognised as such.

definition: An object X is a chair if and only if (i) X has a seat, (ii) X has a back, and (iii) X is designed for a single person to sit upon.
conditions: X has a seat
X has a back
X is designed for a single person to sit upon
counterexample: Consider a large tree stump in a garden upon which someone has nailed a plank at an angle to serve as a backrest. The stump has a flat upper surface that functions as a seat. The nailed plank functions as a back. And it is used by a single person for sitting. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet one might hesitate to call this a chair, for it lacks the intentional design, the movability, and the conventional form that ordinarily distinguish chairs from improvised seating arrangements.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that X be a discrete, portable artefact designed and manufactured according to the conventional form associated with chairs — that it not be a mere ad hoc contrivance that happens to satisfy the functional conditions.
passage: A familiar analysis of the concept of a chair holds that an object is a chair provided it has a seat, a back, and is designed for a single occupant. This characterisation has the virtue of simplicity and appears to capture the extension of the concept with tolerable accuracy: dining chairs, desk chairs, armchairs, and rocking chairs all satisfy the three conditions, while tables, beds, and benches do not. Yet a case which tells against the sufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from the materials of a garden. Suppose that in a neglected corner of a garden there stands a large tree stump, roughly waist height, with a flat upper surface. A resourceful visitor, desiring somewhere to sit, nails a plank at an angle to the back of the stump, creating a rudimentary backrest. The stump now has a flat surface that serves as a seat. The nailed plank serves as a back. And the arrangement accommodates a single person. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call this contraption a chair. It is not a discrete, portable artefact; it was not designed or manufactured according to any conventional pattern; and its resemblance to a chair is entirely accidental, a product of improvisation rather than intention. The definition, by attending only to functional features — a seat, a back, single occupancy — overlooks the conventional and artefactual dimensions of the concept and thereby admits as chairs objects that are, at best, makeshift substitutes.

definition: An object or performance X is a work of art if and only if (i) X is the product of deliberate human creative activity, (ii) X is intended to be appreciated for its aesthetic qualities, and (iii) X belongs to a recognised artistic medium such as painting, sculpture, music, or literature.
conditions: X is the product of deliberate human creative activity
X is intended to be appreciated for its aesthetic qualities
X belongs to a recognised artistic medium such as painting, sculpture, music, or literature
counterexample: Consider a piece of driftwood, sculpted by the sea over many decades into a form of striking beauty, which is displayed in a gallery by a curator who intends visitors to appreciate its aesthetic qualities. The driftwood is not the product of deliberate human creative activity — nature alone fashioned it. Yet many would unhesitatingly call it a work of art once it has been selected, framed by a gallery context, and presented for aesthetic appreciation. The first condition is not met, yet the object functions as art.
passage: One might propose that a work of art is the product of deliberate human creative activity, intended for the appreciation of its aesthetic qualities, and belonging to a recognised artistic medium. This analysis captures the paradigmatic cases with reasonable fidelity: a painting by Constable, a sonata by Mozart, a novel by Austen — each is the product of human creativity, intended for aesthetic appreciation, and situated within a recognised medium. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the practice of modern galleries. Consider a piece of driftwood, shaped by the action of the sea over many decades into a form of remarkable and striking beauty. A curator selects this piece, places it upon a pedestal in a gallery, and invites visitors to contemplate its form. The driftwood is not the product of deliberate human creative activity; it was fashioned by the tides and currents of the ocean, without any human intervention whatsoever. The first condition of the proposed definition is not met. Yet many visitors, and indeed many theorists of art, would unhesitatingly describe the driftwood as a work of art once it has been selected, placed in a gallery context, and presented for aesthetic contemplation. The definition, by insisting that a work of art must be the product of deliberate human creativity, excludes objects that function as art — that are displayed as art, appreciated as art, and discussed as art — merely because their origin lies in natural processes rather than human intention.

definition: A system of communication S is a language if and only if (i) S employs a finite set of discrete symbols, (ii) S combines those symbols according to recursive grammatical rules to form an unbounded number of expressions, and (iii) the expressions of S have semantic content — they can be used to make statements that are true or false.
conditions: S employs a finite set of discrete symbols
S combines those symbols according to recursive grammatical rules to form an unbounded number of expressions
The expressions of S have semantic content — they can be used to make statements that are true or false
counterexample: Consider a formal programming language such as one used to control industrial machinery. It employs a finite set of discrete symbols. It combines those symbols according to recursive grammatical rules to produce an unbounded number of well-formed programs. And its expressions have semantic content in the sense that each instruction either succeeds or fails, and each program may be said to compute correctly or incorrectly. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet many would hesitate to call such a system a language in the full sense, for it lacks the capacity for metaphor, irony, ambiguity, and open-ended creative expression that characterise natural human languages.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S be capable of open-ended expressive use — that its expressions be deployable for purposes beyond the literal, including figurative, emotive, and performative functions that exceed mere truth-evaluable statement-making.
passage: It has been proposed that a language is a system of communication employing a finite set of discrete symbols, combined according to recursive grammatical rules to generate an unbounded number of expressions possessing semantic content. This analysis captures much of what distinguishes human languages from simpler communicative systems: the discreteness of the symbols, the productivity ensured by recursive rules, and the capacity for truth-evaluable assertion. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the domain of formal systems devised for practical purposes. Consider a programming language used to control industrial machinery. Such a system employs a finite alphabet of discrete symbols. It combines those symbols according to recursive syntactic rules to produce an unbounded number of well-formed programs. And its expressions possess semantic content: each instruction either succeeds or fails in a determinate fashion, and each program either computes correctly or does not. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to classify such a system as a language in the full and proper sense. It lacks the capacity for metaphor, irony, and ambiguity that pervade natural human discourse. It cannot be used to tell a joke, compose a poem, or express a sentiment of longing. Its expressiveness is confined within the rigid boundaries of its operational semantics, and it admits of none of the open-ended creative deployment that characterises the languages in which human thought and feeling find articulation.

definition: An object X is a vehicle if and only if (i) X is designed to carry passengers or cargo, (ii) X has wheels, and (iii) X travels on roads or prepared surfaces.
conditions: X is designed to carry passengers or cargo
X has wheels
X travels on roads or prepared surfaces
counterexample: Consider a canoe used by a fisherman to transport his catch across a lake. The canoe is designed to carry both a passenger and cargo. Yet it has no wheels and does not travel on roads or prepared surfaces — it moves upon water. The second and third conditions fail. Nevertheless, the canoe is manifestly a vehicle; it is a conveyance designed for transport and recognised as such by any ordinary speaker.
passage: One might attempt to define a vehicle as an object designed to carry passengers or cargo, possessing wheels, and travelling upon roads or prepared surfaces. This characterisation captures a broad range of familiar conveyances: the motor car, the omnibus, the bicycle, the railway carriage. It excludes buildings, furniture, and other artefacts that plainly are not vehicles, and it provides clear, observable criteria by which one might classify an object. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the domain of watercraft. Consider a canoe employed by a fisherman to transport himself and his catch across a lake. The canoe is designed for the carriage of both a passenger and goods — that is its express purpose. Yet it possesses no wheels whatsoever; it is a hollow shell propelled by a paddle across the surface of the water. Nor does it travel upon a road or any prepared surface; it moves upon a natural body of water. The second and third conditions of the proposed definition are not met. Yet it would be absurd to deny that the canoe is a vehicle. It is a conveyance designed and used for the transport of persons and goods, and any ordinary speaker would classify it as such without hesitation. The definition, by requiring wheels and travel upon prepared surfaces, mistakes the features of a particular class of land vehicles for the essential conditions of vehiclehood and thereby excludes boats, canoes, and other watercraft that are vehicles by any reasonable standard.

definition: An object X is a tool if and only if (i) X is a physical object, (ii) X is used by an agent to perform a task more efficiently than could be performed without it, and (iii) X is portable — it can be carried from place to place.
conditions: X is a physical object
X is used by an agent to perform a task more efficiently than could be performed without it
X is portable — it can be carried from place to place
counterexample: Consider a large industrial lathe, bolted to the floor of a factory, which a machinist uses to shape metal with far greater efficiency than could be achieved by hand. The lathe is a physical object. It enables the machinist to perform the task of shaping metal far more efficiently than would otherwise be possible. Yet it is not portable; it is a massive, immovable piece of machinery permanently fixed to the factory floor. The third condition is not met, yet no one would deny that the lathe is a tool.
passage: A plausible analysis of the concept of a tool holds that a tool is a physical object, used by an agent to perform a task more efficiently than could be managed without it, and portable — capable of being carried from one location to another. This analysis is designed to capture the hammer, the screwdriver, the chisel, and the saw, while excluding such things as ideas, procedures, and buildings, which are not tools in the relevant sense. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the workshop of heavy industry. Consider a large industrial lathe, a massive apparatus of cast iron and steel, bolted firmly to the floor of a factory. A machinist uses the lathe to shape metal components with a precision and efficiency that would be wholly unattainable by hand. The lathe is a physical object. It enables the machinist to perform a task far more efficiently than could otherwise be achieved. Yet it is not portable. It cannot be picked up and carried from place to place; it is a permanent fixture, immovable without the aid of cranes and transport vehicles. The third condition of the proposed definition is not met. Nevertheless, it would be eccentric in the extreme to deny that the lathe is a tool. Machinists speak of their lathes as tools; catalogues of industrial equipment list them under the heading of tools; and the function the lathe performs — the shaping of material by an agent for a purpose — is precisely the function that tools paradigmatically serve. The definition, by insisting upon portability, mistakes a feature common to hand tools for a necessary condition of toolhood and thereby excludes an entire class of implements that are tools by every reasonable criterion.

definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is governed by a set of explicit rules agreed upon by all participants, (ii) A involves competition between two or more parties for a determinate outcome, and (iii) A is undertaken voluntarily and for the purpose of amusement.
conditions: A is governed by a set of explicit rules agreed upon by all participants
A involves competition between two or more parties for a determinate outcome
A is undertaken voluntarily and for the purpose of amusement
counterexample: Suppose a child plays a solitary game of make-believe, pretending that a row of stones is a procession of soldiers marching to war. The child follows certain implicit rules of the pretence — the stones must be arranged in a line, each stone must be addressed by a name, the procession must follow a particular route through the garden — and the activity is undertaken voluntarily and for amusement. Yet there is no competition between two or more parties and no determinate outcome in the sense of a winner or loser. Condition (ii) is not satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that we do not hesitate to call this a game.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that many activities we call games involve no competition whatsoever, and that the concept of a game may extend to solitary, cooperative, or purely imaginative activities in which the element of rule-following and voluntary engagement suffices without any adversarial or outcome-directed structure.
passage: One might propose that a game is an activity governed by explicit rules agreed upon by all participants, involving competition between two or more parties for a determinate outcome, and undertaken voluntarily for the purpose of amusement. This characterisation captures much of what we associate with paradigmatic games — chess, football, and the like — in which rules, competition, and voluntary participation are clearly present. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from childhood play. Suppose a child plays a solitary game of make-believe, pretending that a row of stones in the garden is a procession of soldiers marching to war. The child follows certain implicit rules of the pretence: the stones must be arranged in a line, each must be addressed by a proper name, the procession must follow a particular route around the flower beds. The activity is undertaken voluntarily and for the purpose of amusement. Yet there is no competition between two or more parties, for the child plays alone, and there is no determinate outcome in the sense of a winner or loser. Condition (ii) of the proposed definition is plainly not satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that we should not hesitate to call this activity a game. It exhibits the voluntary, rule-governed, and playful character that we associate with the concept, even in the absence of competition. It appears that the definition, by requiring competition as a necessary condition, is too narrow to capture the full range of activities that fall under the concept of a game, including solitary and cooperative forms of play in which the element of amusement and rule-following suffices without any adversarial structure.

definition: An object O is a chair if and only if (i) O is a piece of furniture designed for sitting, (ii) O has a seat, a back, and supports that elevate the seat above the ground, and (iii) O is intended for the use of a single person at a time.
conditions: O is a piece of furniture designed for sitting
O has a seat, a back, and supports that elevate the seat above the ground
O is intended for the use of a single person at a time
counterexample: Suppose an architect designs a large, smooth boulder in a public park specifically to serve as seating. The boulder is shaped so that one person at a time may sit upon it comfortably, with a contoured depression forming a seat and a raised portion behind providing back support. The boulder is elevated above the surrounding ground by its natural mass. It is designed for sitting, has a seat and a back and supports, and is intended for a single person. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet we should hesitate to call this boulder a chair; it is a shaped natural object repurposed for seating, not a piece of furniture in any ordinary sense. Calling it a chair stretches the concept beyond its natural boundaries.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the object be a manufactured artefact produced through a process of construction from assembled components, rather than a natural object that has been shaped or repurposed to serve a seating function.
passage: One might define a chair as a piece of furniture designed for sitting, having a seat, a back, and supports that elevate the seat above the ground, and intended for a single person at a time. This analysis appears to capture the central features of chairs as we ordinarily understand them. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from the domain of public design. Suppose an architect, commissioned to furnish a public park, designs a large, smooth boulder to serve as seating. The boulder is carefully shaped so that one person at a time may sit upon it comfortably. A contoured depression forms a natural seat, and a raised portion behind provides back support. The boulder is elevated above the surrounding ground by its own mass, resting upon a prepared base. It is explicitly designed for sitting, has a seat and a back and supports, and is intended for the use of a single individual. All three conditions of the proposed definition appear to be satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that we should be reluctant to call this boulder a chair. It is a shaped natural object repurposed for a seating function, not a piece of furniture in any ordinary sense of the term. To call it a chair seems to stretch the concept beyond its natural boundaries. It appears that the definition, by attending only to function, structure, and intended capacity, fails to require that the object be a manufactured artefact produced through a process of construction from assembled components. The chairhood of an object, it seems, depends not merely on what it does and how it is shaped but on what kind of thing it is and how it came to be.

definition: An object or performance W is a work of art if and only if (i) W is the product of intentional human creative activity, (ii) W is intended to be appreciated for its aesthetic qualities, and (iii) W belongs to a recognised artistic medium such as painting, sculpture, music, or literature.
conditions: W is the product of intentional human creative activity
W is intended to be appreciated for its aesthetic qualities
W belongs to a recognised artistic medium such as painting, sculpture, music, or literature
counterexample: Consider Marcel Duchamp's submission of a commercially manufactured urinal, signed with a pseudonym, to an art exhibition in 1917. The urinal was not the product of Duchamp's creative activity in the sense of having been crafted or shaped by him; it was a mass-produced industrial object. Duchamp's intention was not primarily that it be appreciated for its aesthetic qualities but rather that it provoke reflection on what constitutes art. And a urinal does not belong to any recognised artistic medium. None of the three conditions is straightforwardly satisfied. Yet the work has been widely accepted as one of the most significant artworks of the twentieth century.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the institutional and contextual dimensions of art — specifically, that an object may be a work of art by virtue of being presented and received within an art-world context that confers the status of art upon it, regardless of its medium, its aesthetic properties, or the manner of its production.
passage: One might propose that a work of art is the product of intentional human creative activity, intended to be appreciated for its aesthetic qualities, and belonging to a recognised artistic medium. This characterisation captures much of what we ordinarily associate with art: the painter's canvas, the sculptor's marble, the composer's symphony. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of the avant-garde. In 1917, a commercially manufactured porcelain urinal was submitted to an art exhibition under a pseudonym. The object was not the product of creative activity in the sense of having been crafted or shaped by the person who submitted it; it was a mass-produced industrial article selected from a plumber's showroom. The intention behind the submission was not primarily that the object be appreciated for its aesthetic qualities — though it has since been praised for the elegance of its form — but rather that it provoke reflection on the very concept of art. And a porcelain urinal does not belong to any recognised artistic medium: it is neither painting, nor sculpture in the traditional sense, nor music, nor literature. None of the three conditions of the proposed definition is straightforwardly satisfied. Nevertheless, the work has been widely recognised as one of the most consequential artworks of the twentieth century. It appears that the definition, by requiring creative production, aesthetic intention, and a recognised medium, fails to account for the institutional dimension of art — the fact that an object may become a work of art by virtue of being presented and received within an art-world context that confers the status of art upon it, irrespective of its origin, its medium, or the aesthetic qualities it may or may not possess.

definition: A statement S made by a speaker A to a hearer B is a lie if and only if (i) A believes that S is false, (ii) A intends that B believe S to be true, and (iii) A asserts S in a context in which B has a reasonable expectation of truthfulness.
conditions: A believes that S is false
A intends that B believe S to be true
A asserts S in a context in which B has a reasonable expectation of truthfulness
counterexample: Suppose a physician, believing that a terminally ill patient cannot withstand the shock of a truthful prognosis, tells the patient that his condition is improving. The physician believes this statement to be false and intends that the patient believe it to be true. The medical consultation is a context in which the patient has a reasonable expectation of truthfulness. All three conditions are met. Now suppose the physician's diagnosis was incorrect and the patient's condition is in fact improving, though the physician does not know this. The physician has made a statement she believes to be false, with the intent to deceive, in a context of reasonable expectation. Yet the statement turns out to be true. One might question whether a true statement can be a lie, even when all three stated conditions are satisfied.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the statement S actually be false, relying instead solely on the speaker's belief about its falsity; it thus classifies as lies certain statements that are in fact true, which is at odds with the intuition that a lie involves the assertion of something that is not the case.
passage: One might define a lie as a statement that the speaker believes to be false, intends the hearer to accept as true, and asserts in a context where the hearer has a reasonable expectation of truthfulness. This formulation attempts to capture the deceptive character of lying, the communicative intention behind it, and the normative setting in which it occurs. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from medical practice. Suppose a physician, believing that a terminally ill patient cannot withstand the shock of a candid prognosis, tells the patient that his condition is improving. The physician believes the statement to be false and intends the patient to accept it as true. The medical consultation is plainly a context in which the patient has a reasonable expectation of being told the truth. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Now suppose, however, that the physician's original diagnosis was mistaken and that the patient's condition is in fact improving, though the physician does not know this at the time of her assertion. The physician has made a statement she believes to be false, with the intent that the hearer accept it, in a context of reasonable expectation — yet the statement is in fact true. One might observe that there is a strong intuition that a lie must involve the assertion of something that is actually not the case, not merely something the speaker takes to be false. It appears that the definition, by resting entirely on the speaker's belief about the falsity of the statement, fails to require that the statement actually be false, and thereby classifies as lies certain true assertions made in bad faith.

definition: An utterance U by a speaker A constitutes a promise if and only if (i) U expresses A's commitment to perform a future action, (ii) A intends to carry out the action at the time of uttering U, and (iii) U is directed at a specific person or group who is thereby given a claim upon A's performance.
conditions: U expresses A's commitment to perform a future action
A intends to carry out the action at the time of uttering U
U is directed at a specific person or group who is thereby given a claim upon A's performance
counterexample: Suppose an actor, performing a scene in a theatrical production, speaks the line 'I promise to repay every penny by Friday' to another actor on stage. The utterance expresses a commitment to perform a future action. The actor, being a skilled and sincere performer, may even momentarily intend to carry out the action as part of his method of inhabiting the role. And the utterance is directed at a specific person — the other actor — who, within the fiction, is given a claim upon performance. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet we should not regard this as a genuine promise, for the utterance is made within a theatrical context in which all parties understand that no real obligations are being created.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the utterance be made in a context in which the speaker and hearer mutually understand it as creating a genuine obligation, as distinct from a merely performative, fictional, or ceremonial context in which the form of promising is enacted without the substance.
passage: One might characterise a promise as an utterance expressing a commitment to perform a future action, made with the intention to carry it out, and directed at a specific person who is thereby given a claim upon performance. This analysis captures the forward-looking, intentional, and interpersonal character of promising. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the theatre. Suppose an actor, performing a scene in a stage production, delivers the line 'I promise to repay every penny by Friday' to another actor. The utterance plainly expresses a commitment to perform a future action. The actor, following a method of deep immersion in his role, may even form a momentary sincere intention to carry out the repayment as part of his inhabitation of the character. And the utterance is directed at a specific person — the other actor — who, within the dramatic fiction, is given a claim upon the performance. All three conditions of the proposed definition appear to be satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that we should not regard this as a genuine promise.
