The utterance is made within a theatrical context in which all parties understand that no real obligations are being created. The form of promising is enacted, but the substance is absent. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the content of the utterance, the speaker's intention, and the directedness toward a recipient, fails to require that the utterance be made in a context mutually understood by speaker and hearer as one in which genuine obligations are being undertaken.

definition: An object V is a vehicle if and only if (i) V is a conveyance designed to transport persons or goods from one location to another, (ii) V is capable of self-propelled or externally propelled locomotion, and (iii) V operates on or above a surface or through a medium.
conditions: V is a conveyance designed to transport persons or goods from one location to another
V is capable of self-propelled or externally propelled locomotion
V operates on or above a surface or through a medium
counterexample: Consider a large industrial conveyor belt in a factory, designed to transport goods continuously from one end of a production line to the other. The conveyor belt is a conveyance designed to move goods from one location to another. It is capable of locomotion, being propelled by an electric motor. And it operates on a surface — the factory floor. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call a conveyor belt a vehicle. It is a fixed installation, not the kind of mobile, self-contained conveyance that the concept of a vehicle ordinarily picks out.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the conveyance be a discrete, mobile unit that itself changes location during transport, rather than a stationary mechanism that moves goods along a fixed path while remaining in place.
passage: One might define a vehicle as a conveyance designed to transport persons or goods from one location to another, capable of self-propelled or externally propelled locomotion, and operating on or above a surface or through a medium. This characterisation appears to capture cars, ships, aeroplanes, and the other paradigmatic instances of vehicles. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from industrial engineering. Consider a large conveyor belt in a factory, designed to transport manufactured goods continuously from one end of a production line to the other. The conveyor belt is a conveyance expressly designed to move goods from one location to another. It is capable of locomotion, being propelled by an electric motor that drives the belt along its course. And it operates on the factory floor — a surface. All three conditions of the proposed definition appear to be satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that we should be reluctant to call a conveyor belt a vehicle. It is a fixed installation, bolted to the factory floor, that moves goods along a predetermined path while itself remaining stationary. It lacks the mobility and discreteness that we associate with vehicles: a vehicle is the kind of thing that itself goes somewhere, carrying its cargo with it. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the object be a conveyance capable of propelled locomotion on a surface, fails to demand that the conveyance be a discrete, mobile unit that changes its own location during the act of transport.

definition: An object T is a tool if and only if (i) T is used by an agent to accomplish a task that the agent could not accomplish, or could not accomplish as effectively, by bodily action alone, (ii) T is a physical artefact, and (iii) T is manipulated by the agent during the performance of the task.
conditions: T is used by an agent to accomplish a task that the agent could not accomplish, or could not accomplish as effectively, by bodily action alone
T is a physical artefact
T is manipulated by the agent during the performance of the task
counterexample: Suppose a farmer places a scarecrow in a field to frighten away birds from his crops. The scarecrow is a physical artefact. It is used by the farmer to accomplish a task — protecting the crops — that he could not accomplish as effectively by bodily action alone, since he cannot stand in the field all day. Yet the scarecrow is not manipulated by the farmer during the performance of the task; once placed, it operates without any handling or guidance. All three conditions are not jointly met, and yet one might observe that the scarecrow functions as a tool of crop protection in a perfectly natural sense of the term.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate tools that operate passively or autonomously once deployed, requiring active manipulation during the task when in fact many tools — traps, barriers, alarms — accomplish their function without continuous handling by the agent.
passage: One might propose that a tool is a physical artefact used by an agent to accomplish a task that could not be accomplished, or not accomplished as effectively, by bodily action alone, and that is manipulated by the agent during performance of the task. This characterisation captures the paradigmatic instances of tool use — a hammer wielded to drive a nail, a saw drawn through wood, a pen guided across paper. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from agricultural practice. Suppose a farmer constructs a scarecrow and places it in a field to frighten away birds that would otherwise consume his crops. The scarecrow is a physical artefact. It is used by the farmer to accomplish a task — the protection of his crops — that he could not accomplish as effectively by bodily action alone, for he cannot stand in the field from dawn to dusk. Yet the scarecrow is not manipulated by the farmer during the performance of its task; once placed, it operates entirely without handling or guidance, performing its function through its mere presence. The third condition is not satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the scarecrow functions as a tool of crop protection in a perfectly natural sense of the word. It appears that the definition, by requiring active manipulation during the task, is too narrow to accommodate the many tools that operate passively or autonomously once deployed — traps that spring without guidance, barriers that block without handling, alarms that sound without intervention. The concept of a tool, it seems, extends beyond the hand-wielded instrument to include artefacts that accomplish their function through deployment rather than continuous manipulation.

definition: A system of communication L is a language if and only if (i) L employs a finite set of discrete symbols, (ii) the symbols of L can be combined according to syntactic rules to produce an unlimited number of meaningful expressions, and (iii) L is used by a community of speakers to convey thoughts, desires, and information.
conditions: L employs a finite set of discrete symbols
The symbols of L can be combined according to syntactic rules to produce an unlimited number of meaningful expressions
L is used by a community of speakers to convey thoughts, desires, and information
counterexample: Consider the formal system of first-order predicate logic. It employs a finite set of discrete symbols — variables, quantifiers, connectives, and predicate letters. These symbols can be combined according to precise syntactic rules to produce an unlimited number of well-formed formulae. And the system is used by a community — logicians and mathematicians — to convey thoughts and information about the logical structure of arguments. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call first-order predicate logic a language in the ordinary sense. It is a formal calculus, lacking the expressive richness, ambiguity, pragmatic flexibility, and capacity for figurative speech that characterise natural languages.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the system possess the pragmatic and expressive capacities characteristic of natural language — including the ability to perform diverse speech acts, to accommodate ambiguity and context-dependence, and to be acquired naturally by speakers in a community — thereby failing to distinguish formal calculi from genuine languages.
passage: One might define a language as a system of communication employing a finite set of discrete symbols that can be combined according to syntactic rules to produce an unlimited number of meaningful expressions, used by a community to convey thoughts, desires, and information. This formulation captures the combinatorial productivity, the symbolic character, and the communal function that we associate with language. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the domain of formal systems. Consider first-order predicate logic. It employs a finite set of discrete symbols — variables, quantifiers, connectives, predicate letters, and punctuation marks. These symbols can be combined according to precise formation rules to generate an unlimited number of well-formed formulae, each with a determinate semantic interpretation. And the system is used by a community — logicians, mathematicians, and philosophers — to convey thoughts and information about the logical structure of arguments and the validity of inferences. All three conditions of the proposed definition appear to be satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that we should be reluctant to call first-order predicate logic a language in the ordinary philosophical sense of the term. It is a formal calculus, devoid of the expressive richness, the pragmatic flexibility, the capacity for figurative and indirect speech, and the context-dependence that characterise natural languages. It appears that the definition, by requiring only a finite symbol set, syntactic combinability, and communal use for communication, fails to distinguish between formal calculi and genuine languages, and that what is missing is the requirement of the pragmatic and expressive capacities that natural language uniquely possesses.

definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A has a defined goal or objective that participants aim to achieve, (ii) A is governed by rules that constrain the means by which the goal may be pursued, and (iii) the outcome of A is uncertain at the outset.
conditions: A has a defined goal or objective that participants aim to achieve
A is governed by rules that constrain the means by which the goal may be pursued
The outcome of A is uncertain at the outset
counterexample: Consider a case in which a grandmaster plays a game of chess against a novice who has learned the rules only an hour before. The activity has a defined objective — to checkmate the opponent's king. It is governed by the rules of chess. Yet the outcome is not genuinely uncertain at the outset: the grandmaster will win with near certainty. The third condition fails, and yet we should not deny that this is a game of chess. Conversely, consider a courtroom trial: it has a defined objective, is governed by rules constraining how it may be pursued, and its outcome is uncertain. Yet a trial is not a game.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the activity be set apart from the serious conduct of life — that it be undertaken within a domain recognised by participants as distinct from practical, moral, or legal obligation — thereby conflating games with rule-governed, goal-directed activities of a wholly serious character.
passage: One might propose that a game is an activity with a defined goal, governed by rules constraining the means of pursuit, and characterised by uncertainty of outcome. This formulation captures the goal-directedness, rule-governance, and suspense that we associate with many familiar games. Yet cases which reveal the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from both directions. Suppose a grandmaster sits down to play chess against a novice who learned the rules only an hour earlier. The activity has a defined goal — to checkmate the opponent's king. It is governed by the rules of chess, which constrain the permissible moves. Yet the outcome is not genuinely uncertain: the grandmaster will win with near certainty, and both parties are aware of this. The third condition appears not to be met, and yet one might observe that we should not hesitate to call this a game of chess. Now consider, from the opposite direction, a courtroom trial. The trial has a defined objective — the determination of guilt or innocence. It is governed by elaborate procedural rules constraining how evidence may be presented and arguments advanced. And its outcome is uncertain at the outset. All three conditions are satisfied, yet a courtroom trial is not a game. It appears that the definition, by requiring only a goal, rules, and uncertainty, cannot distinguish games from other rule-governed, goal-directed, uncertain activities. What is missing is the requirement that the activity be set apart from the serious conduct of life — that it be undertaken within a domain that participants recognise as distinct from practical, moral, or legal obligation.

definition: An object T is a tool if and only if (i) T has been designed or selected for the purpose of performing a specific type of task, (ii) T extends or augments the physical capacities of the user, and (iii) T is portable and can be carried to the site of use.
conditions: T has been designed or selected for the purpose of performing a specific type of task
T extends or augments the physical capacities of the user
T is portable and can be carried to the site of use
counterexample: Consider a pair of reading spectacles. Spectacles have been designed for the specific task of correcting vision. They extend the physical capacities of the user by enabling the perception of fine detail that would otherwise be inaccessible. And they are portable, easily carried to any location. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet spectacles are not ordinarily classified as tools; they are personal accessories or medical devices. One does not say that a person reading a book is 'using a tool' in the same sense as a carpenter wielding a chisel.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the object be used to act upon or manipulate some external material or object, rather than merely enhancing the user's own perceptual or cognitive faculties; it thereby classifies as tools items that serve a corrective or augmentative function directed inward rather than outward.
passage: One might define a tool as an object designed or selected for a specific task, that extends the physical capacities of the user, and that is portable. This characterisation appears to encompass hammers, screwdrivers, and saws, and to capture the functional, augmentative, and mobile character of tools. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from everyday experience. Consider a pair of reading spectacles. They have been designed for the specific task of correcting impaired vision. They extend the physical capacities of the user by enabling the perception of fine print and small detail that would otherwise be inaccessible. And they are eminently portable, carried in a pocket or worn upon the face with ease. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that spectacles are not ordinarily classified as tools. They are personal accessories, medical devices, or aids to perception, but not tools in the sense in which a hammer or a saw is a tool. One does not say of a person reading a book that she is 'using a tool.' It appears that the definition, by requiring only task-specificity, capacity-extension, and portability, fails to require that the object be used to act upon or manipulate some external material or entity. Tools, in the ordinary sense, are directed outward — toward wood, metal, earth, or other materials — whereas spectacles serve a corrective function directed inward, enhancing the user's own perceptual faculties rather than enabling action upon the external world.

definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is governed by a set of rules that participants voluntarily accept, (ii) A has a determinate outcome or goal toward which participants direct their efforts, and (iii) A is undertaken primarily for the purpose of amusement or recreation.
conditions: A is governed by a set of rules that participants voluntarily accept
A has a determinate outcome or goal toward which participants direct their efforts
A is undertaken primarily for the purpose of amusement or recreation
counterexample: Consider a case in which professional chess players compete in a tournament for a substantial monetary prize. The activity is governed by rules that the players voluntarily accept, and it has a determinate outcome — one player wins, the others lose. Yet the participants do not undertake the activity primarily for amusement or recreation; they do so for professional advancement, financial gain, and competitive prestige. For many such players, the experience is marked more by anxiety and exhaustion than by enjoyment. Condition (iii) appears to fail, and yet no one would hesitate to call chess a game.
missing_condition: The definition must allow for activities that possess the structural features of games — rules, competition, determinate outcomes — regardless of the participants' primary motivations
passage: It has seemed natural to many who have reflected upon the concept of a game to identify three conditions that are jointly sufficient for an activity to count as one: it must be rule-governed, it must be directed toward a determinate outcome, and it must be pursued primarily for the sake of amusement. These conditions appear to capture the essential character of games as we ordinarily understand them — the child's board-game, the garden party's round of croquet, the Sunday afternoon cricket match. Yet one may observe that certain activities which we unhesitatingly classify as games fail, upon examination, to satisfy the third condition. Let us consider the case of professional chess. The grandmasters who compete in international tournaments accept the rules of chess voluntarily and direct their efforts toward a determinate outcome — victory. But their primary purpose in engaging in the activity is not amusement. They play for their livelihood, for ranking points, for the esteem of their peers, and for the not inconsiderable prize money. The experience of playing at the highest level is frequently described as gruelling rather than pleasurable, marked by intense concentration, psychological strain, and physical fatigue extending over many hours. It would be quite misleading to say that such players are primarily seeking recreation. And yet chess remains paradigmatically a game; indeed, it is often cited as the very exemplar of the concept. The case suggests that the proposed analysis is too narrow in its third condition, and that what makes an activity a game is not the motivations with which it is pursued but rather its structural features — the presence of rules, of competition, and of determinate outcomes. A satisfactory definition must accommodate the professional and the amateur alike, without requiring that amusement be the primary end.

definition: An object O is a chair if and only if (i) O is a piece of furniture designed for sitting, (ii) O has a seat, a back, and supports that raise it from the ground, and (iii) O is intended for the use of a single person at a time.
conditions: O is a piece of furniture designed for sitting
O has a seat, a back, and supports that raise it from the ground
O is intended for the use of a single person at a time
counterexample: Consider a case in which a large flat-topped boulder in a garden has been adopted by its owner as his customary seat. He has placed a cushion upon it, and he regularly retires to it for reading in the afternoon. The boulder serves the function of sitting; it provides a seat surface and is raised from the surrounding ground by its own bulk; one might even say that its broad, slightly concave upper surface furnishes a rudimentary back-rest. It is used by a single person. Yet it is not a piece of furniture, was not designed for sitting, and possesses no supports in the ordinary sense. The conditions are approximately but not properly satisfied, and yet the owner and his visitors commonly refer to it as 'his chair.'
missing_condition: The definition must address the distinction between objects designed and manufactured as chairs and objects that merely serve the function of a chair by adoption or convention
passage: The concept of a chair may appear too humble to warrant philosophical scrutiny, yet it proves surprisingly resistant to precise analysis. One might propose that a chair is a piece of furniture designed for sitting, possessing a seat, a back, and supports that raise it from the ground, and intended for the use of a single person. This definition seems to capture the paradigmatic cases well enough — the kitchen chair, the armchair, the office chair on its swivelling base. Yet one may observe that our ordinary use of the word extends to objects that satisfy these conditions only loosely, if at all. Suppose that a gentleman who is fond of reading discovers in his garden a large, flat-topped boulder of convenient height. He places a cushion upon it, makes a habit of sitting there each afternoon, and comes to regard it as his accustomed seat. The boulder serves the function of sitting; its upper surface is broad and slightly concave, furnishing something that might pass for a seat and even a rudimentary back-rest where the stone rises at one edge; and it is raised from the surrounding ground by its own mass. It is used by a single person. His visitors, upon being directed to 'his chair,' understand perfectly well what is meant. Yet the boulder is not a piece of furniture in any ordinary sense. It was not designed for sitting — it was not designed at all — and it possesses no supports in the sense that the legs of a chair are supports. The conditions of the proposed definition are at best approximately satisfied, yet the word 'chair' is applied without strain. This suggests that our concept of a chair is governed not by strict necessary and sufficient conditions but by something more flexible — a functional criterion, perhaps, that permits objects to qualify as chairs when they serve the relevant purpose, whether or not they conform to the structural specifications of the proposed analysis.

definition: An object or performance X is a work of art if and only if (i) X is the product of intentional human creative activity, (ii) X is intended to be appreciated for its aesthetic qualities, and (iii) X belongs to a recognised artistic medium such as painting, sculpture, music, or literature.
conditions: X is the product of intentional human creative activity
X is intended to be appreciated for its aesthetic qualities
X belongs to a recognised artistic medium such as painting, sculpture, music, or literature
counterexample: Consider a case in which an artist exhibits, in a gallery, a manufactured bottle-rack purchased from a department store. The bottle-rack is not the product of the exhibitor's creative activity — he did not make it — and it was not manufactured with the intention of being appreciated for its aesthetic qualities. Nor does it belong to any recognised artistic medium in the traditional sense. Yet it has been received by critics and the public as a work of art, discussed in journals of aesthetics, and displayed in museums of modern art for decades. All three conditions fail, and yet the object is, by widespread consensus, a work of art.
missing_condition: The definition must account for the role of institutional or contextual factors — such as presentation in an art-world context — in conferring the status of art upon an object
passage: The question of what constitutes a work of art has exercised aestheticians considerably, and a familiar first attempt at an answer proceeds along the following lines: a work of art is the product of intentional human creative activity, intended to be appreciated for its aesthetic qualities, and belonging to a recognised artistic medium. This analysis has the advantage of capturing what we ordinarily mean when we speak of paintings, symphonies, novels, and sculptures. The artist creates; the audience appreciates; and the work takes its place within an established tradition of artistic production. Yet one may observe that the history of art in the twentieth century furnishes cases that resist this analysis in the most striking fashion. Suppose an artist enters a department store, purchases an ordinary manufactured bottle-rack of the kind used in commercial kitchens, transports it to a gallery, places it upon a pedestal, and signs it. The bottle-rack is emphatically not the product of the exhibitor's creative activity in any ordinary sense — he did not design it, fabricate it, or alter it in any material way. It was manufactured for a purely utilitarian purpose and was never intended to be appreciated for aesthetic qualities. And it belongs to no recognised artistic medium: it is neither painting nor sculpture nor music nor literature. By the terms of the proposed definition, it is not a work of art. Yet it has been exhibited in galleries, discussed in learned journals, reproduced in books on modern art, and treated by the art-world as a significant artistic achievement for the better part of a century. The case reveals that the proposed conditions, however plausible they may seem when applied to traditional works, are insufficient to account for the ways in which objects acquire the status of art. What appears to be missing is some recognition of the institutional or contextual dimension — the role of galleries, critics, and the art-world in conferring artistic status upon objects that possess none of the qualities traditionally associated with art.

definition: A statement S made by a speaker A to an audience B is a lie if and only if (i) A believes that S is false, (ii) A intends B to believe that S is true, and (iii) A asserts S in a context where sincerity is expected.
conditions: A believes that S is false
A intends B to believe that S is true
A asserts S in a context where sincerity is expected
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man, believing that his friend's new hat is hideous, says 'What a splendid hat!' in a tone of such exaggerated enthusiasm that both parties understand the remark as ironic. The speaker believes the statement to be false, and the context — a friendly conversation — is one in which sincerity is ordinarily expected. Yet the speaker does not intend his friend to believe the hat is splendid; he intends the irony to be recognised, and it is. Condition (ii) appears to fail, yet many would say the remark is not a lie precisely because of this. The difficulty is that the definition gives the right result here but for the wrong reason — it classifies the ironic remark as a non-lie because condition (ii) fails, when in fact the deeper reason is that the communicative act is not a genuine assertion at all.
missing_condition: The definition must distinguish genuine assertions from ironic, performative, or otherwise non-literal uses of language in which the appearance of assertion is present but the illocutionary force is different
passage: The concept of a lie has seemed to admit of a tolerably clear analysis: one lies when one asserts what one believes to be false, with the intention that the audience should accept it as true, in a context where truthfulness is the norm. These conditions capture the central cases well enough — the child who denies having eaten the biscuit, the witness who misrepresents what he saw. Yet the analysis proves less satisfactory when applied to certain common forms of speech in which falsehood is uttered but lying, in the fullest sense, does not occur. Suppose a man, upon being shown his friend's new hat — a hat he privately considers quite dreadful — exclaims with theatrical enthusiasm, 'What a splendid hat!' His tone is so exaggerated, his manner so obviously performative, that both parties understand the remark as ironic. The speaker believes the statement to be false, satisfying condition (i). The context is a friendly conversation in which sincerity is ordinarily expected, satisfying condition (iii). Yet he does not intend his friend to believe that the hat is splendid; he intends the irony to be transparent, and it is. Condition (ii) is thus unsatisfied, and the definition correctly classifies the remark as something other than a lie. But the correctness of this verdict is, one might say, somewhat accidental. The deeper reason the remark is not a lie is that it is not a genuine assertion — it is an ironic performance, a communicative act whose illocutionary force is quite different from that of a straightforward assertion. The definition, by focusing upon the speaker's intention to deceive, arrives at the right classification but misses the underlying explanation. A more adequate analysis would need to incorporate a condition distinguishing genuine assertions from ironic, performative, and otherwise non-literal uses of language, so that the exclusion of such cases rests upon the correct grounds.

definition: A speech act S by a speaker A constitutes a promise if and only if (i) A undertakes an obligation to perform some future action φ, (ii) A communicates this undertaking to a specific addressee B, and (iii) B accepts the undertaking.
conditions: A undertakes an obligation to perform some future action φ
A communicates this undertaking to a specific addressee B
B accepts the undertaking
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man, in a fit of remorse at his dying father's bedside, says 'I promise I shall look after your garden.' The father, deeply sedated, gives no indication of hearing the words and dies shortly afterwards without regaining consciousness. The son has undertaken an obligation — he feels it keenly — and he has communicated it to a specific addressee, namely his father. Yet the father has not accepted the undertaking in any recognisable sense; he may not even have perceived it. Condition (iii) fails, and yet the son and those who witness the scene would say, without hesitation, that a promise has been made.
missing_condition: The definition must allow for promises that are binding even in the absence of the addressee's acceptance, such as deathbed promises or promises made to those unable to respond
passage: The making of a promise has seemed to many philosophers to require three things: that the speaker undertake an obligation to perform some future action, that this undertaking be communicated to an addressee, and that the addressee accept it. When all three conditions are satisfied — as when one man promises another to return a borrowed book, and the other nods in acknowledgement — the case is unproblematic. The obligation is real, the communication is clear, and the acceptance is manifest. Yet one may construct a case which calls the necessity of the third condition into serious doubt. Suppose a man sits at the bedside of his dying father, who lies heavily sedated and is, by all appearances, insensible to his surroundings. The son, moved by grief and a sense of duty, says aloud: 'I promise I shall look after your garden when you are gone.' The father gives no sign of hearing; he does not stir, does not speak, does not so much as open his eyes. He dies within the hour without regaining consciousness. Now, the son has undertaken an obligation to perform a future action — the tending of the garden. He has communicated this undertaking to a specific addressee — his father. But the father has not accepted the undertaking, for he has not perceived it. Condition (iii) is plainly unsatisfied. And yet it would seem most strange to deny that a promise has been made. The son feels bound by it; those who witnessed the scene regard it as a genuine promise; and there is a long tradition, both moral and legal, of treating deathbed promises as among the most solemn of obligations. One is therefore led to suspect that the acceptance of the addressee, however natural it may seem as a condition, is not in fact necessary for the making of a promise. The obligation, it appears, can be incurred unilaterally, by the act of undertaking itself, without requiring the concurrence of the party to whom it is directed.

definition: An object O is a vehicle if and only if (i) O is designed for the transportation of persons or goods, (ii) O is capable of self-propelled movement or is drawn by an external motive force, and (iii) O travels along or over the surface of the earth.
conditions: O is designed for the transportation of persons or goods
O is capable of self-propelled movement or is drawn by an external motive force
O travels along or over the surface of the earth
counterexample: Consider a case in which a large helium balloon is fitted with a basket and used to transport passengers across a valley. The balloon is designed for the transportation of persons, satisfying condition (i). It is drawn aloft by an external motive force — the buoyancy of the gas — and carried by the wind, which may count as an external force under a generous reading, satisfying condition (ii). Yet the balloon does not travel along or over the surface of the earth; it travels through the air at considerable altitude. Condition (iii) fails, yet no ordinary speaker would hesitate to call the balloon a vehicle.
missing_condition: The definition must not restrict vehicles to surface travel but must encompass objects that transport persons or goods through any medium, including air and water
passage: One might propose that a vehicle is an object designed for transportation, capable of movement whether by its own power or by an external force, and travelling along or over the earth's surface. This definition appears to accommodate the motor-car, the horse-drawn carriage, the railway locomotive, and the bicycle with equal facility. Yet a moment's reflection reveals that our ordinary concept of a vehicle extends well beyond surface travel. Suppose a large helium balloon is fitted with a wicker basket and employed to convey passengers across a mountain valley. The balloon is manifestly designed for the transportation of persons; it is borne aloft by the buoyancy of its gas and carried laterally by the wind — forces that are, in a broad sense, external motive agencies. It satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) on any reasonable construction of those requirements. But it does not travel along or over the surface of the earth; it travels through the atmosphere at a height of several hundred feet. Condition (iii) is unsatisfied. Yet one would be hard pressed to deny that the balloon is a vehicle. It transports passengers from one place to another, and its passengers speak of 'travelling' and of their 'conveyance' without any sense of impropriety. The same may be said of boats, submarines, and aircraft, all of which we unhesitatingly call vehicles despite the fact that they do not travel along the earth's surface. The proposed analysis is therefore too restrictive in its third condition, and a satisfactory account must allow that vehicles may operate in any medium — on land, on or under water, or through the air — provided they serve the function of transporting persons or goods.

definition: An object O is a tool if and only if (i) O is a physical artefact, (ii) O is designed or selected for the performance of a specific practical task, and (iii) O extends or augments the physical capacities of its user.
conditions: O is a physical artefact
O is designed or selected for the performance of a specific practical task
O extends or augments the physical capacities of its user
counterexample: Consider a case in which a carpenter's spirit level is used to determine whether a surface is horizontal. The spirit level is a physical artefact, designed for a specific practical task, and it is universally regarded as a tool. Yet it does not extend or augment the physical capacities of its user — it does not enable him to exert greater force, reach higher, or move faster. Rather, it augments his perceptual or cognitive capacities by providing information that his unaided senses cannot reliably furnish. Condition (iii) is not satisfied on any natural reading that restricts augmentation to physical capacities, and yet the spirit level is unquestionably a tool.
missing_condition: The definition must extend the notion of augmentation to include perceptual, cognitive, and informational capacities, not merely physical ones
passage: The concept of a tool is commonly analysed in terms of three conditions: the object must be a physical artefact, it must be designed or selected for a practical task, and it must extend or augment the physical capacities of its user. A hammer augments the force of the blow; a lever extends the reach and the mechanical advantage of the arm; a saw enables the cutting of materials that the hand alone could not sever. These are paradigmatic tools, and the proposed analysis captures them admirably. Yet there exists a large and familiar class of objects that we ordinarily call tools but that do not, upon reflection, augment any physical capacity of the user. Consider the carpenter's spirit level — a simple device consisting of a sealed tube of liquid with an air bubble. It is a physical artefact; it is designed for a specific practical task, namely determining whether a surface is truly horizontal; and it is found in every carpenter's kit, as indispensable as the hammer and the chisel. Yet what does it augment? Not the carpenter's strength, nor his reach, nor his speed. The spirit level provides information — it tells him something about the world that his unaided senses cannot reliably determine. Its contribution is perceptual or cognitive rather than physical; it augments his capacity to make accurate judgements about the orientation of surfaces. If condition (iii) is read as requiring the augmentation of physical capacities specifically, then the spirit level fails to qualify, which is an unwelcome result. The case suggests that the analysis is too narrow in its conception of what tools do for their users, and that a satisfactory definition must recognise that tools may augment perceptual, cognitive, and informational capacities no less than physical ones.

definition: A system of communication C is a language if and only if (i) C employs a finite set of discrete symbols, (ii) C possesses a grammar that generates an infinite number of well-formed expressions from those symbols, and (iii) C is used by a community of speakers for the purpose of conveying meaning.
conditions: C employs a finite set of discrete symbols
C possesses a grammar that generates an infinite number of well-formed expressions from those symbols
C is used by a community of speakers for the purpose of conveying meaning
counterexample: Consider a case in which the last surviving speaker of an isolated dialect dies, leaving behind a collection of written texts and a partial grammar compiled by a visiting linguist. The system has a finite set of discrete symbols — its alphabet and vocabulary — and a grammar capable of generating infinitely many well-formed sentences. Yet there is no longer a community of speakers who use it for communication. Condition (iii) fails, and yet we should not wish to say that the dialect has ceased to be a language at the moment of the last speaker's death. It remains, by any reasonable standard, a language — albeit a dead one.
missing_condition: The definition must account for languages that are no longer actively spoken but retain their systematic and structural character, distinguishing active use from inherent linguistic status
passage: It has seemed to many theorists that a language may be adequately defined as a system of communication that employs a finite stock of discrete symbols, possesses a generative grammar, and is used by a community of speakers to convey meaning. This definition captures much of what is distinctive about human language — its combinatorial richness, its conventional character, and its social function. English, French, and Mandarin are languages on this account, while the cries of animals and the signals of traffic lights are not. Yet the analysis encounters a difficulty when applied to cases of language death. Suppose that an isolated dialect, spoken for centuries by a small community on a remote island, loses its last native speaker. The old woman who spoke it has died, and no one remains who uses the system for daily communication. There exist, however, a number of written texts in the dialect, together with a partial grammar compiled by a visiting linguist some years before. The system still possesses a finite set of discrete symbols and a grammar capable of generating infinitely many well-formed sentences. But condition (iii) is no longer satisfied: there is no community of speakers who use the system for the purpose of conveying meaning. If we accept the proposed definition, we must say that the dialect ceased to be a language at the precise moment of the last speaker's death. But this seems wrong. The dialect is a language — a dead language, to be sure, but a language nonetheless. Latin is a language, though it has not been anyone's native tongue for many centuries. The case reveals that the proposed analysis conflates the active use of a language with its status as a language, and that some revision is needed to accommodate systems that retain their structural character and their capacity for meaningful expression even after the community that once used them has vanished.

definition: A statement S is a lie if and only if (i) S is a declarative assertion made by a speaker A, (ii) A believes S to be false at the time of utterance, and (iii) A makes S with the intent to cause the hearer to form a false belief.
conditions: S is a declarative assertion made by a speaker A
A believes S to be false at the time of utterance
A makes S with the intent to cause the hearer to form a false belief
counterexample: Consider a case in which a doctor, believing that her patient has a terminal illness, tells him 'You are going to be fine' with the intent that he should believe this falsehood and thereby remain calm enough to undergo a necessary procedure. She satisfies all three conditions: she makes a declarative assertion, she believes it to be false, and she intends the patient to form a false belief. The statement is, by this analysis, a lie. Yet suppose the doctor is mistaken in her diagnosis and the patient is in fact going to be fine. She has asserted something she believed false, with deceptive intent, and yet the content of her assertion is true. Must we say she has lied, even though what she said was true?
missing_condition: The definition must address whether the actual falsity of the statement is required for a lie, or whether the speaker's belief in its falsity is sufficient regardless of the truth of the matter
passage: The analysis of lying would seem straightforward: a lie is a declarative assertion that the speaker believes to be false, uttered with the intent to produce a false belief in the hearer. These three conditions appear both necessary and jointly sufficient. The dishonest witness who testifies to what he knows is untrue, the child who denies the misdeed he knows he committed — each satisfies the conditions and is, on any ordinary reckoning, lying. Yet there are cases that bring to light a peculiar tension within the analysis. Imagine a doctor who, upon examining her patient, forms the sincere belief that he is suffering from a terminal condition. Wishing to keep him calm for an urgent surgical procedure, she tells him, in measured tones, that he is going to be fine. She makes a declarative assertion; she believes it to be false; and she intends the patient to form a false belief — namely, the belief that he will recover. All three conditions are satisfied, and the definition classifies her statement as a lie. But now let us suppose that the doctor's diagnosis is mistaken. Subsequent tests reveal that the patient's condition is entirely benign, and he is, in point of fact, going to be fine. The content of the doctor's assertion turns out to be true. She has said something she believed false, with the intention of deceiving, and yet the proposition expressed is in fact correct. One may ask whether the doctor has lied. The proposed definition says she has, since it attends to the speaker's belief and intention rather than to the truth of the statement. But this verdict is by no means uncontroversial. There is a strong intuition that a lie must involve the assertion of something actually false, and that a merely believed-false statement that turns out to be true is something different — an attempted lie, perhaps, or a deception that falls short of mendacity. The case reveals an ambiguity in the analysis that calls for resolution: must a lie be actually false, or is the speaker's belief in its falsity sufficient?

definition: An object O is a tool if and only if (i) O is a portable physical object, (ii) O is used by an agent to bring about a desired change in the physical world, and (iii) O is manipulated by the hand or body of the agent during use.
conditions: O is a portable physical object
O is used by an agent to bring about a desired change in the physical world
O is manipulated by the hand or body of the agent during use
counterexample: Consider a case in which a farmer sets a trap for a fox. The trap is a physical object; it is used to bring about a desired change — the capture of the fox — and the farmer manipulates it with his hands when setting it. Yet once set, the trap operates without any bodily manipulation by the farmer; it catches the fox while the farmer sleeps. If condition (iii) requires manipulation during the use that produces the desired change, then the trap fails, for the relevant use — the capturing — occurs in the farmer's absence. Yet a trap is, by common consent, a tool.
missing_condition: The definition must allow for tools that operate autonomously after being set or configured by the agent, rather than requiring continuous bodily manipulation
passage: One might suppose that a tool is a portable physical object, manipulated by the hand or body of its user, employed to effect some desired change in the world. This analysis seems to fit the hammer, the screwdriver, and the paintbrush with admirable precision. The agent grasps the object, applies it to the task, and by manual effort produces the intended result. Yet consider the case of a trap — a device used by farmers, hunters, and pest-controllers since the earliest times. The farmer takes the trap in his hands, carries it to a suitable location, and sets it with care — manipulating it, as the definition requires, with his body. He then departs. The trap, left to itself in the hedgerow, catches a fox during the night while the farmer sleeps in his bed. The desired change in the physical world — the capture of the fox — has been brought about by the trap, and the trap is certainly a portable physical object used by an agent for this purpose. But condition (iii) demands that the object be manipulated by the agent during use, and the use that actually produces the desired outcome occurs entirely without the farmer's bodily involvement. He manipulated the trap when setting it, but not when it performed its function. If we insist upon condition (iii) as stated, the trap is not a tool — a conclusion that conflicts sharply with ordinary usage and with the long history of regarding traps as among the most ancient and essential of human tools. The case suggests that the definition must be broadened to accommodate tools that are configured by the agent and then operate autonomously, effecting the desired change without continuous manual guidance.

definition: An activity is a game if and only if (i) it is governed by explicit rules, (ii) it involves two or more participants competing against one another, and (iii) it has a definite outcome in which one party wins and another loses.
conditions: The activity is governed by explicit rules
It involves two or more participants competing against one another
It has a definite outcome in which one party wins and another loses
counterexample: Suppose that a group of children engages in a ring-around-the-rosy game. The activity is governed by explicit rules: the children hold hands, circle, recite a specific verse, and all fall down at the appointed moment. Yet there is no competition between the participants and no outcome in which one party wins and another loses. The activity fails conditions (ii) and (iii). Nevertheless, it is paradigmatically a game, one of the first activities we teach children under that very name.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires competition and a win/lose outcome, excluding cooperative and non-competitive games that are governed by rules and engaged in for their own sake.
passage: The question of what constitutes a game has proven remarkably resistant to straightforward analysis. One natural proposal holds that a game is an activity governed by explicit rules, involving competition among two or more participants, and terminating in a definite outcome that distinguishes winners from losers. One might observe that this characterisation fits many familiar cases: chess, football, and card games of various sorts all satisfy these three conditions. Yet we should hesitate to regard the analysis as adequate. Consider the children's activity of ring-around-the-rosy. This pastime is governed by explicit rules: the participants join hands, form a circle, move in unison while reciting a prescribed verse, and all fall to the ground at the designated moment. The rules are understood and followed with considerable fidelity, even by very young children. Yet the activity involves no competition whatsoever. The children do not strive against one another, and there is no outcome in which one child is declared the winner and another the loser. The second and third conditions are plainly unsatisfied. Nevertheless, ring-around-the-rosy is paradigmatically a game; it is among the first activities we introduce to children under precisely that designation. The case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis, for it shows that competition and definite win-lose outcomes are not necessary features of games. What appears to be missing is a recognition that games may be cooperative or purely participatory.

definition: An object is a chair if and only if (i) it has a seat, (ii) it has a back, (iii) it is designed for a single person to sit upon, and (iv) it has legs that raise the seat off the ground.
conditions: The object has a seat
The object has a back
The object is designed for a single person to sit upon
The object has legs that raise the seat off the ground
counterexample: Consider a case in which a beanbag chair has a seat (the indentation formed when one sits upon it), has a back (the portion of the bag that rises behind the sitter and supports the torso), and is designed for a single person to sit upon. Yet it has no legs whatsoever; it rests directly upon the floor as an amorphous mass. The fourth condition fails, yet we unhesitatingly call it a chair, and it is marketed and purchased as such.
missing_condition: The definition unnecessarily requires legs, when what matters is merely that the object supports a seated posture at a usable height, whether by legs, a pedestal, a base, or the object's own bulk.
passage: The concept of a chair might appear so familiar as to require no philosophical scrutiny, yet attempts at precise definition reveal unexpected difficulties. A seemingly adequate proposal holds that a chair is an object possessing a seat, a back, a design intended for a single person's use, and legs that elevate the seat above the ground. One might observe that this captures the standard kitchen chair, the dining chair, and the desk chair with equal facility. Yet we should hesitate to accept this analysis as comprehensive. Let us imagine an ordinary beanbag chair of the sort found in many homes and informal settings. When a person settles into it, the flexible material forms a seat, and the portion rising behind the sitter provides genuine support for the back and torso. It is plainly designed for a single occupant. However, the beanbag possesses no legs; it rests upon the floor as a pliant, amorphous mass, its supporting function achieved entirely through the bulk and pliability of its filling. The fourth condition is therefore not satisfied. Yet no one hesitates to call this object a chair; it is manufactured, sold, and used under that very description. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis, for it demonstrates that legs are not a necessary feature of chairs. What is required is not legs per se, but some means of supporting a seated posture at a usable height.

definition: An object is a work of art if and only if (i) it is an artefact produced by human intentional activity, (ii) it is produced with the primary intention of being aesthetically appreciated, and (iii) it possesses formal properties that reward sustained contemplation.
conditions: The object is an artefact produced by human intentional activity
It is produced with the primary intention of being aesthetically appreciated
It possesses formal properties that reward sustained contemplation
counterexample: Suppose that Marcel Duchamp's 'Fountain' — a mass-produced porcelain urinal submitted to an art exhibition in 1917 — is considered. It is an artefact produced by human intentional activity (manufactured in a factory), and it was submitted to an exhibition with the deliberate intention of provoking thought about the nature of art itself, not primarily for aesthetic appreciation. Its formal properties are those of an ordinary sanitary fixture, not designed to reward sustained contemplation of beauty or form. Yet it is widely regarded as one of the most significant works of art of the twentieth century.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for conceptual and institutional dimensions of art, overlooking that an object may be a work of art by virtue of being presented within an art-world context with the intention of challenging or expanding the concept of art itself.
passage: The endeavour to define what makes something a work of art has occupied aestheticians for generations, and a familiar proposal holds that a work of art must be a human-made artefact, produced with the primary intention of aesthetic appreciation, and possessed of formal properties that reward contemplation. One might observe that this analysis captures many paradigm cases: a carefully composed oil painting, a sculpted marble figure, or a sonata written for the concert hall. Each is a human artefact, made for aesthetic enjoyment, and rich in formal properties that repay attentive study. Yet we should hesitate to accept this analysis as complete. Consider the celebrated case of a mass-produced porcelain urinal, submitted to an art exhibition in the early twentieth century by a figure who wished to challenge prevailing assumptions about what art is. The object is indeed a human artefact, yet it was not produced with the primary intention of being aesthetically appreciated; it was manufactured for a wholly utilitarian purpose, and its submission to the exhibition was motivated by a desire to provoke reflection rather than to furnish aesthetic pleasure. Its formal properties are those of ordinary industrial design, not calculated to reward sustained contemplation. Nevertheless, this object is widely regarded as among the most significant works of art of its century. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis, for it shows that art may derive its status from conceptual and institutional circumstances rather than from aesthetic intention or formal richness alone.

definition: A system of communication is a language if and only if (i) it employs a finite set of symbols, (ii) the symbols are combined according to syntactic rules to form meaningful expressions, and (iii) competent users can produce and understand a potentially infinite number of novel sentences.
conditions: The system employs a finite set of symbols
The symbols are combined according to syntactic rules to form meaningful expressions
Competent users can produce and understand a potentially infinite number of novel sentences
counterexample: Consider a case in which a formal programming language, such as a simple variant of machine code, employs a finite set of symbols (binary digits and operation codes), combines them according to precise syntactic rules to form meaningful instructions, and allows a programmer to write a potentially infinite number of novel programs. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call machine code a language in the sense ordinarily meant by the term, for it lacks the pragmatic, social, and expressive dimensions that characterise natural languages: it cannot be used to tell a joke, express sorrow, or conduct a philosophical argument.
missing_condition: The definition fails to include a condition requiring the system to serve as a medium for the full range of human communicative purposes, including social, expressive, and pragmatic functions beyond mere information transmission.
passage: The attempt to analyse the concept of a language in terms of symbols, syntax, and generative capacity has a long and distinguished history. On such an account, a language is a system employing a finite stock of symbols, governed by syntactic rules that determine how those symbols may be combined into meaningful expressions, and possessed of the productive power to generate indefinitely many novel sentences. One might observe that this analysis captures what is most striking about human linguistic competence: the ability to deploy a finite repertoire of elements in accordance with combinatory principles so as to produce and comprehend utterances never before encountered. Yet we should hesitate to regard the analysis as fully satisfactory. Suppose we consider a simple variant of machine code. This system employs a finite set of symbols — binary digits and operation codes — and combines them according to precise syntactic rules into meaningful instructions. Moreover, a competent programmer can write a potentially infinite number of novel programs within the system. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, it seems plain that machine code is not a language in the ordinary sense. It cannot be used to express grief, to make a promise, to deliver an ironic remark, or to conduct a philosophical dispute. It lacks the social, expressive, and pragmatic dimensions that characterise natural languages. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis.

definition: A statement is a lie if and only if (i) the speaker asserts a proposition, (ii) the speaker believes the proposition to be false, and (iii) the speaker intends to deceive the hearer into believing the proposition.
conditions: The speaker asserts a proposition
The speaker believes the proposition to be false
The speaker intends to deceive the hearer into believing the proposition
counterexample: Suppose that a prisoner of war, under interrogation by an enemy who both parties know will not be told the truth, asserts that the military base is located in the northern valley. The prisoner believes this to be false. Yet the prisoner has no intention of deceiving the interrogator, knowing full well that the interrogator will disbelieve anything said and is merely going through the motions of interrogation. Both parties understand the assertion is a formality without genuine communicative force. The first two conditions are satisfied, but the third is not. Nevertheless, the prisoner's statement is naturally described as a lie: he said something he believed to be false.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires an intention to deceive, when a statement may be a lie simply in virtue of being an assertion the speaker believes to be false, regardless of whether deception is intended or expected.
passage: The analysis of lying has attracted considerable attention from philosophers of language and moral philosophers alike, and a widely held view maintains that a lie is an assertion of a proposition believed to be false, made with the intention of deceiving the hearer. One might observe that this analysis captures the paradigmatic case admirably: the dishonest merchant who asserts that his goods are of the finest quality, believing this to be false, with the aim of inducing the customer to believe otherwise. Yet we should hesitate to accept the analysis without qualification. Let us imagine a prisoner of war undergoing interrogation by an enemy officer. Both parties understand the conventions of such encounters: the prisoner will not reveal accurate information, and the interrogator expects falsehood. The prisoner asserts that the military base is situated in the northern valley, believing this to be false. Yet he has no intention whatsoever of deceiving his interrogator, for he knows that the interrogator will disbelieve every statement made and is conducting the interrogation as a matter of procedure. The third condition is not satisfied. Nevertheless, it seems natural to describe the prisoner's statement as a lie; he has asserted something he believes to be false, and this alone appears sufficient. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis, for it suggests that lying does not require the intention to deceive but only the assertion of what one believes to be untrue.

definition: An utterance constitutes a promise if and only if (i) the speaker explicitly states an intention to perform some future action, (ii) the speaker intends to be bound by the commitment, and (iii) the hearer understands and accepts the commitment.
conditions: The speaker explicitly states an intention to perform some future action
The speaker intends to be bound by the commitment
The hearer understands and accepts the commitment
counterexample: Consider a case in which a dying mother, in her final moments, whispers to her unconscious infant child, 'I promise you will be cared for.' She explicitly states an intention regarding a future action, and she sincerely intends to be bound by this commitment, having already arranged for a guardian. Yet the infant, being unconscious and incapable of understanding language, neither understands nor accepts the commitment. The third condition fails. Nevertheless, we recognise this as a genuine promise, one that the mother's family and friends would regard as binding and the violation of which would constitute a betrayal.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires the hearer's understanding and acceptance, failing to account for promises made to those who cannot comprehend them, such as infants, the unconscious, or the absent, where the binding force of the promise derives from the speaker's solemn commitment rather than from bilateral agreement.
passage: The nature of promising has been a subject of philosophical investigation at least since the discussions of obligation and social contract in the early modern period. A natural analysis holds that a promise is constituted by the explicit statement of an intention to perform a future action, the speaker's intention to be bound by that commitment, and the hearer's understanding and acceptance of it. One might observe that this account captures the ordinary case well: when one friend tells another, 'I shall return your book by Friday,' and the other nods in acknowledgement, all three conditions are satisfied and a promise has clearly been made. Yet we should hesitate to regard the third condition as truly necessary. Suppose that a dying mother, in her last moments, whispers to her unconscious infant child a solemn assurance that the child will be cared for. She explicitly states her intention regarding a future state of affairs, and she is wholly sincere in regarding herself as bound by this commitment, having already taken steps to arrange for a guardian. But the infant, being unconscious and as yet incapable of language, neither understands nor accepts the commitment. The third condition is not met. Yet it appears beyond doubt that this utterance constitutes a genuine promise, one whose violation would be rightly condemned. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis, for it shows that the binding force of a promise may reside in the solemnity of the speaker's commitment rather than in bilateral agreement.

definition: An object is a vehicle if and only if (i) it is a manufactured conveyance, (ii) it is designed to transport persons or goods from one place to another, and (iii) it is powered by a motor or engine.
conditions: The object is a manufactured conveyance
It is designed to transport persons or goods from one place to another
It is powered by a motor or engine
counterexample: Suppose that a bicycle is considered. It is a manufactured conveyance, designed to transport a person from one place to another, and yet it is powered entirely by the rider's own muscular effort rather than by any motor or engine. The third condition is not satisfied. Nevertheless, a bicycle is classified as a vehicle in common usage, in traffic law, and in ordinary thought: cyclists must obey traffic signals, may use designated vehicle lanes, and are treated as operators of vehicles in legal and administrative contexts.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires motorised propulsion, when what matters is merely that the object be a manufactured conveyance designed for transportation, regardless of the source of motive power.
passage: The concept of a vehicle might seem too plain to merit philosophical attention, yet attempts at precise definition expose hidden difficulties. A familiar proposal holds that a vehicle is a manufactured conveyance, designed to transport persons or goods, and powered by a motor or engine. One might observe that this analysis fits many central cases: the motorcar, the railway locomotive, the aeroplane, and the motorboat all satisfy the three conditions without difficulty. Yet we should hesitate to regard the analysis as fully adequate. Consider the ordinary bicycle. It is indisputably a manufactured conveyance; factories produce millions of them each year according to precise engineering specifications. It is designed to transport a person from one location to another, and it fulfils this function with notable efficiency. However, it is powered not by any motor or engine but entirely by the muscular exertion of the rider. The third condition is therefore unsatisfied. Yet no one doubts that a bicycle is a vehicle. Traffic legislation classifies it as such, municipalities provide vehicle lanes for its use, and in ordinary speech we refer to it without hesitation as a vehicle. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis, for it demonstrates that motorised propulsion is not a necessary feature of vehicles. The concept appears to require only that the object be a manufactured conveyance designed for transportation, with the source of motive power left open.

definition: An object is a tool if and only if (i) it is a physical artefact, (ii) it is designed to extend or augment a human physical capacity, and (iii) it is manipulated by hand during use.
conditions: The object is a physical artefact
It is designed to extend or augment a human physical capacity
It is manipulated by hand during use
counterexample: Consider a case in which a foot-operated sewing machine treadle is examined. It is a physical artefact, designed to extend the human capacity to sew fabric far more rapidly and uniformly than is possible by hand, and yet it is not manipulated by hand during use; the operator powers and controls it with the feet while the hands guide the fabric. The third condition fails. Yet the treadle mechanism is paradigmatically a tool, essential to the craft of sewing for over a century.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires hand manipulation, failing to account for tools operated by other parts of the body, such as foot pedals, shoulder harnesses, or mouth-operated instruments.
passage: The analysis of the concept of a tool has implications for philosophy of technology and for our understanding of human agency in the material world. A familiar proposal holds that a tool is a physical artefact designed to extend or augment human physical capacity and manipulated by hand during use. One might observe that this analysis captures many standard cases: the hammer, the screwdriver, and the saw are all physical artefacts designed to augment human capacities and operated by hand. Yet we should hesitate to accept the third condition as necessary. Suppose we consider the treadle of a foot-operated sewing machine, of the sort used throughout the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. The treadle is a physical artefact and is designed to extend the human capacity to sew fabric with greater speed, uniformity, and endurance than hand-stitching permits. Yet it is operated not by the hands but by the feet: the operator pumps the treadle with rhythmic foot pressure while the hands attend to guiding the fabric. The condition of hand manipulation is plainly unsatisfied. Yet the treadle is paradigmatically a tool; it was essential to the craft of sewing for over a century and is described as such in every relevant context. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis. What appears to be required is not hand manipulation specifically but rather bodily manipulation of some kind.

definition: An activity is a game if and only if (i) it is engaged in voluntarily, (ii) it is distinct from ordinary life in time and place, and (iii) it produces no material goods or wealth.
conditions: The activity is engaged in voluntarily
It is distinct from ordinary life in time and place
It produces no material goods or wealth
counterexample: Suppose that a professional poker tournament is considered. The players enter voluntarily and participate in an activity clearly distinct from ordinary life, held at a designated venue during designated hours. Yet the tournament produces substantial material wealth: prize pools often amount to millions of currency units, and players participate precisely in order to win these prizes. The third condition is not satisfied. Yet a poker tournament is paradigmatically a game; no one would deny it that designation merely because material wealth is at stake.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires that a game produce no material wealth, failing to account for professional and competitive games in which substantial economic rewards are integral to the activity without compromising its status as a game.
passage: Attempts to provide a strict definition of what it is to be a game have a long and contentious history. One influential proposal, drawing upon ideas about the separateness of play from ordinary life, holds that a game is a voluntary activity, distinct from everyday existence in time and place, that produces no material goods or wealth. One might observe that this analysis captures many cases admirably: children playing in a garden, friends gathered for an evening of parlour games, or amateur athletes competing on a weekend pitch. In each case, participation is voluntary, the activity occupies a bounded time and place, and no material wealth is generated. Yet we should hesitate to accept the third condition as necessary. Consider a professional poker tournament of the sort held regularly in major cities. The participants enter voluntarily and engage in an activity that is clearly demarcated from ordinary life, taking place in a dedicated venue during specific hours. Yet the tournament produces substantial material wealth: prize pools may amount to millions, and the players participate with the explicit aim of winning these sums. The third condition is therefore unsatisfied. Nevertheless, the poker tournament is paradigmatically a game; to deny it that status merely because money changes hands would be to distort the concept beyond recognition. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis, for it demonstrates that the production of material wealth is entirely compatible with an activity's being a game.
